Foreword

Foreword
I am grateful to all those personnel who gave of their time, outside of
normal commitments, in the research and compilation of this 3rd edition
of An Garda Síochána’s Crime Investigation Techniques Manual. The
production of the new edition is modern and dynamic with every member
of the organisation receiving it in electronic format. I believe the manual
will provide clear and helpful guidance to all Garda members involved in
the investigation of crime.
Contributions were received from the Technical Bureau, Crime Policy and
Administration, the Forensic Science Laboratory and crime investigation
practitioners who have collectively shared their considerable knowledge
and skills with those who make use of this manual. My thanks also to the
editorial board and typists associated with this project for their dedicated
and enthusiastic work.
It is apparent that a greater interdependence is required on specialist
services in the technical and forensic disciplines to bring about a successful
conclusion in criminal investigations. With this in mind all investigators are
urged to embrace this manual as a guiding tool in the course of
investigations to enhance the service level provided to the public.
Thank you to all involved in the compilation of this, the third edition.

Noel Conroy
Commissioner
Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park, Dublin 8
November 2007

Preface to the Third Edition

Preface to the Third Edition
The dynamics of modern policing coupled with advances in forensic science
and investigative techniques are some of the catalysts which prompted a
review of the second edition of An Garda Síochána’s Crime Investigation
Techniques Manual.
This third edition, similar to the previous two editions, is a comprehensive
reference publication providing guidance and instruction to all Gardaí when
involved in complex and challenging criminal investigations. This
publication should not be seen as an exhaustive template for any
particular discipline, but it will serve the user well in terms of providing
assistance when investigating a wide range of criminal offences.
The European Convention on Human Rights Act 2003, which came into force
on 31st December 2003, provides that ‘every organ of the State shall perform
its functions in a manner compatible with the State’s obligations under the
Convention provisions’. As An Garda Síochána is an ‘organ of the State’ for
the purposes of the 2003 Act, Gardaí should be aware of the human rights
implications of their actions.
It is therefore recommended that all Gardaí familiarise themselves with
Appendices I and II of this manual which deals specifically with the European
Convention on Human Rights and related procedures to be followed by
members in the course of their duties. I wish to acknowledge with thanks the
materials and assistance provided by the Dean of the Law School at The
Honorable Society of Kings Inns, Dublin in compiling these guidance notes.
The health and safety policy of An Garda Síochána is to provide, in so far
as is reasonably practicable, a safe and healthy environment for all members
of An Garda Síochána, civilian employees and persons who come in contact
with the work activities of An Garda Síochána. In the context of this
publication all Gardaí and civilian employees are expected to co-operate in
carrying out this policy and to take reasonable care for their own safety,
health and welfare and that of others who may be effected by their acts or
omissions while at work.
Finally, for the avoidance of doubt, nothing contained in this manual should
be interpreted as sanctioning any action which is contrary to law or
standing instructions issued to An Garda Síochána, or which conflicts with
the written or oral instructions of the Commissioner.

Fachtna Murphy
Deputy Commissioner
Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park, Dublin 8
November 2007

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

1.1. Introduction
Crime Prevention and Reduction are core areas of professional responsibility for An Garda Síochána. The
provision of these quality services to the community is a key element of our mission. Crime prevention and
reduction are essentially about providing professional policing services, support and information, which in
conjunction with the professional services of other agencies, will contribute towards the reduction of crime,
the opportunity to commit crime and the safety of persons, the community and the State.
Crime Prevention may be defined as the ‘anticipation, recognition and informed appraisal of a crime risk or
target and the initiation of some action to remove or reduce it’. When preparing crime prevention or
reduction strategies An Garda Síochána must be able to recognise crime risks, understand how offenders
are motivated and then put in place suitable measures or systems to protect and defend an intended victim
or target from criminal attack.

1.2. Manual of Best Practices in Crime Prevention and Reduction
This chapter is primarily intended to give Gardaí an overview of the principles involved in crime prevention
and reduction. All District Headquarters have a comprehensive crime prevention manual for those tasked
with delivering a crime prevention service. The manual provides detailed advice on security systems and
equipment including the security of different property, premises and locations.

1.3. The Principles of Crime Prevention
Crime prevention measures are those which aim to remove or reduce the opportunities for crime.
Each measure, whilst it may stand alone as a preventative method, can be reliant on the others to form
a total concept of prevention. The measures can, therefore, conveniently be called the ‘Principles of
Crime Prevention ’.
They are:
• Target Hardening
• Target Removal
• Removing the means to commit crime
• Reducing the payoff
• Access Control
• Surveillance - (Formal, Natural or by Employees)
• Environmental Design
• Increasing the chances of the offender being caught
• Deflection

Target Hardening
This involves denying access through greater physical and/or electronic protection of the target. This is
achieved through the creation of physical barriers, single or multiple that must be overcome for the criminal
to reach their intended target. It can take the form of locks, bolts, fences, alarm systems and so on.

Target Removal
It is not always appropriate to harden a target. On some occasions it may be better to remove the target
from the risk area. Good examples are the payment of wages by cheque instead of cash to employees or
the installation of card reading meters instead of coin boxes in gas or phone utilities. In this way the risks
are reduced by removal of the intended target, i.e. the cash.

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

Removing the means to commit crime
Frequently, crimes are committed because of the availability of some item to assist the perpetrator. Rubbish
skips left uncollected are full of potential missiles for vandals; similarly, street furniture insecurely fixed can
be stolen, used on bonfires or used to cause obstruction on the roadways. Houses subject to burglary may
have conveniently provided the instruments of housebreaking by the failure of the occupants to secure
household tools in their sheds or outhouses. Systematic ‘good housekeeping’, both at local authority and/or
private level, can achieve a reduction in opportunistic crime.

Reducing the Pay-Off
Making the target less valuable in the hands of the criminal reduces opportunities for crime. Property
marking schemes have the effect of reducing the attraction of stolen items because they are more difficult to
dispose of. Similarly, the use of operating codes for car radios has impacted greatly in reducing their theft.
The use of ‘smoke and dye’ bags in cash transportation or enforcing a shop till cash ceiling in commercial
enterprises also help to limit the ‘payoff ’ where robbery or larceny of cash is the intended crime.

Access Control
In tandem with the physical barriers mentioned for target hardening, the use of layered or restricted access
controls to the intended target is another established crime prevention principle. If any one party has
complete access to and control of the desired commodity, then security is compromised. It also leaves the
individual concerned more vulnerable to coercion or compromise. Those who are tasked with ordering or
purchasing stock should not also be the party to pay the supplier. A bank manager who holds the keys to
the building should not be allowed also to possess the keys to the vault or safe. Similarly, time-delay or
time-lock safes can deny immediate access to their contents.

Surveillance - Formal
The presence of Gardaí on the streets is the best recognisable form of formal surveillance. Industrial sites,
shops, banks and so on, however, may employ private uniformed security guards to maintain security
through surveillance and patrolling.

Surveillance - Natural
This is achieved by the spatial definition of an area. It enables people to supervise their immediate
environment whilst going about their everyday business. It is linked closely with the concept of defensible
space in environmental design (see 1.11). The people who reside or work in an area will usually
observe naturally an occurrence that is ‘out of the ordinary’.

Surveillance - By Employees
This is the creation of the awareness of particular crime problems amongst the workforce. Employees
working in a jewellery store will have a heightened sense of security awareness because of the attraction
to a thief of the goods they are selling. A receptionist on duty in a hotel will be alert to impostors posing
as guests intent on burglary or trying to steal from patrons.

Environmental Design
Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) is the creation through design of a physical
environment conducive to the overall security of the community. It is achieved through the establishment
of defensible space by extending the citizen’s area of territorial concern from purely private to include
public space and includes the potential for community interaction and natural surveillance.

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

Increasing the Chances of the Offender Being Caught
Historically, a ‘wanted poster’ with the offer of reward money became an inducement to ordinary law
abiding citizens to assist with the arrest of suspect persons. The ‘wanted and reward’ poster not alone
advertised the fact that a crime had been committed, it served to alert the public as to the identity of an
offender. In today’s world information technology (eg the Internet) allows for greater information collation
and dissemination. This in turn can help to inform and educate the ordinary citizen on criminal behaviour
and assist the Gardaí in crime prevention.
It also increases the chances of offenders being apprehended. The circulation of information, by
Neighbourhood Watch groups and the active participation of communities in such projects all threaten the
potential criminal activity of troublemakers.

Deflecting Offenders
This measure curbs criminal activity by introducing added physical or psychological obstacles for the criminal
to contend with. The use of modern technology such as Closed Circuit Television, where conduct can be
observed on camera, can influence the potential offender in deciding the risks are too great to proceed
with their criminal activity. They are effectively deflected.
Another good example is the Gardaí, through the Juvenile Diversion Programme, exercising control over the
potential wrongful action of minors through the threat of prosecution and punishment for past misbehaviour
or future misdeeds. Offenders are no longer free to ignore the further consequences of their actions.

1.4. The Role of the National Crime Prevention Office
While the task of reducing the opportunities for committing crime and securing the co-operation of the
public involves all members of An Garda Síochána it is important that a coordinated and systematic approach
to crime prevention and reduction be maintained. The National Crime Prevention Office, through Chief
Superintendent Community Relations, will be responsible to Assistant Commissioner Strategy for the
development of crime prevention and reduction practices for An Garda Síochána.
Their remit includes:
Research
• Research into best crime prevention and reduction practices with the development of suitable
national policies for implementation.
• Exploring and promoting, where appropriate, modern security technology.
Development
• The development of strategies and plans to combat and reduce the incidence of crime.
• Expansion of the concept of Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design.
• Development within An Garda Síochána of Crime Pattern Analysis principles and processes.
Liaison
• Liaison with all other related crime prevention/reduction agencies - e.g. The National Crime Council.
• Liaison with Universities and external agencies to help develop more long-term strategies towards
crime prevention and reduction.
• Regular liaison with the Security Industry on related topics.

Information
• Establishing and maintaining a suitable reference library, in all related crime fields, for An Garda Síochána.
• Full use of the Garda Web Site to disseminate Crime Prevention information publicly.
• Offering advice and guidance to the public generally through the established media - Press, Radio,
and Television - on all aspects of crime prevention and reduction.
Service Delivery
• Assist in the training of Garda personnel, including Crime Prevention Officers, by way of In-Service,
promotion and continuous professional development courses in crime prevention techniques.
• Providing security reviews, surveys and audits for Government, business and commercial
institutions as requested.
• Supporting the planning and implementation of Crime Prevention/Reduction strategies in each
Garda region.

1.5. The Role of Divisional Crime Prevention Officers
Divisional Crime Prevention Officers report to their local Divisional Officers. The Divisional Crime Prevention
Officers will help promote and implement all crime prevention and reduction strategies that are identified
both regionally and nationally. They will maintain direct liaison with staff at the National Crime
Prevention Office.
They will also:
• Utilise all statistical and analytical data prepared by Criminal Intelligence Officers for the purpose of
developing Crime Prevention strategies
• Educate and advise all members of An Garda Síochána and the public generally on good practice in
matters relating to crime prevention and reduction
• Identify incidents of repeat victimisation and advise those targeted on best security measures to
avoid recurrence
• Conduct security reviews and audits, on request, of private and public premises and lands
• Liaise with all external agencies and bodies associated with crime prevention and reduction
• Promote and further develop the CPTED concept

1.6. The Role of Individual Members
Responsibility for the bulk of the day to day reduction of crime opportunities and improving the security of
persons and property inevitably devolves on Gardaí employed on operational policing duties.
Each Member employed on outdoor duty should have a thorough knowledge of his/her sub-district. They
should be conversant with the places where crimes have been committed recently and the areas where crime
is most prevalent, including knowledge of known criminals and wanted persons. This becomes vital when
questioning suspected persons.

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

Improving the Security of Premises and Property
Each member should use every opportunity to advise the public on the need to improve the security of his
or her premises and property. Detective branch personnel making enquiries at the scene of a crime have
an excellent opportunity to advise on security for the future; as the victim is usually more amenable to
suggestions for the improvement of security when he/she has just suffered a loss.
Members, given adequate instruction by the Crime Prevention Officer, could also inspect ‘low risk’ premises
and make security recommendations. If advice is sought in relation to premises where the overall
circumstances involve special or difficult security considerations, the matter should be referred to the Crime
Prevention Officer for his/her personal attention.
Premises with a history of breaking offences are of particular interest to the Gardaí, and the inspecting
member should know the methods by which they have been entered previously. In determining the type of
security necessary, it is useful to imagine yourself in the position of the criminal and decide how and where
you would make your attack and the escape routes for removal of the goods - these may not be the same
as the entry point.

Surveying Premises
The following are some of the considerations when deciding upon the level of physical security
at a given location:
• Type of business
• Value of stock, and whether easily transportable
• If occupied continuously
• Location - eg main well-lit street, isolated etc.
• Construction of premises
• Means of entry from adjoining premises
• Available Electronic and Alarm protection

Fences and walls
Boundary fences and walls can be made more effective against unlawful intrusion by the addition of
security fixings such as cacti-spiked irons, barbed wire etc. Gates and doors should give protection equal to
that of the fence or wall and should be capable of being securely locked. The storage of boxes and other
equipment near perimeter walls offers ready scaling assistance to thieves and should be prohibited. While
good perimeter defences will not keep out the determined intruder, they will prevent access by vehicles and
the easy transfer of goods from inside the premises to an accomplice outside.

Lighting
Lighting is a proven deterrent to thieves. Its use can be categorised as follows:
• Perimeter lighting - with consideration to external approach routes
• Area lighting - for large open spaces such as car and lorry parks etc.
• Floodlighting - providing illumination over buildings and walls
• Internal lighting - providing inspection illumination for security personnel etc.

Doors
Exterior doors and internal doors leading to high-risk areas should be in good repair, well fitting and of
substantial construction. The strength of a door lies in its physical construction and fittings. Hollow core

5

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

doors should never be used externally. A good lock is useless if fitted to an inadequate door. The replacement
of the door is often as important as that of the lock. All doors allowing access to business premises, except
emergency escape doors, should be secured from the inside by the fitting of internal bars and padlocks.
Doors frames should be securely fixed to the surrounding masonry and strong enough to accept striking
plates and staples.
Cellar flaps can provide an easy means of access to building basements and should always be secured by
bolts padlocked in position on the inside.

Windows
Vulnerable windows present special security problems. As an alternative to locks, windows may be pegged,
barred, fitted with grilles, or the glazing laminated. Display windows, even when laminated, should be
protected with shutters or grilles.

Skylights
These can provide easy access to buildings. Apart from the complete removal of the skylight it is easy to
break the glass or perspex. Wired reinforced glass gives some protection but the best method is to fix steel
bars, steel mesh or grilles on the inside, sunk into the surrounding masonry where possible.

Fall Pipes, Ladders etc.
These are often used to gain entry to upper floors. Where pipes give access to windows, “umbrella” spikes
can be fitted at intervals, or anti-climb paint can be applied from a height of 2.5 meters above ground level.
Thieves will be quick to make use of ladders left lying about outside unoccupied premises. If not locked away
in a shed, they should be chained to some fixed object and padlocked.

Cash and Valuables
Overnight cash holdings should always be kept to a minimum. Persons keeping cash or valuables on the
premises should be advised to fit a wall safe, a floor safe or other modern conventional safe of good quality;
the choice depends on the amount of property involved and the security requirements specified by the
insurance company. Safes weighing less than one ton should be securely anchored to the floor as criminals
often utilise forklift trucks or other such means to remove heavy safes which are not so secured.

Cash in Transit
The protection of cash in transit is always a major security consideration and any associated crime is often
accompanied by personal violence and substantial monetary loss. Where a professional Cash in Transit security
company is not employed the risks of moving cash can be mitigated when simple precautions are taken:
• Only employ people whose references have been authenticated for this task
• Varying the time and route to the bank as much as possible
• Details of times and routes should be communicated strictly on a ‘need to know’ basis
• Provide security satchels or cases as appropriate
• Taxis should not be used for the conveyance of cash; if private cars are utilised, they should be
fitted with special locks, immobilisers and alarm devices.

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

Keys
Keys should be housed in locked cupboards and kept as unobtrusive as possible. They should not be labelled
with the names of rooms to which they refer but should be numbered and the code kept in a separate place.
Key coding systems are commercially available.
The use of combination locks, especially in respect of high-risk premises, affords a distinct security advantage
as it is simple to vary the combination and effectively avoids risks associated with the loss of keys.

Vehicles
Some firms allow drivers to take company vehicles home. Employers should be urged to ensure that drivers
do not leave laden vehicles overnight in streets or unsupervised car parks, and should also be urged to supply
their drivers with details of the location of secure parking facilities. Ignition keys of vehicles kept on premises
overnight should be removed from the vehicles and stored where intruders cannot see them.
Many thefts from parked cars could be prevented by the observance of reasonable precautions. Motorists
should be advised to remove all valuables from unattended cars and not to leave radios, cameras or other
property exposed to view. They should also be advised to secure all windows, lock doors and boot, and
remove the ignition key before leaving the vehicle.
At night, vehicles should, if possible, be parked in well-lit streets. Documents such as registration book,
insurance certificate or driving licence should not be left in an unattended vehicle. There are several
anti-theft and alarm devices now available for cars and commercial vehicles and the fitting of such
devices is recommended.

Home security during holiday periods
Householders should be advised to take the following basic security precautions when vacating their
dwellings over any holiday period:
• Arrange with a trusted neighbour to pay attention to the premises and to report anything unusual
or suspicious to the Gardaí without delay
• (Notify local Gardaí before departure and supply them with address of destination and telephone
number together with names and addresses of persons authorised to enter the premises during
householder’s absence
• Cancel deliveries of bread, milk, newspapers, etc. Postal authorities will withhold mail deliveries on
request, alternatively, arrange for a neighbour to remove the mail from the letterbox daily
• Secure all outhouses and garages ensuring that ladders, garden implements and so on are locked
away, and in the case of firearms and ammunition that special care is taken to ensure they will not
be discovered even if a criminal forces entry to the premises
• Never leave money or valuables lying about the house
• Install timer lighting switches or ask a neighbour to oblige and draw the curtains
• Secure all outer doors, windows, skylights, fanlights and cellar openings and hide the keys
• Notify the Gardaí when occupation is resumed
• Householders should be advised to join Neighbourhood Watch, Community Alert or a similar
crime prevention scheme.

Intruder Alarms
Intruder alarms provide a high degree of security at domestic and commercial premises and act as a deterrent
to criminals. Typical alarms consist of detectors designed to activate when intruders enter or attempt to enter
the building. Personal attack buttons are also included in modern installations; these may be portable or fixed.
Most alarms on commercial premises, and many on domestic properties, are connected to a Central
Monitoring Station where alarm activations are assessed and then relayed to the Gardaí. This connection is
usually provided by means of a special dialling unit (digital dialler), located in the protected premises, which
sends a coded message to a Monitoring Centre via the telephone lines. For high-risk premises a radio signal
may also be sent.
Monitoring centres must comply with the terms of Irish and European operating Standards and alarm
companies who provide connections to such centres must be approved by the National Standards Authority
of Ireland (NSAI). Approval follows inspection by NSAI engineers and inspections are conducted on an
ongoing basis to ensure a consistently high standard of service. The current European Security Standard
(En50131) outlines the specifications for the equipment to be used in alarm systems and for the method
of installation and maintenance.
Note: All monitored alarms must comply with the current Garda Intruder Alarm Policy.

1.7. The Role of the Public, Public
Opinion and the Media
The media is a powerful instrument for informing and
educating the public, but it is a medium that must be
used with skill and caution. Gardaí should be careful not
to disclose publicly details of a particular crime or modus
operandi used by criminals, which may educate the
culprits or other potential wrongdoers. The criminal has
a greater vested interest in crime prevention and
detection procedures.
At local level, community radio programmes and regional
newspapers will gladly welcome the active participation
of Gardaí with quality submissions on crime related
subjects. Nationally, the advice and services of the Garda
Press Office or the offices of Crime Call should be sought
before any individual contribution is made to a
radio/television programme or newspaper article. The
Divisional Officer should always be consulted and his/her
approval sought before any such interviews or
submissions are undertaken.
It may also be possible to enlist the aid of public or private organisations with the distribution of suitable
literature. Security advice can be incorporated in any booklet or publication that may be issued by the
housing authorities to local authority tenants. Similarly, it can be included in the journals of particular trades
or industries, or in the newsletters of tenants’ or residents associations, church magazines, etc.
The Gardaí should never miss an opportunity to talk to trade and business associations on methods designed
to reduce the opportunities for committing crime. Crime prevention advice will improve relations between
the Gardaí and the public. Furthermore, a good crime prevention programme, whether on a national or local
basis, may also help in the detection of crime. The education of the public on the need for practical crime

prevention precautions will also make them more conscious of the need to assist the Gardaí as well as the
important and useful role which can be played by responsible individual members of the community.
Such assistance might include:
• The timely reporting of crime or crime situations to the Gardaí;
• Co-operating with the Gardaí during investigations;
• Assisting the Gardaí by providing or protecting clues useful in locating the criminal.
In all cases, the advice given must be designed to engage the attention of the public and to awaken public
awareness of their responsibility for the prevention of crime.

1.8. Evaluation of Crime Prevention/Reduction initiatives
The development of strategies and the deployment of valuable Garda resources with the aim of preventing
or reducing certain forms of crime should be measured against the successes achieved. Divisional Crime
Prevention Officers aided by The National Crime Prevention Office and the Garda Research Unit will assist
in researching and evaluating crime prevention and reduction initiatives. The expertise of all other crime
prevention practitioners and agencies should also be enlisted.

1.9. Repeat Victimisation
Repeat victimisation occurs when the same person or place suffers from more than one criminal incident over
a specified period. Research in Ireland and elsewhere has shown that repeatedly victimised people and places
account for a significant proportion of all crime. It has also been established that crime is generally not evenly
distributed but concentrated on a relatively small number of people and places.
The common features of repeat victimisation include:
• Victimised people or places are more likely to be victimised again
• Repeat Victimisation is likely to occur within a short time period - known as the ‘heightened risk’ period
• Repeat victimisation patterns have been identified across different types of crime. These include
burglary, larceny from shops, car crime and criminal damage
• High crime areas are most likely to experience repeat victimisation
• The risk of becoming a victim again, increases with each victimisation.
Knowing where and when a crime is likely to take place helps the District Officer with his/her Crime Prevention
responses to target their resources where they can be most effective. Prevention Strategies should assist those
at potential risk to take the necessary precautions to reduce their risk of becoming repeat victims of crime.

1.10. Designing out Crime
The design of the built environment should aim to reduce the opportunity for crime. The causes of crime
and vandalism are complex but it is widely accepted that environmental factors can play a part. Desolate,
sterile and featureless surroundings can engender feelings of hostility, anonymity and alienation.

Chapter 1 Crime Prevention

The use of sensitive environmental planning, commonly called Crime Prevention Through Environmental
Design (CPTED), can be instrumental in producing attractive and well-managed environments that help to
discourage anti-social behaviour.
Natural Surveillance is a key principle of CPTED and is created when residents, interested in the welfare of
their neighbour, have clear and unobstructed views of their property.
If an area looks safe it feels safe. People can know instinctively whether the area is safe or not. Those
involved in the planning, development and maintenance of the built environment should ensure that new
developments take the security of people and property fully into account. Reducing the fear of crime greatly
improves the quality of life for residents and those working in and visiting the area.
Sound security principles should accompany any new development and this requires creative solutions which
do not necessarily lead to increased costs. Where increased costs are necessary to achieve a safe solution to
design problems, such investments will enhance the value and attractiveness of the development.
Crime prevention measures should not be an afterthought to any design. Superficial measures used to
improve poorly designed developments compare very unfavourably with crime prevention measures which
have been incorporated into the design from the outset.

1.11. Defensible Space
Defensible Space is the combination of real or symbolic barriers and defined areas of influence with improved
opportunities for surveillance which bring an environment under the control of its residents. Four levels of
space are recognised - private, semi-private, semi-public and public.
• Private Space is that area of space under the total control
of the occupant and not visually or physical accessible to
the public: - eg the inside of a home or private office;
• Semi-private Space is that area of space under the control
of the occupant, but is visually and physically accessible to
the public: - eg the garden of a house;
• Semi-public Space is that area of space under the control
or within the area of responsibility of a specific group of
occupants and is accessible to the public: eg hallways and
lobbies of multi-occupancy flats, common recreation and
parking areas of multiple housing complexes;
• Public Space is that area of space to which the public has
access by right: eg a road.
It is possible to create, by design, an atmosphere in which the criminal feels uncomfortable and exposed.
A simple example is the country village where a stranger is immediately obvious.
Options to assist in increasing street activity and safety include traffic calming, improved lighting, provision of
wider footpaths and cycle lanes, and pedestrian friendly crossings which avoid long detours and underpasses.

1.12. The Role of the Garda Crime Prevention Design Advisor
A National Crime Prevention Design Advisor for An Garda Síochána was appointed in August 1998.
In January 2001, Regional Crime Prevention Design Advisors were appointed. All interested parties have
acknowledged this new initiative as a proactive approach by An Garda Síochána to reduce crime by using
Crime Prevention Through Environmental Design (CPTED) methods.
The key to success in designing out crime depends on the early involvement of the Garda Crime Prevention
Design Advisor as part of the initial design team on a project. The Garda Crime Prevention Design Advisor
is available to consult all those concerned in a project in order to achieve the objective of designing out
crime. This includes the developers, planning officers, architects, road engineers and so on. The Garda
expertise is the knowledge of crime and criminal behaviour and a thorough knowledge of security measures
and equipment. All development schemes must be designed so as to reduce the potential for criminal
activity and anti-social behaviour.

1.13. Detection and Punishment as a Deterrent
Garda activities such as surveillance of known criminals and technical investigations at scenes of crime are
measures that assist in crime prevention. The speedy detection of offences strengthens public confidence in
the Gardaí and ensures continued public cooperation. The obvious and immediate risk of being caught has,
undoubtedly, a strong deterrent effect on would-be offenders. It is a fundamental policing principle to
maintain public order by preventive and constructive policies as well as by physical control. All crime coming
to the notice of the Gardaí should be thoroughly investigated and full use be made of the technical aids and
specialist services available within the organisation.

Chapter 2 Observation and Description

2.1. Introduction
Observation means seeing accurately and completely what is being looked at. Description is the ability to tell
accurately and completely what is observed. This dual capacity is an essential requirement for all Gardaí.
Too much emphasis cannot be placed upon the importance of observing, as closely as possible, everything
that can be observed; not merely in a general way, but in minute detail. In fact, most people who look only
see the general detail. This is vital in obtaining a full and exact description. For example, in a road accident
involving persons and vehicles, the trained observer will be very much aware of the details of the picture; the
number of persons involved, their sex, age, height, weight, clothing, etc; the number of motor vehicles
involved, their colour, model, direction of travel, speed, etc.
In Garda work, observation means the perception of details pertaining to persons, objects, places or scenes,
and events, through the use of the five senses. While sight and hearing are relied upon most often, the
senses of smell, taste and touch may occasionally be employed advantageously.
Observation without the complementary ability of description is valueless in investigative work. It is not
sufficient to observe well - the individual Garda must be equally expert in describing what is observed.
Accurate and all inclusive observation by an individual is meaningless to all but himself unless he can tell
clearly and precisely what he saw. For the observation to have value, others must be able to get a clear and
accurate picture of the person, object, place, or event described.
Each member must train themselves to observe and
perceive persons, objects, places and events as they
really are. They must learn to estimate distance, speed,
the passage of time, the age, weight and height of
persons; to recognise colours and to distinguish
between variations in light conditions, etc. They should
not rely on his memory alone, as the observations may
need to be presented at a future date, and much time
may elapse. Recollections become hazy as time elapses
and as new observations accumulate. Certainly, each
Garda should and must develop their memory, but they
should not burden it with a multiplicity of observations,
all of which require descriptions. Therefore,
observations and descriptions should be supported by
notes, reports, photographs and sketches.
The very nature of general Garda work makes it mandatory for the individual Garda to be able to observe
accurately and fully so as to be able to perceive investigative leads, to evaluate the validity of witness
statements and to make accurate reports. This ability is developed, like most other skills, through practice and
experience. In this context, it is essential for each member to be aware of the influences that tend to impede
or otherwise affect observation, so that they can recognise and make due allowance for those elements and
factors that may detract from the ability to observe and describe accurately. These include situational factors
that cause inaccuracy in observation, mechanisms which cause inaccuracy in recollection and factors which
lead to inaccuracy in description.

2.2. Situational factors which cause inaccuracy in observation
Location of the Observer at the Time of Observation
It is unlikely that two or more persons will witness an incident from exactly the same location. Differences in
location may often account for differences in observation. For example, a person who witnessed an event

Chapter 2 Observation and Description

from a great distance may be able to give a good overall description of what took place but be unable to give
details of the persons or objects involved. On the other hand, a person who witnessed the same event at very
close range may be able to describe in detail the persons, objects and component actions involved but be
unsure as to the overall picture of what took place. Similarly, the height of the observer above or below the
subject of observation influences his interpretation as to size, shape, colour, texture and relative proportion.

Weather and Light Conditions
The effects of weather and light variations upon the observations of the individual are fairly self evident.
The Garda should not, however, fail to make proper allowance for them.

Absence or Presence of Distracting Events or Circumstances
Unrelated but concurrent events may greatly influence a witness's perception of an event’s importance in a
particular incident. Thus, an exciting passage of play in a football game may cause a spectator to fail to
observe closely or accurately what the person sitting next to him is doing.

Predispositions of the Observer
An individual's perception is influenced by past experiences, physiological and psychological factors and
training. Within the framework of these individual factors, an individual evaluates and interprets stimuli
received by the senses. It is essential for the Garda to recognise this and take into account its effects upon
their own observations, as well as the observations of witnesses and victims.

Past Experiences
The individual's evaluation and interpretation what they have seen tends to be influenced by their past
experiences of similar or related occurrences. The size of an object is, for instance, compared with the size
of another object with which the individual is familiar. Familiar sounds, odours, tastes, and comparable
perceptions will usually be properly interpreted by an observer, while incoming stimuli with which there are
no past comparable perceptions will often be misinterpreted.
Similarly, inaccurate interpretation of past experiences may have formed a pattern which will influence the
individual's subsequent interpretation of similar observations. For example, a person with normal colour
perception, who has from infancy been taught that the colour blue is yellow, would invariably interpret the
colour of a blue object as yellow.
In general, it should be borne in mind that:
• Young persons have limited past experience on which to base their interpretations; yet, depending upon
their individual interests, they may be entirely capable of quite accurate observations. For example, many
boys can quickly recognise and identify the make and model of motor cars but would not be likely to
perceive details of the driver, the registration particulars, or condition of the tyres;
• More mature persons normally have had a variety of experience upon which to base their interpretations.
Their experience, however, is limited to normal occurrences in their area of residence and employment.
Thus, a resident of a city may be entirely incapable of accurately describing a farm scene;
• In the case of elderly persons, physiological influences may often preclude the proper application of
patterned interpretation as moulded by experience, or experiences may be so varied and interrelated as
to result in a confused interpretation. For example, an aged person may not be able to recall details of
what they have observed; or they may tend to supply incorrect details from some similar, but unrelated,
past experience.

Chapter 2 Observation and Description

Physiological Influences
Defects, both permanent and temporary, in the physical condition of an individual may greatly affect their
ability to observe accurately and interpret observations properly. Such factors as age, disease, injury,
underdevelopment, and under-nourishment must be considered whenever appropriate. Pain, hunger, fatigue,
and unnatural positions of the body may cause an individual to interpret inaccurately observations which they
would normally place into proper mental perspective. The following general factors should be considered:
• A person of unusually short or tall stature may misinterpret the size of another person. For example, a
person 1.8 metres tall may appear very tall to an observer who is 1.5 metres tall, while such person
would appear to be of normal height to an observer who is 1.78 metres tall;
• The senses of hearing and touch of a blind person are usually developed far beyond those of a person
with normal vision. Thus, a blind person may frequently perceive sounds or note details of objects
touched which a sighted person may fail to observe;
• The senses of taste and smell are subject to frequent distortion by physical disorders and by external
stimuli. The presence of a strong taste or odour may completely conceal the presence of other tastes or
odours; consequently, these two senses are usually considered the least reliable of the senses as a basis
of interpretation. For example, the presence of strong cooking odours in a room may result in an
individual's failing to note the presence of a subtler odour of importance in a particular case. Similarly,
the taste of a strong ingredient in meal may cause a person eating the food to fail to note the presence
of a contaminant with little taste.

Psychological Influences
Temporary or permanent emotional disturbances such as fear, anger, worry, prejudice, or mental instability,
may impair the functioning of a person's senses and result in inaccuracies in observation. Dramatic situations
will tend to heighten the emotional state of the observer, and as the emotional state is aroused accuracy is
diminished. For example, the victim of a robbery may be in such fear of a weapon used by the perpetrator
that their recollection of the incident will only be that of the size of the bore of the weapon and they will
not be able to describe accurately the perpetrator. Furthermore, such an individual might logically be
expected to exaggerate the size of the bore.

2.3. Mechanisms which cause inaccuracy in recollection
Substitution and Addition of Detail
One of the most common errors in recollection is the addition of details which were not in the original picture.
These additions are often plausible. Usually they are newer experiences, but may be older ones, made to fit
into a particular recollection in order to make it accord with personal predispositions. The person who does
this is not conscious of a deliberate fraud in their additions. In this way, recollection is open to distortion by
the unconscious insertion of added items. When some items are dropped from one observation and other
items from different observations inserted, the error is known as substitution.

Transposition of Detail
Recollections are also subject to transpositions in time, sequence, and arrangement of details. This is
frequently noticed in Court evidence of witnesses in relation to what occurred in accidents. Several persons
who witnessed the same occurrence may relate the same detail but in contradictory arrangement and
sequence. Again, this may be an unconscious occurrence.

Focussing of attention on specific detail
If the recollection of one detail of an object or event is proved accurate, it does not mean that recollection
of other details will also be accurate as some persons, through training or otherwise, will have focussed
their attention on certain details only and be oblivious of the rest.

Transmission of Common Descriptions
When several persons, describing the same person, object(s), place(s) or event(s), agree on certain details it
should be established that they were not in communication with each other and unconsciously transmitted
a common description. While it is usually assumed that such agreement establishes a valid recollection,
there are cases on record that disprove this theory.

Simplification of detail
Another common source of error in recollection, which results in faulty description, is incompleteness or
simplification. In fact, omission or simplification of details frequently occurs. If, for example, one asks a
group of persons to look at a display window with the purpose of listing later all objects seen, the resulting
lists will illustrate this point of omission or simplification.

2.4. Factors which cause inaccuracy in description
Some of the factors which affect the accuracy of descriptions are given below.

Lapse of time since the observation was made
The passage of time between observation and recall can greatly influence an individual's description of what
they witnessed. The imaginative person may often tend to “fill in” the gaps in their knowledge about the
incident, particularly if they have subsequently learned that the incident is important in an investigation. On
the other hand, many persons will tend to forget or confuse details of an incident with the passage of time.

For this reason it is extremely important that witnesses and victims be
interviewed as soon as possible after they have made their observation and
before they have time to adjust their observations, consciously or unconsciously,
to fit the pattern of other information they may have seen or heard.
Problems of language
Transmission of information leads to inaccuracy. Words
are used to convey descriptions, but if the description is
to be accurate we must be careful in our choice of
words. In fact we all have difficulty in translating our
observations into language that will convey accurately
our picture of what we observed. The fault here is with
words; finding suitable words that will create for the
listener (or reader) an image comparable to the one
observed and described. For this reason, the
standardisation of Garda descriptive terms is essential.

Relating only those terms the observer feels important
Some observers tend to relate, in their descriptions, details only they deem to be important information and
omit those they believe to be unimportant. This is primarily a problem with civilian witnesses who, in most
cases, will be untrained observers.
While little can be done to influence or mould the observations of these witnesses, the Garda could
enhance the value of their observations through the employment of skilful techniques of questioning.
Through the employment of these techniques, the Garda could obtain a complete description of what
the witness observed.
Of course, it must also be borne in mind that witnesses may intentionally withhold information of what
they observed. They may, for example, be motivated by a desire not to become involved so as to avoid
personal inconvenience.

2.5. Observation and Description of Persons
The ability of the individual Garda to use a description is important. They must be able to take a description
of a person given by another, create a mental image of the person described, and recognise that person.
If the Garda cannot describe a person, it is likely that they also cannot make use of a description
communicated to them.
To observe a person and give an oral or written portrait of them is an ability that can be developed and
enhanced if the Garda knows what to look for and how to describe what they see. Therefore, it is essential
for Gardaí to have a standard form of reference for the classification of all human characteristics which
may be useful in the description of individuals.
It is necessary firstly so that the individual Garda may be able to detect and describe the unusual details of a
person's description and, secondly, that the subject's unique characteristics as described by them will be
readily, clearly and exactly understood by all other Gardaí to whom the description is transmitted.
Unless a description contains particular identifying items it is of little value. Personal descriptions usually
contain the following information: name, aliases, sex, nationality, age, height, weight, colour of eyes, colour
of hair and complexion. Here the description stops. The result is a vague, blurred picture of a unremarkable
individual without personality or outstanding characteristics. On the basis of this description it would be
highly improbable that another person would be able to pick out the subject from a crowd.

It should be remembered that unless the description points out the subject
specifically, it has not served its purpose. The description of a subject should be
restricted to information which actually tells something. It should be a positive
statement. If the subject has no scars or marks, no mention should be made of
them; in describing a subject an effort should be made to bring out the
distinctive and to get away from the normal, the average, and the everyday.
Observing Persons
Begin the observation of a person at the head and progress downwards to the feet. Take inventory on the
way down. Note physical characteristics and pay particular attention to anything unusual. The necessity for
locating and noting some feature, mannerism, peculiarity, or characteristic unique to the person described
cannot be overemphasised. The ability to do so requires acute observation and exact description.

Chapter 2 Observation and Description

Observation should proceed methodically as follows:
• First: general characteristics, such as sex, build, weight, age;
• Second: specific characteristics, such as colour of hair and eyes, shape of head and face,
distinguishing marks and scars, mannerisms, and habits;
• Third: changeable characteristics, such as clothing worn, use of cosmetics, hair styling, etc.,
at time of observation.
When attempting to identify a person from a description, the pattern of observation may be modified or even
reversed, particularly if the individual sought has some very noticeable personal characteristic; for example, a
man with a limp, or a very tall woman. After first noting such a characteristic, further observation of general
characteristics (such as height, weight and age) and additional specific characteristics may then complete the
identification of the individual as the person being sought.

Describing Persons
Every individual has some distinguishing characteristic or combination of characteristics which set them apart
from other people. These distinctive features are the most important part of the description of a person.
As in the case of observation, the accurate description of a person is facilitated by following a pattern which
normally proceeds from general characteristics to specific characteristics.
The following commonly accepted and understood words, terms and methods are recommended for use in
describing the general characteristics of a person:
Sex
Nationality
Age
When age is estimated, this fact should be clearly indicated.
Estimates of age should be in multiples of five years. In
describing the age of a person, it may often be of particular
importance to indicate not only the actual age, but also the
general age indicated by appearance.
Height
When the height is estimated, this fact should be clearly indicated. A convenient method of estimating
height involves a simple comparison with one's own height. It is good practice to state estimated height in
5 centimetre blocks, for example, 1.72m to 1.77m.
Build (including posture)
• Describe general build as large, average or small;
• Describe specific build as obese (very stout), stout, stocky, medium or slim (slender);
• Describe posture as straight (erect), medium, or stooped;
Weight
When the weight is estimated, this fact should be clearly stated. As in the case of height, a simple method
of estimation involves comparison with one's own weight. Estimates should be stated in 5 kg amounts.
For example, weight 75kg to 80kg.

Complexion
Describe skin colour; as pale, fair, dark, ruddy, sallow (sickly pale), natural, florid (flushed), red, brown,
yellow or black.
Describe skin condition as clear, pimpled, blotched, freckled, pockmarked, etc.
In the case of a woman, the description should include make up habits, such as none, heavy or other
applicable phrase. In the case of persons of colour, skin colour should be indicated as light, medium or dark.

Specific characteristics
The following words, terms, phrases and methods are recommended for standard use in describing the
specific characteristics of a person:
Head size and shape as:
• Large, medium or small;
• Long, medium or short;
• Broad, medium or narrow;
• Round, flat in back, flat on top, egg shaped, high in crown, bulging in back etc.
Profile
Except in the case of peculiarities, the description of the profile is not normally as important for
identification purposes as is the description of the frontal view of the face.
Face
Describe, as seen from the front, as round, square, broad, oval or long.
Hair
• Describe colour as blonde (light or dark), brown (light or dark), red (light or dark), auburn, black, grey,
streaked with grey, or white. In the case of bleached, tinted, or dyed hair, both the artificial and natural
colour should be indicated when possible.
• Describe density as thick, medium, thin or sparse.
• Describe hairline, as low, medium, receding, etc.
• Describe baldness, as receding over temples, total receding, frontal, whole top of head, or the appropriate
combination of types.
• Describe type as straight, wavy, curly or kinky.
• Describe texture as fine, medium or coarse.
• Describe appearance as neat, bushy, unkempt, oily or dry.
• Describe length as long, medium or short.
• Describe parting on the left, on right, in centre or not parted.
• State whether extending to long or short sideburns.
• State whether ears are partly or entirely concealed.

Current descriptive terms of hairstyles which are readily and widely understood should be used as appropriate.
Wigs, toupees, and hairpieces should be described carefully and in detail. The careful observer can often
determine whether a person is wearing a toupee or other hairpiece from such indications as difference in
hair texture, colour, density, type or appearance; furthermore, the arrangement of false hair will often be
too nearly perfect, and the edges of the hair piece will often be evident upon close scrutiny.
Forehead
• Describe height as high, medium or low*.
• Describe slope as receding, medium, vertical, prominent, or bulging.
• Describe width as wide, medium or narrow.
• Describe wrinkles or age lines as none, light, deep, horizontal, curved (up or down), or vertical.
*The height of the forehead is measured from the brow line to the start of the hair growth and is normally
one third of the length of the entire face. If the height is less than or greater than one third, then it
certainly should be recorded either as "low" or "high". In order to see the slope of the forehead, the
face has to be seen in profile.
Eyebrows
• Describe colour, including any difference from hair colour.
• Describe as slanted from centre, slanted upwards, slanted downwards, or horizontal.
• Describe line as straight or arched.
• State whether separated or connected.
• Describe texture as heavy, bushy, medium or thin.
• State whether plucked or pencilled.
In describing women, it is often important that both the natural and the artificial appearance and
outline of the eyebrows be indicated.
The colour of the eyebrows need not be mentioned in the description unless it differs from the hair of the
head. When eyebrows "meet" by virtue of hair growth continuing over the bridge of the nose, the eyes
may seem closer together than they really are. This is but one of many optical illusions which can cause
error in recollection.
Eyes
• Describe as deep set (sunken), medium or bulging.
• Describe size as large, medium or small.
• Describe spacing or distance between the eyes, as wide, medium or narrow.
• Describe slope as down turned, horizontal or up tilted at outer corners.
• State whether hooded, staring or seemingly "lidless".
• State whether crossed, squinted, watery, red, "bags" under the eyes, or other noticeable peculiarity.

• Describe colour of iris, as blue, grey, brown or hazel.
• Describe eyelids, as drooping, puffy, red, etc.
• Describe eyelashes as to colour, length (long, medium or short) and whether straight, curled or drooping.
• Describe eye shadow, as none, light, dark or irregular.
In describing women, the normal eye make up habits should be noted as to colour, type and extent. In
observing the spacing between the eyes, it should be borne in mind that normally there is the length of an
eye between the eyes. In observing the slope, it should be borne in mind that a line drawn through the
centre of both eyes should be horizontal but this is not always so.
The normal position of the eyelids makes a considerable difference to a person's appearance. For instance,
when the top lid cuts across the pupil in a half closed position, it gives the person a "sleepy" appearance and
the eyes are described as "hooded". When the white of the eyeball is visible round the entire iris, that person
is described as having "staring" eyes. Again, owing to the short space between the eyebrows and the top of
the eye some narrow, deep set eyes give an appearance of being lidless, and are so described. Disease causes
the lids to droop and we often get an exposed lower lid that is inflamed, as in the case of very old people.
The most usual colours of the iris are blue, grey, brown or hazel. In describing the colour of the iris, the
predominant colour should be selected without regard to any conflicting colours or flecking that may be
present. Of course when the iris is flecked with a mixture of colours, without any obviously predominant
one, the colour is described as hazel.
Eyeglasses (when appropriate)
Eyeglasses should be described in detail to include style (shape) and colour of frames, type and colour of
lenses, and method of attachment to face.
Contact lenses may prove difficult to observe; however, the careful observer will note such indications of
the presence of contact lenses as watery eyes and excessive blinking.
Eyeglasses, or spectacles, further test our ability to observe accurately, because their outline is so likely to be
registered instead of the actual eye shape. This is especially so when the frames and wings are heavy and
dark; or even when only the upper frame makes a thick dark line across the brows. Although thick lenses
distort the eye shape impression, they are worth remembering because they indicate defective eyesight.
When sunglasses are worn, the eye disguise is, of course, total.
Nose
• Describe length, as short, medium or long.
• Describe width as narrow, medium or broad.
• Describe projection as large, medium or short.
• Describe base as turned up, horizontal, or turned down.
• Describe shape as flat, small, medium or broad.
• Describe line as concave, straight, convex, roman.
• Describe tip as pointed, bulbous, flattened, cleft, or bi lobed.

• Describe nostrils as pinched, normal or dilated and if the division is exposed this fact should be stated.
• Describe peculiarities such as nose broken, twisted to the right or left.
• State whether hairy, deep pored or pock marked.
• The colour, if unusual, should be clearly indicated.
The nose is a very important part of the face for identification purposes if correctly and accurately described.
While every nose is not capable of a long and detailed description, there is generally something of importance
that could be included, and be useful, in the description of each nose.
The normal length of the nose is one third of the length of the
face; the nose occupying the middle third. It is not always an exact
third, and is often smaller or larger. The length of the nose is
judged in comparison with the upper and lower thirds of the face;
being termed short if less than one third length of the face, and
long if more than one third length of the face.
The nose projection, measured along the base line, is usually
about 25mm in males. This line may be longer or shorter than
the normal and, appropriately, in such cases the projection is
referred to as being long or short.
The root of the nose is the junction between the nose and the forehead and on occasions can be a very
distinctive feature. Once a person has been in Garda custody, it should be possible to give a very accurate
and useful description of the nose, as well as other parts of the face.
Mouth
• Describe size (as viewed from front) as small, medium or large.
• Describe expression as stern, sad (corners drooping), pleasant or smiling. The size (width) of the
mouth is normally about one third of the facial width.
• Describe peculiarities such as prominent changes made when speaking or laughing, twitching,
habitually open, etc.
Lips
• Describe thickness as thin, medium or thick (as viewed from the front).
• Describe upper lip as long, medium or short (as viewed in profile).
• Describe position as normal, lower lip protruding, upper lip protruding, or both lips protruding.
• Describe peculiarities such as pouting lip, compressed, loose or hare lip, and lips which in normal repose lie
partly open showing teeth.
In the case of women, the colour, type, and extent of lip rouge should be described. The length of the upper
lip is taken as the space between the red part of the upper lip and the nasal juncture.
Teeth
• Describe prominence as protruding, receding or normal.
• Describe size as large, medium or small.

• Describe general slope as normal, receding or jutting (as viewed in profile).

Ears
• Describe size as small, medium or large.
• Describe shape as oval, round, triangular, rectangular or other applicable term.
• Describe lobe shape as descending, square, medium, gulfed or lobeless.
• Describe condition as pierced, red, swollen, pimply etc.
• Describe separation from the head as close, normal or protruding.
• Describe setting as low, normal or high.
• Describe slope as vertical or sloping.
• Describe top rim, as narrow or broad.
The ear normally measures one third of the facial length, its top rim being on the imaginary line with
the brows. When the ear is situated above or below this position, its setting is described as "high" or
"low" as appropriate.
It is important to note if the separation between the ears and the head is close or wide. Both may be
protruding or it may be only one, in which case it is important to record the correct ear. Some persons
have what are known as "cauliflower" ears, due to the borders and folds having been flattened out, usually
in the boxing or wrestling ring.

Ears are an excellent means of identification (when studied in detail) and unlike
fingerprints they are generally visible by close inspection. Since the ear pattern
is unique to every person, its importance in facial identification from
photographs cannot be too greatly stressed. It is the fact that ears do not (in
their external construction) undergo any changes of pattern, other than through
injury, throughout the life of the individual that makes them so useful and
important in the identification field. (See also Chapter 7.7)
Hearing aids (when appropriate).
Hearing aids should be described in detail as to type, (such as inside the ear, behind the ear, with cord,
cordless, etc.), colour, ear in which worn.

Cheeks
• Describe as full, bony, angular, fleshy, sunken or flat.
• Describe cheekbones as high (prominent), medium or receding.
• In the case of women, make up habits should be noted.
• Describe moustache and beard (when appropriate).
• Describe colour including any difference from head hair colour.
• Describe style and configuration.
• Describe state of grooming.
Beards and Moustaches
A beard is likely to distort or confuse an impression of the whole face because it blurs the outline of chin and
jaw and can affect a judgement of proportion. If it is a full beard it may obscure the important mouth outline,
and its massed hair shape will doubtless lead the eye away from an exact consideration of the upper features.
The resulting recollection may be reduced simply to that of "a man with a beard". Here good identification
of this single "accessory" requires an awareness of the beard colour, shape and texture, whether smooth,
coarse or curly. The moustache in any of its many shapes, textures and styles, presents a particular quality of
expression, which can affect the true observation of the mouth shape. As a disguise, any moustache which
camouflages or contradicts the outline of the top lip can be as effective as a beard.
Where a beard or moustache is an accessory which hides the outline of chin or mouth, observation apart from
noting the type of beard and moustache should be concentrated on the more visible parts of the face.
Headgear
Where the hair and all or part of the forehead is hidden by any kind of headgear, the eye distraction is similar
to that caused by the beard. Instead of registering the head, hair, and forehead shape, the eye chiefly notes
the head covering and the greater this is in height or width the more it deflects attention from the face. A
high crowned, wide brimmed hat may falsely shorten the whole shape. Conversely, flat topped headwear
may lengthen the whole contour. The wider the brim the narrower the face may appear, while a brimless
covering may create the impression of greater breadth from ear to ear.
When the shape of forehead, and the eyes are obscured by headwear or spectacles, the lower sections of the
face deserve more careful attention.
General notes on observing and describing the face
Obviously, looking at a face with the deliberate intention of fixing it in memory is not the same as glancing
with casual interest at what a face projects (or seems to project) in personality or character. The best advice to
assist such a deliberate effort is that the observer should build upon his own personal habit whether he initially
"sees" a point between the eyes or at the mouth, or style of hair or mould of chin.
The purpose of this chapter is to extend, deepen and sharpen all, and
particularly facial, observation, or the foundation of whatever skill or
preference the observer already possesses. The starting point therefore
may be anywhere, provided that it be methodically followed up.
Whatever area of the face initially attracts one's attention, there should
follow a purposeful registering of its shape. Once you begin to apply
this shape recognition principle to whatever part of the face your eye
instinctively first selects, you are likely to continue successfully on the
same principle in assessing the remaining features.

Chapter 2 Observation and Description

The first stages of becoming accustomed to facial shape identification should involve a relaxed and unhurried
approach. Speed comes later, and the more carefully and steadily the groundwork is prepared, the more
accomplished the eventual skill will be.

Collecting and anchoring a face in memory can be aided by a pencil sketch, no
matter how rough and inartistic. Alternatively, or in addition, the jotting down of
a few words, while the recollection is fresh, is a further valuable aid.
Regard must be had to the many ways by which the basic face patterns may be complicated or partly hidden
by various kinds of beards, moustaches, spectacles, hair styles and headwear, which for convenience may be
termed "accessories". Since these are so numerous and so easily altered or discarded, the value of trying to
remember them as part of any identifiable face picture may easily be questioned.
But it should be remembered that if before an event a man has grown a beard or a moustache or worn a
certain kind of hair style which after the event he discards or alters, some or many people are likely to have
noted his previous appearance and be able to remember its details when these are eventually described by
witnesses of the event. A man has to eat, sleep and be seen during the time a moustache or beard grows to
noticeable length. Likewise the headwear he may have worn at an "event" has had to hang somewhere,
previously, even if discarded later.
In the observation of any face, any addition or alteration of detail which affects its overall impression is worth
remembering, the proviso being that the adornment is always of secondary importance to the main theme,
the facial outline and the shape of the individual features.
It should always be firmly borne in mind that the average eye is far more likely to see a hat or spectacles,
moustache or beard than to observe the actual details of the face.

• Describe size as long or short, thick or thin.
• Describe peculiarities such as goitre (prominent "Adam's apple").
• Describe hair as thick below collar line, inclined to the left or right.

• Describe width as narrow, medium or broad.
• State whether shoulders are square or round, level or one side lower.
• Describe as seen in profile as straight, stooped, slumped or humped.
Arms
• Describe length as long, medium or short in comparison with the rest of the body.
• Describe musculature as slight, medium or heavy.
Average or medium length arms terminate with the heel of the hand about half way between the hips
and the knee when the arms are hanging naturally.

Speech
The tone and manner of a person’s speech may often be very important aspects to their complete description.
Habitual tone should be indicated as low, medium or loud, soft or gruff, or by other descriptive qualities.
Manner of speaking should be indicated as cultured, vulgar, clipped, fluent and so on. Peculiarities such as
stuttering, nasal twang, pronounced drawl, or a mute condition should be indicated clearly and explained.
Accent should also be indicated.
Dress
Since a person may change clothing its value for descriptive purposes is limited. Noticeable habits in manner
of dress, such as neatness, carelessness and preferences of style should be indicated. Clothing, including
footwear worn by a person at the time of an offence or when last seen, should be described in detail.
Personal appearance
• Describe as neat or untidy.
• Describe as well groomed or unkempt.
• Describe as refined or rough.
Mannerisms and habits
Often the peculiar mannerisms or traits of a person will constitute the major or key parts of his description.
The Garda must be alert to record in descriptions such characteristics as:
Traits and mannerisms
• Feminine traits in men and masculine traits in women;
• Peculiarities in walking, moving, talking or standing;
• Outward emotional instability, nervousness, or indecision;
• Sub conscious mannerisms such as scratching the nose;
• Running the hand through the hair, pulling on an ear;
• Hitching up the pants, jingling keys or flipping coins;
• Facial tics, muscular twitches, or excessive "talking with the hands";
• Jewellery worn (watches, rings, ear rings etc.) and kinds of jewellery preferred;
Other characteristics
• Type of companions preferred;
• Kinds of recreation preferred or hobbies pursued;
• Drinking and eating habits;
• Conversation matter, pet subjects.
Name, address and occupation
Naturally the first thing to note when describing a person or a body is the name, if known. The surname
should always be written in block capitals followed by all the first names, aliases and nicknames. If dealing
with a married woman, her maiden name, if available, should be mentioned.
The last known address and the home address, if different, should be shown as well as the place of birth and
date of birth, if known. A person's occupation is useful information as a person naturally seeks employment
similar to that to which they are accustomed.

2.6. Observation and Description of Objects
When observing physical objects for later description
or in an attempt to locate a previously described
object, it is best to follow a pattern, proceeding
from general to specific characteristics. Due to
the great variation in size, conformation, shape and
location of objects, it is not possible to outline a
detailed pattern for their observation and description.
The description of the general characteristics should
be such as to define clearly the general category of
the particular object and preclude its being confused
with objects of other or similar categories. The
general characteristics should include those aspects
of an object which are readily discernible and may
serve to effect quick and conclusive recognition.
After describing the general or basic characteristics of an object and establishing its fundamental classification,
the Garda should proceed to describe systematically and in detail its specific characteristics. It is this part of
the description which will distinguish the particular object and set it off from all others similar in
category. Therefore, it is essential to note and describe accurately all the details of the object of interest. The
pattern of this description will vary according to the type of object, but it should follow a general pattern,
such as top to bottom, front to rear, or left to right. Such particulars as distinguishing marks, scratches,
alterations, damaged parts, worn areas, signs of repair, serial numbers, identifying marks, should be indicated.

Identification of Objects
A line up of objects for identification by a witness may be of considerable importance in an investigation.
When this procedure is employed, a group of six or more objects, similar to and including the particular object
of interest, should be assembled for inspection by the witness. The witness should then be requested to
identify the object of interest from the group. The line up should be held in a well lighted room or other area
and away from public view when possible. Techniques for conducting the line up will vary according to the
type of object to be identified, for example:
• A vehicle to be identified may be placed in a convenient car park with other vehicles
• A coat may be hung on an office coat rack among the coats of office personnel
• A firearm may be placed in a rack or cabinet with other firearms
It is usually very difficult for a witness to identify a firearm involved in an incident. If investigation has revealed
that the witness can identify firearms only as to type, the group of weapons exhibited should include one or
two weapons of the type described by them.

2.7. Observation and description of places (Scenes)
Detailed observation of places is usually necessary to establish
• The exact location of an incident or crime
• To detect relevant evidence
• To relate to an incident or crime information which has previously been obtained from witnesses.
In order to present a concise and readily understandable word picture of the scene, incorporating all relevant
elements, it is essential that both the observation and description follow an ordered pattern. This pattern of
