observation and description may vary depending upon whether the place is located in the open or inside a
building or structure.

Outdoors
Most outdoor scenes will either contain or be relatively near natural or man made landmarks that may be
used to pinpoint the general location. Frequently, however, these scenes may not have well defined
boundaries such as roads, fences, streams or buildings. Consequently, it is necessary to assign practical limits
to the area that is to be observed and described. In these cases, care should be taken to ensure that the
selected limits preclude both overextension and illogical limiting.
A convenient procedure for the observation and description of outdoor scenes is to observe and describe:
• The general location relative to landmarks, such as roadways, railways, or streams etc
• Exact location in relation to specific fixed or semi fixed features such as houses, fences,
trees and pathways
• Outstanding objects of features within the scene, and details of the scene and details of items
of particular interest

Indoors
The observation of indoor scenes is simplified by the existence of obvious and definite boundaries, such as the
walls of a room, or the area of a hallway. Indoor scenes contain many specific objects which could complicate
the task of complete observation and description. It is therefore particularly important that a methodical
pattern be followed.
The following should be noted
• The location of the building, including the section of building in which the scene is located, front,
rear, floor level etc
• Relationship to building entrances and distances to stairways, elevators etc
A convenient pattern is to observe and describe:
• Room number or other designation
• Details of immediate entrance(s) to the specific area of interest
• Objects located within the area. (The Garda should use a clockwise or other methodical progression
of observation from a designated initial point to identify such objects)
• Exact location, in relation to other objects, or items of specific interest

2.8. Observation and Description of Events
Gardai will seldom observe the complete crime or other incident at the time of occurrence. Their observation
of connected actions that may occur subsequent to the event itself may supply major clues as to what has
already occurred. It is only through alert and sensitive perception of all facets present at the scene of an event
that sufficient observation will be achieved. This is the only way to ensure that true and significant clues are
not overlooked.

Chapter 2 Observation and Description

Such a small but often very significant action or circumstance as an inappropriate remark, a state of
excitement, a furtive gesture or glance, an expression of unusual curiosity, interest, or disbelief, or an
unlikely profession of lack of knowledge may often provide the necessary lead to develop an important
aspect of an investigation. Similarly, such details as the way a fire burns, the pressure of certain fumes or
odours, the pitch of a voice, or the warmth of a body, may provide significant information and aid in the
reconstruction of the event.
If the Garda is present at the time of the occurrence of the event, they must be able to observe objectively,
accurately and rapidly (or concurrently) all essential factors relating to time, place, persons, objects, and
actions involved, as well as the immediate results of the event.
The description of an event should represent as accurately as possible a reconstruction of the event as it
actually happened.
The description should be as complete as time and circumstance make practicable and should always
contain the essential facts of:
• Time
• Location
• Sequence of action
• Objects involved
• Persons involved
• Immediate results of the occurrence
In compiling a thorough description of an event the Garda should consider all available information resulting
from their own observations, statements made by witnesses and suspects, and the evaluation of physical
evidence obtained at the scene. In appropriate cases the description will be supplemented by sketches
and/or photographs.
The ability to accurately observe actions and events through employing all five senses is a skill which is
developed only through concentrated training and practice. Complete and accurate observation of events is
the result of conscious applied effort rather than mere chance and is made easier by following the procedures
recommended for the full and systematic observation of persons, objects and places.

Chapter 3 Witnesses

3.1. Introduction
The detection of a crime is largely dependent on the efficiency with which the investigation is carried out.
It is therefore of supreme importance that all potential witnesses be located as speedily as possible and
information in their possession concerning the crime be obtained from them. The interviewing of witnesses
demands a high degree of skill and patience which can be perfected only through experience. Junior
members should therefore be afforded every opportunity of being present when an experienced member
is interviewing witnesses.
This Chapter deals solely with witnesses. If at any stage a witness becomes a suspect for the particular crime
under investigation the provisions of Chapter 4 will apply.

3.2. Obligation to Seek Witnesses
When a crime has been committed a clear obligation
rests on the investigating members to take positive
steps to locate and interview thoroughly all possible
witnesses. If it is found later that some of the witnesses
will not be required, it will in no way reflect upon the
members concerned or hamper the prosecution case in
Court. On the other hand, it would most definitely
reflect very unfavourably on the members concerned
were it to be discovered when a prosecution case has
failed because an individual who possessed vital
information had not been located due to lack of real
effort on the part of the investigating members.
The main objective in seeking out witnesses is to secure evidence that a crime has been committed and/or
that a particular individual did or did not commit the crime.
Members should be aware of the obligation on them in respect of the identification of suspects or
witnesses, particularly in respect of video evidence and the necessity to preserve it until the case has been
dealt with in Court.
The leading cases in this area have been dealt with by the Supreme Court in Daniel Braddish v the DPP and
Judge Haugh [2001] IESC 45; Robert Dunne v DPP [2002] IESC 27 and Michael Scully v DPP [2005] IESC 11.
In the Dunne case the following part of the judgement is of particular relevance. “It is the duty of the
Gardaí, arising from their unique investigative role, to seek out and preserve all evidence having a bearing or
potential bearing on the issue of guilt or innocence. This is so, whether the prosecution proposes to rely on
the evidence or not, and regardless of whether it assists the case the prosecution is advancing or not.”
Later in the judgement, having further considered this duty, the following passage occurs:
“ …….. [The duty] cannot be interpreted as requiring the Gardaí to engage in disproportionate commitment
of manpower or resources in an exhaustive search for every conceivable kind of evidence. The duty must
be interpreted realistically on the facts of each case”.

3.3. Classes of Witnesses
A witness may be described as a person, other than a suspect, who gives information concerning a crime.
Persons sought as witnesses fall under the following classes:
• Victims and complainants
• Eye witnesses to the crime
• Persons who can give details establishing that a crime has been committed
• Persons who can testify as to the actions of the victim, prior to and/or subsequent to the crime
• Persons who saw the accused going to or leaving the scene
• Persons who can testify as to the action and whereabouts of the accused at or about the time the
crime was committed
• Persons who can testify as to the actions of the accused prior to and/or subsequent to the crime
• Persons who know facts or heard the accused say certain things that would tend to establish a
motive for committing the crime
• A scientific or technical specialist who examined the physical evidence e.g. expert witnesses from
the various technical units
• A person who by his knowledge of certain facts can contribute to the overall knowledge of the case
• Persons serving only as sources of information.

3.4. Locating Witnesses
Witnesses should be sought in the following manner.

At the scene of the crime
The first members to arrive at the scene of a suspected crime should secure adequate information as to the
identity of all persons found there on arrival and persons who may have left the scene prior to the arrival of
the first members and who were at the scene either immediately before, at about the time of or immediately
after the crime was committed.

Neighbourhood canvass
All residences, places of business, apartments, supermarkets, shops, and service stations in the general vicinity
of the scene of the crime should be visited and occupiers, customers, employees and visitors interviewed.
Delivery persons, drivers of delivery vans, bus and taxi drivers, and school children should also be interviewed.
CCTV footage should be obtained from the relevant scenes as this can reveal witnesses previously unknown.
The area to be canvassed may have to be much wider in a case where firearms are suspected to have been
used from a distance. It will be up to the senior investigating officer usually the District Officer to decide on
the area to be canvassed. Then the following factors would have to be considered:

Chapter 3 Witnesses

• Possible path of the bullet
• Range of the weapon used
• Obstruction in the line of fire.
In the investigation of major crime, a house to house questionnaire is normally used. Best practice dictates
that a Sergeant is given responsibility for all house to house enquiries and it is their responsibility to sign off on
all enquiries made. Detailed forms for each person in every household must be completed and analysed.

Re visiting the scene of the crime
Re visiting the scene of the crime can be very effective in locating witnesses. Motorists and others who do
not live or work in the area but who travel through or past the area at about the same time daily or weekly
often prove to be useful witnesses. Such witnesses are usually found by way of a checkpoint. Flyers are
given to motorists or they are asked a set number of questions which are recorded on a questionnaire which,
in turn, is returned to the incident room. The scene should be re visited daily for a number of days at about
the time the crime was committed and weekly (for a few weeks) on the day of the week and about the
same time of day and all passers-by interviewed.

Informants
Neighbourhood sources of information which may be helpful in locating witnesses should be utilised to the
fullest. The importance of members developing information sources in their areas during the performance
or routine duties cannot be over emphasised. The code of practice relating to Covert Human Intelligence
Sources (CHIS) should be used/appreciated and the roles/responsibilities, assessments, management and
administration should be understood by all.

News Media
In attempting to locate witnesses to major crimes, use should be made of all modern communications
media including national and appropriate regional newspapers, radio and television as well as the use of
Crimestoppers/Crimecall and the Garda website.

Church Services
In the investigation of major crimes the use of announcements at local religious services should be
considered in attempting to locate witnesses.

Victims, friends and associates
In murder and in some sexual assault cases, the friends and associates of the victim are sometimes helpful in
locating potential witnesses.
Enquiries among neighbours or fellow workers of the victim could supply a few names and addresses to
begin this line of enquiry. Each witness interviewed may be able to disclose the names of other persons
known to the victim. Diaries or telephone books and computer media are often found to be very useful for
identifying persons known to the victim.
Usually, this searching for witnesses in murder cases is tied in with the portion of the enquiry which
attempts to reconstruct the last hours of the victim or to locate an unusual friendship or association likely to
produce useful leads.
Even during the hearing of the case, vital information and possibly the identity of new witnesses may come
to light. If this occurs, the member in charge of the case should be notified.

3.5. Purpose of interviewing witnesses
An interview is the questioning of a person believed to possess information or facts relevant to a crime.
It is basically a search for the truth.
Interviews are used for the following purposes:
• To establish the facts of a crime which may provide leads which will identify the perpetrator of the
crime under investigation and/or of other crimes committed
• To verify information already known by:
• Corroborating or disproving statements
• Inferences derived from physical evidence
• Linking physical evidence of a suspect with the case
• Developing evidence which eliminates an individual as a suspect for a crime
• To secure evidence that may establish guilt or complicity of a suspect, facilitate the recovery of the
proceeds of a crime or to identify accomplices.

3.6. Preparing for Interview
Interviews are classified as informal or formal. Informal interviewing is used mainly at the scene of a crime to
obtain initial relevant details of the crime and to help in locating witnesses. Even with informal interviews a
note should be taken in a Garda notebook and the person should be asked to sign it. No preparation is
possible before an informal interview but there are two points which should be remembered:
• Each witness should be interviewed separately
• Accurate details should be obtained as preliminary action may have to be based on these.
Formal interviewing is conducted to obtain specific information concerning a crime from a person believed
to have such information. In this case a written statement is taken. In serious cases, experienced members
should be detailed for this work.
Generally not more than two members should carry out a formal interview of a witness and no other person
should be present; when the witness is a child or young person a parent, adult relative or guardian may also
be present. Where two members are present at the interview, one should concentrate on writing the
statement and the second on obtaining the story of the witness.
It is very important that members prepare themselves adequately to conduct a formal interview. Of necessity,
the preparation may be hasty, sometimes based on no more than a few details of the case or on a brief
conversation with the members who first arrived at the scene.
When time permits, the following preparation should be made:
• The interviewers should be in possession of all known details of the crime. They should also pay particular
attention to the specific details, especially those that have not become public knowledge
• They should familiarise themselves with aspects of the law relating to the crime(s) under investigation,
especially the Judges Rules, and should also acquire some knowledge of subjects which may relate to
the particular investigation. For example, an interviewer of a witness in a case of company fraud would
require some knowledge of book keeping and accountancy

• The interviewer should consider in advance (where possible) what information is to be sought in the
interview. They should prepare a set of questions which they can consult unobtrusively during the
interview. The questions should be designed to induce the witness to tell their story rather than to elicit
"yes" or "no" answers. Care must always be taken not to overestimate or underestimate the witness
as a source of information.

Cultural and Diversity Awareness
Members should familiarise themselves with the customs and traditions
of the various ethnic groupings in our increasingly culturally diverse
society through the literature circulated from time to time by the Garda
Human Rights Office at the Garda College. This will ensure that
interviews with witnesses from all community groups will be conducted
with maximum professionalism and sensitivity.

3.7. Time of interview
A witness should be formally interviewed as soon as possible after the crime, in order:
• To obtain the information when it is still fresh in the mind of the witness
• To do so before any possible intimidation of the witness can take place
• To prevent collaboration between the witness and others
The time chosen for the interview should be convenient to the witness and should provide adequate
opportunity for a thorough interview. Interviewing late at night should be avoided if at all possible.
Improper scheduling could result in a rushed interview in which essential details might be overlooked.

Time is not a factor, however, when there is a problem about the condition of the witness. A formal interview
should not be conducted with a witness who is obviously intoxicated, under the influence of drugs or under
sedation. In the case of a witness who is ill or in hospital, it is necessary to be guided by medical advice.

3.8. Place of Interview
The interviewer must decide, on the basis of the facts in each case, what they think
their best chances are for encouraging the witness to disclose all the information in
their possession. At times it is best to interview a witness among familiar
surroundings, such as their home. At other times it is best to hold the interview at
a place where the witness is deprived of a familiar environment. In many cases the
home of the witness would be suitable, but generally the best place is a
Garda Station.
The place of employment of a witness is not a suitable location. This would not apply to certain witnesses,
such as those with routine details of a crime that occurred at their place of employment.

In sensitive cases, such as an interview with an informant, great care must
be exercised in the selection of a place for the interview in order not to
attract undue attention.
In many instances, it will be necessary to carry out a formal interview of a witness in a hospital. There may
be cases where a person is admitted to hospital with serious injuries of an unknown origin and because of
their condition a member may be refused admission to interview them. In this case, members should be
very tactful and endeavour to get the doctor or hospital staff to ask the patient how they sustained the
injuries and to give the result to all members as soon as possible.
It is very important that investigating members keep in close touch with hospital staff regarding a witness or
victim of a crime and carry out a formal interview at the earliest possible time. (For Dying Declarations, see
Chapter 9, The Investigation of Homicide and Suspicious Death.)
On many occasions it may be useful to visit the scene of the crime with a witness and if it is thought that
this would help in any way, there should be no hesitation in doing so.

3.9. Presence of Parent or Guardian when Interviewing Children
There are now detailed guidelines set out in Section 8.12 of the Children First guidelines which should be
complied with. As a general rule every possible effort should be made to have a parent, adult relative or
guardian present when a juvenile (person under 18 years) is being formally interviewed. This is particularly
desirable where the nature of the case is such that a responsible parent would be likely to feel it necessary
to be present to support and reassure the witness, as would be likely to be the case if the crime was, for
instance, one of sexual assault and the witness was the victim.
A child may be formally interviewed in the absence of a parent, adult relative or guardian in the
following cases:
• Where there would be a delay which might endanger the success of the investigation
• Where there is reason to believe that a parent, adult relative or guardian is implicated in the crime
or would be likely to obstruct the investigation or prevent the witness from making a statement
In the case of a child resident in a boarding school or institution, the person in charge of the school or
institution may be regarded as acting in loco parentis. Clearly this would not be appropriate if there were
concerns of abuse at the school/institution. In this instance an independent responsible adult would have
to be sought.
When it is necessary to visit a school to interview formally a child the permission of the principal teacher
should be obtained and if a parent, adult relative or guardian is not present, the head teacher should be
asked to be present or to depute another teacher to be present during the interview. If it becomes obvious
that the presence of the teacher within hearing distance tends to defeat the purpose of the interview they
should be informed tactfully of the difficulty and requested to co operate by moving to a place not within
hearing distance but from where they will have full view of the interviewer and the witness.

3.10. Interviewing Children at School
In general, the interviewing of children at school should be avoided except when really necessary. Children
may be interviewed at school in the following cases:

• When conducting preliminary enquiries regarding an offence committed in the school
• When making general preliminary enquiries into a crime (committed anywhere) which may provide a
lead for investigation. For example, a child who was at or near the scene of a crime sometime after it
occurred may be asked if they heard or saw anything unusual. Likewise, a child or group of children
on the way to or from school may be asked whether they or any one of them, as the case may be,
had seen or heard anything that would help in the investigation
• When to wait for the child to return home is likely to impede or delay enquiries or endanger the
success of the investigation
• When the child is resident in a boarding school. This should be done in the presence of a parent or
other responsible adult

3.11. Distracting Persons
During the course of interviewing witnesses, persons may occasionally be encountered who have no real
connection with the crime or possess no knowledge of it, but who nevertheless offer ‘information’ to
members. Such persons may claim to be witnesses or victims or even the perpetrators of the crime.
Despite the lack of any real basis for their statements, these persons should not be dismissed lightly.
Their stories should always be listened to and the information supplied should be evaluated in relation
to the known facts. The most common types of individuals in this category are listed below.

Sensation or publicity seekers
Some mentally unbalanced or highly imaginative persons may present themselves as witnesses or as
additional victims of known suspects who have received an amount of publicity. Members should be on
their guard for such publicity seekers and every effort should be made to handle them in a manner that
does not hamper the investigation.

Lying and grudge bearing witnesses
Because of previous difficulties with an accused or suspect or for some other reason, a person with no real
knowledge of a crime may volunteer information about, or profess to be a witness to the crime. Such
persons should always be allowed to talk as much as they like. It is extremely difficult to lie consistently.
A thorough familiarity with the known facts and details of the crime will often enable the interviewer to
detect inconsistencies in the story of such a person. The information supplied by lying and grudge bearing
persons may closely correspond with the accounts of the crime that have been released to the press or that
have been allowed to circulate through other channels.

Where the real motives of such a person are obscure to the interviewer, all
possible background information should be probed to disclose the untruths
and the motivation for the statements.
False Accusers
A false accuser may make a charge that later investigation will prove to be groundless. A false charge can be,
at times, an exaggerated version of an actual crime of a lesser nature but is sometimes made when no offence
has been committed. False charges may occur in almost every type of case. A false charge may represent the

sincere, though erroneous, thinking of the victim, or may rest on the victim's reaction to previous ill will,
suspicion or jealousy. An amount of skill is required in the initial interview with an accuser to separate
truthful from unfounded accusations.

S.12 of the Criminal Law Act, 1976 deals with persons making false reports
or statements. S.5 of the Protections for Persons Reporting Child Abuse
Act, 1998, deals with false reporting of child abuse.
Where there are grounds for suspecting that a person is motivated by malice in making a complaint of a crime
or motivated by prejudice against a minority group, no follow up action such as the arrest or search of the
accused person or their dwelling should be carried out until such time as the bona fides of the complainant
have been established. (See also Appendices I and II in this context.)

3.12. Human factors which affect the degree of success
in interviewing
The following factors should be considered by members investigating crime. Evaluation and analysis of these
factors can be critical to the overall success of the investigation. Many of the skills described can only be
gained by experience and junior members should therefore avail themselves of every opportunity to develop
these skills be observing and assisting experienced members.

Perception and Memory
The validity of the information divulged during an interview is influenced by the ability of the witness to
perceive correctly what happened in their presence, to recollect that information and to communicate it
correctly to the interviewer. A mistake made in recalling a particular incident is frequently due to:
• A weakness in the witness's ability to see, hear, smell, taste or touch;
• The location of the witness in relation to the incident at the time of the incident;
• A lapse of time since the occurrence of the incident.
The witness may have had no reason for attaching much importance to the particular incident when
it occurred.

The account given of an incident at a later time may well be coloured,
consciously or unconsciously, by what the subject has heard or seen
regarding the incident since its occurrence.
Furthermore, a witness may fill in the gaps in their knowledge of a particular incident by rationalising what
they actually did see or hear, and may repeat the entire mixture of fabrication and fact to the interviewer as
the truth.

This is the main reason why a witness should be interviewed as soon as
possible after the crime. Even then, skill is required to discover what the
witness actually observed.

8

Prejudice
When making a statement, the witness may be influenced by prejudice. Members should be alert to this
possibility and attempt to discover the motivation behind such prejudice. A statement influenced by prejudice
should be evaluated carefully and closely examined for reliable information that may be helpful in the
investigation.

Reluctance to talk
A person who is reluctant to divulge information may be encountered fairly frequently. The most common
reasons for reluctance to talk are:
• Fear of self involvement: many people are unfamiliar with Garda procedure to the extent that they
are afraid to be of assistance. They may:
• have committed a trivial offence which they believe will be brought to light upon the least
involvement with the Gardaí.
• be afraid of being suspected of being themselves the perpetrators.
• be of the opinion that the crime or its detection is not their concern, or that guilt lies jointly on the
victim and culprit
• fear the publicity that may be given to persons involved in any way with the case, or they may
fear reprisals
• Inconvenience: persons may disclaim knowledge of incidents because they do not wish to be
inconvenienced by either being subjected to questions or by being required to appear in Court and
subjected to rigorous cross examination
• Sympathy for accused person: the witness may be a friend of the accused or may see an accused
person as an underdog pitted against authority
• Sexual assault victims: Persons who have been the victims of a sexual assault may be reluctant to
disclose all details about intimate details to a male or female interviewer depending on the sex of the
victim. In such a case there should be no hesitation in arranging to have a Garda of a different sex
conduct the interview. See Chapter 11.3 and 11.5
• Personality conflict between interviewer and witness: the lack of success in an interview may be due
to a personality conflict between the interviewer and the witness. When that is the case, the interviewer
should recognise this factor and withdraw in favour of another member before a stage is reached
where all chances of success are lost. In certain cases, a witness might wish to be interviewed by a
named member. As far as possible this should be arranged, particularly if it is believed that the witness
is in possession of useful information or if it is a serious crime

3.13. Formulating techniques for interviewing
The interviewer must adapt his techniques of interviewing to the different personalities and mentalities
encountered in life. Unreliable persons or liars should always be allowed to talk as much as they like. If such
a witness is allowed to give their own account of things related to the investigation they may finally
contradict themselves and tell the truth.
Shy and nervous persons should be approached tactfully and a patient and methodical process of questioning
adopted. Such persons are slow to divulge information and interviews with them are likely to be more
productive if conducted in their own homes.
The talkative person presents many difficulties. Although patience may be strained to the limit it is necessary
to let them give their own account. The interviewer, through skilful questioning, must keep the witness on

the right path. The risk of misunderstanding such witnesses is great if they are not allowed to use their own
expressions. It should be borne in mind that they are apt to give exaggerated and false information.
Children are generally good witnesses when handled with care. They are usually keen observers. It is
important that the interviewer be friendly towards a child witness in order to give them confidence and
encourage them to talk. As children easily confuse their own experiences with those of others, it is important
that they be told to relate only about their actual experiences and not what they have heard.

3.14. Order in which witnesses should be interviewed
The order in which witnesses are to be interviewed should be carefully considered, particularly in a case
where there is a large number of witnesses. No hard and fast rule can be laid down. Generally the
complainant or victim should be interviewed first, if possible, to ascertain that the crime did occur as alleged.
Next in order should be the person who reported the
matter to the Gardaí, or who caused the matter to be
reported. Then the person or persons who were with
the victim or complainant at the time of the crime,
those in the vicinity at the time, and those who last
saw the victim before the crime.
In cases of crimes against property, the person who
has charge of the property, the person who last saw
it intact and the person who discovered the crime
should be interviewed at the earliest opportunity.
When these persons have been interviewed, it may then be found convenient to take other statements in
chronological order from various witnesses according to the facts established by the investigation.

3.15. Conducting the interview
In interviewing a witness it is best to have a certain amount of suspicion, even if the witness seems to be
truthful and serious.
It must always be borne in mind that the primary responsibility of the interviewer is to get all relevant
information from the witness.
The witness should be made aware of the reason for the interview. The introduction is very important. A
hasty introduction or an appearance of haste at the beginning of the interview may make the witness think
that their presence is of little importance and that the information they are in a position to give is of little
value. A little time spent on a proper introduction is never wasted. It gives the interviewer time to evaluate
the witness and to decide on the best approach to adopt in the interview.
As a rule, the witness should be asked to give an account of what they know of the matter in their own way.
Generally speaking, it is advisable to take notes when the witness is relating details in the first instance. It
must be borne in mind that such notes could become subject to a defence disclosure request. Most persons
when interviewed raise no objection to discreet note taking by the interviewer. The most important aspect of
note taking is that the interviewer can then take the statement in a chronological sequence of the events as
they occurred.

10

The attitude and actions of the interviewer usually determine the success or failure of the interview. Some
witnesses may require firm handling but as a rule best results are obtained through kindness, patience, and
sympathy. The interviewer should endeavour to put the witness at their ease and encourage them to talk
about the crime and give a true and complete account of their knowledge of it.
When a witness is in a talkative mood they should be permitted to relate the complete story without
unnecessary interruptions from the interviewer. In some instances, however, it may be necessary to guide
the conversation towards the witnesses actual knowledge of the case.
The interviewer must try to make the witness realise that they have important and necessary information.
They should frequently guide the witness in order to help them recall and relate the facts of a crime as they
were observed. If memory fails the witness about the time of an occurrence it may help to ask them about
some associated event such as, for instance, what they were doing on the day in question.
Questions should be phrased so as to maintain a free flow of talk from the witness and should be designed
to develop a detailed account of their knowledge. Questions of a leading nature must be avoided as far as
possible. Leading questions are those which can be answered by ‘yes’ or ‘no’ and are not regarded as being
very helpful in an investigation. An unstable person or a person whose memory of events is hazy will often
give answers that are suggested to them by leading questions. On the other hand, open ended questions such as ‘then what?’, ‘what gives you that impression?’ - will assist the witness in continuing a story.
When a witness displays a general unwillingness to talk, a more direct type of questioning may have
to be adopted.

No member should ever disclose to a witness the identity of another witness
in a case before giving evidence in Court; nor should a member disclose to
a witness the source of any information received.

3.16. Witnesses who fail or refuse to give evidence in accordance
with their statements of evidence - Part 3 of the Criminal Justice
Act, 2006 (CJA 06)
At common law, the general rule is that the previous statements of a witness, whether consistent or
inconsistent, go to credibility only and are inadmissible as evidence of the truth of their contents. This
common law rule has now been significantly modified by Part 3 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2006.
There have been occasions in the past where witnesses have failed or refused to give evidence in accordance
with their statements, often due to intimidation. Where the Officer in charge of the investigation has any
suspicion that such an eventuality may arise, the procedure set out in this paragraph should be followed in
order that an application to admit the original statement made by the witness under Part 3 of the Act may be
considered. Essentially the criteria for admissibility is set out in Section 16 of CJA 06 and includes the
following;
• That the statement of the witness is relevant to the proceedings. In other words, does the statement
tend to prove or disprove that the accused committed the act with which they are charged.
• The contents of the statement must be admissible in evidence. If the statement contained an expression
of opinion, for example, which would not be admissible if the witness were giving oral evidence, then
Section 16 may not be used as a way to circumvent this rule. In general this means that the admissibility
of the statement will be restricted to facts stated therein of which the witness had personal knowledge.

11

11/12/2007

11:24

Page 12

Chapter 3 Witnesses

• The witness must either;
• Refuse to give evidence
• Deny making the statement, or
• Give evidence materially inconsistent with it
• The witness must confirm or it must be proved that they made the statement. If the witness does not
confirm or denies that they made the statement, then the making of the statement must be proved.
The best proof in this regard will be where the taking of the statement was video recorded. If the
statement was taken in writing, the court will have to be satisfied by the evidence of the persons who
were present when the statement was made and that the written record produced accurately records
the statement of the witness. It is likely in this instance that major conflicts of evidence will arise if the
witness subsequently denies the making of the statement. Accordingly, it will be a decision for the
Officer in charge of the investigation to determine which witness statements should be video recorded
from the outset of the investigation.
• The witness must be available for cross-examination.
• The statement must have been made voluntarily. A leading statement of the law in this context in
regard to confessions is to be found in the judgement of Griffin J. in the People (DPP) v Shaw
[1982] I.R. 1 at 60/61 where he stated;
“the primary requirement is to show that the statement was voluntary, in the sense in which that
adjective has been judicially construed in the decided cases. Thus, if the tendered statement was
coerced or otherwise induced or extracted without the true and free will of its maker, it will not be held
to have been voluntarily made. The circumstances which will make a statement inadmissible for lack of
voluntariness are so varied that it would be impossible to enumerate or categorize them fully. It is
sufficient to say that the decided cases show that a statement will be excluded as being involuntary if
it was wrung from its maker by physical or psychological pressures, by threats or promises made by
person in authority, by the use of drugs, hypnosis, intoxicating drink, by prolonged interrogation or
excessive questioning, or by any one of a diversity of methods which have in common that result or
the risk that what is tendered as a voluntary statement is not the natural emanation of a rational
intellect and a free will.”
• The statement must be reliable. In this regard, Section 16(2)(c) of the CJA 2006 provides:
“…(c)and, either(i) the statement was given on oath or affirmation or contains a statutory declaration by the witness
to the effect that the statement is true to the best of his or her knowledge or belief, or
(ii) the court is otherwise satisfied that when the statement was made the witness understood the
requirement to tell the truth.
Section 16(3) provides
“…(3) In deciding whether the statement is reliable the court shall have regard to (a) whether it was given on oath or affirmation or was video-recorded, or
(b) if paragraph (a) does not apply in relation to the statement, whether by reason of the
circumstances in which it was made, there is other sufficient evidence in support of its reliability,
and shall also have regard to (i) any explanation by the witness for refusing to give evidence or for giving evidence which
is inconsistent with the statement, or
(ii) where the witness denies making the statement, any evidence given in relation to the denial.
• The statement must be made under oath or affirmation or contain a statutory declaration of truth
or the court must otherwise be satisfied that, when the statement was made, the witness understood
the requirement to tell the truth.

12

• The court must not admit the statement if it is of the opinion;
• In all the circumstances, including the risk of unfairness to the accused or any of them, that it
is in the interests of justice that the statement not be admitted, or
• That the admission of the statement is unnecessary having regard to other evidence given in
the proceedings.
• Section 16(5) provides in estimating the weight, if any, to be attached to the statement, regard is
to be had to all the circumstances from which any inference can reasonably be drawn as to its
accuracy or otherwise.

Meaning of “statement”
“Statement” is defined in Section 15 of CJA 06 as follows:
“statement” means a statement the making of which is duly proved and includes (a) any representation of fact, whether in words or otherwise,
(b) a statement which has been video recorded or audio recorded, and
(c) part of a statement.
Best practice will dictate that where it is envisaged that recourse will be made to Section 16 of the CJA 06
in regard to the statement of any witness, their statement should be video-recorded in its entirety. This is a
task which should be conducted if possible by experienced members. Prior to the commencement of the
statement, the witness should be put on oath or the member may take a statutory declaration in accordance
with Section 17 CJA 06. The standard declaration under Section 21 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984
should not be relied upon in this context. The member taking the statement should impress on the witness
the importance of telling the truth and that should the witness wilfully lie, that they may be liable to
prosecution in the event of the statement being admitted in evidence.

3.17. Testing a witness for credibility
The first test of the credibility of a witness is whether they were present at the event and were alert and
attentive. It is not that inattention destroys credibility, but the story of the witness should be confined to
the things they saw and heard when they were paying attention. Any attempt to fill in gaps resulting from
any inattention can have the effect of ruining what is otherwise credible evidence.
The next test of credibility is concerned with establishing that the witness has personal knowledge of the
event. How did the witness happen to be in a position to see or hear what they have disclosed to the
interviewer? How do they recall the incident?
In the past witnesses have made complaints or witness statements for malicious purposes and this aspect
should be kept in mind in appropriate cases.
The following will be found useful in verifying points which frequently arise in interviewing witnesses:
• Date: The date is often important. How is it fixed by the witness, by reference to calendar, newspaper,
television or radio programme, events in daily life, fairs, markets, funerals, sporting events, etc.?
• Time: The time, when of consequence, is very important. How is it fixed by the witness? Is it by clock
or watch? Was it correct? By what standard was it checked? Can the witness read the time accurately?
Was time fixed by reference to a television or radio programme, by a special event, sunrise or sunset,
position of the sun, phases of the moon, movements of other persons, church bells, movement of buses
or trains, time taken to reach a certain place?

13

Page 14

Chapter 3 Witnesses

• Sounds: How are they estimated by the witness? What is the witness’s experience of similar sounds?
If of shooting, can witness distinguish between shots from a rifle, sporting gun, revolver, pistol, back
firing motor vehicle? How do they fix direction, distance?
• Size: How is it estimated by the witness? Under what conditions was the object seen? Attention
should be paid here to light, weather, etc
• Colour: Conditions of light may affect visibility or the appearance of colours. What conditions existed?
• Period of time: How was it fixed? Was it by reference to time taken to do a certain task, time taken to
move from one place to another - walk, cycle, or travel by motor vehicle? Experiments should be carried
out to validate the witness’s account of time frame
• Identification (See also Chapter 2.5 & 2.6):
• Personal: How does the witness identify a person? By appearance, dress, speech, gait, previous
acquaintance? If they know the person, for how long and when did they last see them?
• Articles of property: How do they identify an article of property? By general appearance, take
note of serial number or special mark? Can they distinguish between the article identified and similar
articles? Can any other person identify the article?
• When the question of identification arises, the factors which cause inaccuracy in observation should be
considered (see Chapter 2.2).
(For further information on identification generally see also Chapters 6 and 7)
Interviewers should ensure that the witness's vision was not obstructed. They should be satisfied that lighting
conditions were adequate and the distance was reasonable.
They should ascertain whether the witness’s eye sight was good or bad. That where the hearing of the
witness is involved, it is not defective or impaired and was not interfered with by other noises at the time.
They should also ascertain that where there is any impairment or defect in vision or hearing if it is of a nature
to cast doubt about the ability of the witness to make their reported observations.
Some people are able to lie without displaying any noticeable outward signs and persons who are truthful
sometimes display noticeable signs of extreme nervousness. For these reasons, and for the additional reason
that it is easy to misinterpret physical signs, the testing of a witness for credibility from signs of emotional
disturbance may be a matter for consideration, but cannot be regarded as conclusive.

Second and subsequent interviews of a witness
Normally, it is not recommended that witnesses be re interviewed repeatedly, because if the witness has
been properly interviewed in the first instance, a second interview should not be necessary. In complex
investigations, such as fraud cases, it is often the case that witnesses have to be interviewed on a number
of occasions. In these cases it would be impractical to do otherwise.
In testing the credibility of a witness, the need may arise for a second interview to elaborate on something,
in relation to some new information that has only come to light, or other problems which have developed.
It may be a simple matter of confirming information that indicates a witness is not reliable and not likely to be
telling the truth; it may be complicated by the need to discover why the witness has failed to measure up to
reasonable standards. It may be that the story of another witness has exposed gaps in the story of a witness
interviewed earlier, and a second interview is necessary in an attempt to fill voids in their memory. Major
conflicts may develop and earlier witnesses must be re-interviewed to determine how and why such conflicts
came about. Whatever the reason for re interviewing, the aim must be to keep re interviews to a minimum.

3.18. Evaluation of information received
The information received during an interview has no real value until it is properly evaluated and, if possible,
checked for accuracy and truthfulness as follows:
• The information should be compared in detail with the known facts of the crime
• Conflicting statements concerning the same case should be compared both in general and in specifics.
Conflicting statements should also be compared with the known facts of the case and considered against
the background information compiled on the witnesses. The human factors that affect the powers of
perception and memory may be the cause of the differences; they should be considered but should not
be relied upon too greatly to explain discrepancies or conflicts in different accounts of the same event
• The member in charge of the investigation should carefully review the entire case as it develops and
should give particular attention to any gaps appearing in the evidence
The formal interviewing of witnesses in serious cases should be carried out by members experienced in this
field. When a member has carried out a formal interview they should evaluate their own performance in
relation to their approach and their manner. This is particularly necessary when the interviewer has been
unsuccessful in extracting from the witness all the information that they were believed to possess thus
necessitating a change of approach.

3.19. Recording Statement
The purpose of taking a written statement from a witness is generally to:
• Provide a written record that will allow a decision to be made on the appropriate action which
should be taken
• Assist in the examination of witnesses in Court
• Justify the action taken by Gardaí
The most important thing in taking a statement is to get the full, complete story. The value of detail cannot be
over estimated. If in doubt as to whether or not something should be recorded in the statement, include it.
Hearsay evidence of a witness (although it may not be admissible in Court proceedings) may be very useful
and should not be excluded automatically as otherwise a statement may be difficult to follow for lack of
continuity. When used, hearsay should be shown clearly to be hearsay.
A statement should be in the active and not in the passive voice, for example, never use the phrase "A letter
was posted . . ." but always "I posted a letter . . ." Expressions like last Monday, last May or last Christmas
should be avoided. However the statement should of course be taken down in the precise language used by
the witness. Such expressions as outlined here can be clarified in the statement.
Where the accused was known to the witness before the date of the crime, details of previous knowledge or
association should be included in the statement. Whenever a person is referred to by description or by
nickname, their surname should be inserted in brackets.

The following rules should be observed in all cases:
• The statement should be written in longhand in ink or biro, preferably on the standard pre-printed
statement form that’s used in most cases; Form C8
• The writing should be from edge to edge of the paper, leaving no margin
• If necessary, both sides of each sheet of paper should be used
• Where more than one page is necessary, each should be numbered in numerical order, and the bottom
of each completed page should be initialled by the witness
• Any alterations or corrections necessary should be made by drawing a line neatly through the word or
phrase to be altered, leaving the original entry legible. There should be no scratches or erasures. Every
alteration and correction should be initialled by the witness
• Every statement should be headed with the full first name(s), surname, age, occupation and address
of the witness whether married or single, date, place and by whom taken. The following is a
specimen heading.
STATEMENT OF (full first names and surname)
Date of Birth:
of (full address):
taken on:
at (place)
by (name, rank, regd. no. and station)

(occupation):

Tel No.:

The standard declaration under Section 21 of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 as amended is printed on the
Form. This declaration allows the admission of certain statements in evidence in uncontested cases subject
to certain conditions. These conditions are set out at Section 21 (2) of the Act.
• When every material point has been covered, the statement should be read over to the witness, or, if they
so desire, be read by them. The witness should then be invited to make any alterations or additions they
consider necessary. These should be initialled by the witness and the Garda taking the statement. When
this has been done, the statement should be concluded in this manner. "This statement has been read
over to (or by) me, and I have been invited to make any corrections thought necessary in it; it is correct".
• The witness should be requested to sign the statement. The signature should be written on the next
available line and should be witnessed by the member(s) present. In the case of a child the signature
should also be witnessed by a parent, adult relative or guardian, if present. If a witness refuses to sign
a statement, the following should be inserted in place of the signature: "Declined to sign" and any
assigned reason for refusing stated. In the case of an illiterate person, they should be requested to make
a mark in place of the signature and should be clearly indicated as follows:
• His/her X mark (first name) (surname). This should be witnessed by members involved in the taking
of the statement who should also sign it.
• Nothing should be added to and no annotations should be written upon an original statement except
what the person making it has stated and signed. In major investigations original statements are
numbered with pencil. This has to be done to ensure an efficient filing of the statements.
Occasionally a witness will hand in a prepared statement. In this case the member receiving it should read
over the statement to the witness and have any necessary clarifications made therein. Any alterations or
additions should be initialled by the witness and, if necessary, the statement should be signed in the
presence of the member. The same procedure would apply in the case of a statement received other than
by personal delivery.

3.20. Negative Statements
Sometimes the most difficult cases to deal with are those in which an alibi is set up. The best way to
counter an alibi is to see and take statements from all persons who are thought likely to be called in support
of it at the earliest possible moment. Such a statement is called a ‘negative statement’ and it is taken from
a person in order to:
• Show that they know nothing of the person, matter or thing in question
• Prevent them from being used to give false evidence on behalf of the accused
If this is done, there is little likelihood of the witness being influenced later to give false evidence. Even if they
did give false evidence, it is doubtful if their evidence would receive much credence when it is revealed that
they made a statement, perhaps shortly after the crime, which was contrary to that evidence.
Great consideration, however, should be given to the taking of a negative statement from a person who is
known or believed to be in possession of some relevant information. There is the danger that if a negative
statement is taken immediately from such a person it may well have the effect of discouraging them from
disclosing at a later stage the relevant information which he possesses. In deciding whether or not to take a
negative statement, much will depend upon the character of the person and their relation to or sympathy
with the suspect. If it is believed that they will not change their mind and are likely to be used in establishing
an alibi, a negative statement should be taken.

3.21. Wife or husband of suspect
A wife or husband of the accused may not always be a compellable witness for the prosecution but their
statements are often useful and should be obtained unless there is some special reason for not doing so.
Sections 21 to 25 of the Criminal Evidence Act 1992 govern competence and compellability of spouses and
former spouses.

3.22. Report of member taking the statement
When a member takes a statement from a witness as to a fact, the member should note whether the witness
is confident, nervous, stutters or whether there is any reason to believe that they have defective sight or
hearing. In a major investigation, the member may take notes of such things and these may be discussed at a
conference or they might be included on a job sheet, or in a report to the incident room. However, it would
not be done in every case where a statement is taken. This would only be appropriate in major investigations.
The member should also give their opinion as to the witness’s veracity and any reason for thinking that at the
time of the crime they was under the influence of intoxicating liquor or drugs, or otherwise incapable of full
perception. Any relationship to, or association with, the accused should be stated, and also any reason to
believe him to be favourable or hostile to the accused or the prosecution.
In the case of a witness in a major crime who was near the scene at or about the time of the occurrence, the
report of the member taking the statement should contain a description of the witness, details of how he was
dressed at time of crime and descriptive particulars of any vehicle he may have observed at the scene. This
would normally be included in the statement itself.

3.23. Preservation of original statements
Original statements of witnesses should be preserved by
the member taking them until they are produced and
handed into Court. In major investigations where
incident rooms are set up, original statements will
always be handed in to the member in charge of
statements, to be filed and held there.
When the case is finally disposed of, the originals (if not
retained by the Court) should be forwarded to the
District Officer concerned for preservation with the file.
The District Officer will normally forward the completed
file to the Garda National Repository at Santry Garda
Station in accordance with the procedures outlined at
Section 7, Appendix II of this manual.
Copies of the above statements may be made by
another member, care being taken that the originals are
conveyed by hand to that member for attention as
outlined above. See also Chapter 4.34 and 8.23.
In the case of all witnesses, the original statements should be forwarded to the member in charge of the
investigation, and should be available for production at every Court hearing of the case. When the case is
finally disposed of, the original statements should be forwarded to the District Officer concerned if the latter
is not the investigating officer and preserved with the relevant file.

3.24. Request by a witness to read his statement
If a witness who has made or supplied a statement to a member requests that they be allowed to read the
statement before giving evidence in Court such requests should be acceded to subject to the terms of this
section. It must be borne in mind that trials are often held years after the statements are made, so not
allowing them to read their statements would be detrimental.

Chapter 3 Witnesses

However, care must be taken to ensure that the facility of allowing a witness to refresh their memory is not
used as an opportunity to coach that witness. It is also undesirable that a witness attempt to memorise their
statement. This issue was addressed by the Court of Criminal Appeal in The People [DPP] -v- Donnelly,
unreported, February 22, 1999 as follows:
“There is clearly a danger that if a witness seeks to learn the contents of his or her statement by heart then
when such witness gives evidence in court it may not be about what occurred but about what such witness
had said in the statement had occurred. This would clearly be wrong.”
If there are a number of witnesses, it is important that they are not afforded an opportunity to collude or
synchronise their evidence. The statement of one witness should not be read to another nor an opportunity
given to witnesses to compare statements.
Where prosecution witnesses have been given an opportunity to refresh their memory from looking at their
previous statements, best practice dictates that this fact should be disclosed to prosecuting counsel in
indictable cases so that the defence may be advised.
This decision will be taken by the district officer/prosecuting solicitor/counsel in summary cases.
In R -v- Westwell [1976] 2 All E.R. 812 it was held by the English Court of Appeal that where a witness has
refreshed their memory from a previous statement, this may affect the weight to be attached to their
evidence and this may cause injustice to the accused if the jury is not informed.
This matter was raised before the Court of Appeal in England in R. v. Richardson [1971] 2 Q.B. 484 where it
was held that there could be no general rule that witnesses may not before a trial see the statements which
they had made at some period reasonably close to the time of the event which is the subject of the trial.
The comment was added: "Indeed one can imagine many cases, particularly those of a complex nature,
where such a rule would militate very greatly against the interests of justice"

3.25. Furnishing investigation report and copies of statements
When furnishing the investigation report copies of statements should be fastened together to form a separate
file from the report. Copies of statements should be assembled as nearly as possible in chronological order or
the order in which they are referred to in the report. They should be numbered in sequence but in the case of
two or more statements from the same witness, each should receive the same number but should be
distinguished by letters in sequence. In all major cases, and in all cases where the number of witnesses
exceeds six, copies of statements should be prefixed with an index as follows:
• Statement Number
• Name of Witness
• Date taken
• Occupation e.g. D/Sgt, Postman etc.
It is usual to also include a separate alphabetical index of statements.
When making copies of statements, a margin should be left in the usual way and they should be typed in
double spacing. Great care must be exercised in copying a statement so as to ensure that there is no clerical
error. The copy should be checked, line for line, with the original. Copies of all statements taken during the
investigation of a case should be forwarded to the Law Officers who will decide which witnesses should
be called.

Chapter 4 Suspects

4.1. Introduction
One of the most important aspects of criminal investigation is the interviewing of, and the taking of
statements from, persons suspected of having committed crime. Interviewing suspects properly is generally a
difficult task and should be carried out by experienced personnel only. There is no short cut to becoming
proficient in this work. It requires considerable experience and knowledge of the law governing the
admissibility of confessions.
Even though confessions when properly proved are regarded as the best evidence, there is an inherent danger
in relying solely on confessions as evidence. All possible scientific evidence or other corroboration must be
utilised to the fullest possible extent whether a confession exists or not.
It is generally recognised that the complete success of an otherwise well conducted investigation may often be
enhanced or jeopardised by the investigating member (assuming that the subject is in fact the culprit) when
they come face to face with the suspect for the first time.
Tactically, a badly prepared interview could do more to educate the suspect as to how much or
little is known about their wrongdoing and thereby hinder further investigation. Accordingly, the
conduct of an interview with a suspect is a task which requires careful preparation – see further
at 4.21 below.

4.2. Questioning suspects: Objectives
The two basic reasons for questioning suspects are:
• To detect crime and bring the perpetrators to justice
• To seek the truth, and in doing so to ensure that it will stand the test in Court
A clear distinction must be made between a suspect who has not been arrested and a suspect who is in
custody. The authority of the Garda to question a suspect who has been arrested and taken into custody is
regulated by the Constitution, the Common Law, the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended, the Criminal
Justice Act, 1984 (Treatment of Persons in custody in Garda Siochana Stations) Regulations, 1987, the Criminal
Justice Act, 1984 (Electronic Recording of Interviews) Regulations, 1997 and the Criminal Justice Act, 2007.
Where the person in custody is under the age of 18 years the Children Act, 2001 as amended will govern their
detention. The constitutional aspects are primarily concerned with rules governing the admission of
confession evidence and unlawfully obtained evidence, the right of access to legal advice and the right to
privacy of the suspect.
Additional provisions in relation to the detention and questioning of suspects are contained in section 30 of
the Offences Against the State Act, 1939, as amended, (72 hours). Section 2 of the Criminal Justice (Drug
Trafficking) Act, 1996 (up to 168 hours) and Section 42 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1999 (where the suspect is
in prison). The maximum period of detention authorised by Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as
amended by Section 9 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2006 is 24 hours. Section 50 of the Criminal Justice Act,
2007 authorises a maximum detention period of 168 hours in respect of the offences specified in that section.
Where a suspect is arrested, the onus is on the prosecution to prove that the custody was lawful
and any admissions made were in accordance with the Judges’ Rules.
Where an admission is made by a suspect when they are not in custody, the prosecution must prove that such
admissions are made in accordance with the Judges’ Rules.

4.3. Difference between interviewing witnesses and questioning
suspects
There is a vast distinction between interviewing a witness and questioning a suspect who is subsequently
accused. What a witness states in a statement is very important as it helps to convey to the authorities all
available information, assists counsel or solicitors in the examination of the witness in Court, and justifies the
action taken by the Gardaí. (However, this is subject to the provisions of Part 3 of the Criminal Justice Act,
2006 which renders the statements of certain witness admissible in evidence). What an accused person has
stated is evidence at the trial, provable by the person to whom it was said or provable through the
document/media in which it was recorded. The interviewer when questioning suspects should realise that what
is said to them, or written for them, or dictated to them, by a person ultimately to be accused is of much
greater evidential importance than what is said to them, written for them, or dictated to them by persons who
are witnesses only.
The admissibility of a statement made by a suspect may ultimately depend on:
• Whether the confession was voluntary
• Whether or not the suspect was in lawful custody when they made it
In the People (DPP) v Mc Cann [1988] 4 I.R. 397 the Court of Criminal Appeal quoting from the Supreme
Court decision in the People v Shaw [1982] I.R. 1 stated that a mere desire to interrogate a person cannot be
put forward as a justification for an arrest under Section 30 but once there is proper suspicion it is clear that a
person may be interrogated after they have been arrested or even without being arrested at all. A Section 30
detention was not intended to be a “genteel” encounter. It is clear that the very word “interrogation” means
more than some form of gentle questioning and provided there are no threats or inducements or oppressive
circumstances then the Gardaí will always be entitled to persist with their questioning of a suspect. However,
the Gardai must also have regard to the physical, mental and emotional characteristics of the suspect in this
regard. See further under “oppressive questioning at 4.14.

4.4. Confessions and Admissions
A confession is a statement made by a person charged with a crime admitting that they committed it. The
term "confession" is generally confined to a full admission of guilt. An admission is an acknowledgement,
expressed or implied, by a party to a proceeding, conceding a point that invariably operates to their
disadvantage. It is an admission of some fact relevant to a crime. Insofar as the admissibility in evidence of
confessions and admissions is concerned, there is no distinction whatever. In this chapter a confession includes
an admission.

4.5. Admissibility of confessions in evidence
Before a confession is admitted in evidence the Court has two separate issues to consider as follows:
• Whether the confession was free and voluntary. It is a matter for the Court to decide whether or not
a confession is free and voluntary. A Court has no discretion to admit a confession or statement made by
an accused person which is inadmissible because it was not made voluntarily. lf a Court decides that a
confession was not voluntary, it must be excluded from the evidence - People v. Cummins [1972] I.R. 312.
• Whether the confession was obtained in contravention of the Judges' Rules. If a Court determines
that a confession was voluntary, then it will consider if it was obtained in accordance with the Judges'
Rules. Insofar as admissions alleged to have been made by an accused person are tendered in evidence

and were made or obtained in circumstances which are in contravention of the Judges' Rules, then the
Court has a judicial discretion to accept or reject the admissions in question provided the Court is satisfied
that they were voluntary - People v. Cummins (supra) If held to be admissible a confession is only
evidence against a person making it.

4.6. Origin and status of Judges' Rules
In 1912 the Judges, at the request of the Home Secretary, drew up some rules as a guide to police officers in
the investigation of crime. These rules do not have the force of law; they are administrative directions, the
observance of which the Police Authorities should enforce on their subordinates as tending to the fair
administration of justice. It is important that they should do so, for statements obtained from persons contrary
to the spirit of these Rules may be rejected as evidence by the judge presiding at the trial - R. v. Voisin [1918] 1
K.B. 531.
Four Rules had been stated by 1912. In 1922 five more were added to make a total of nine. The main
distinction between the voluntariness of a statement and the Judges' Rules is that a Court must exclude a
statement which is not voluntary, but in the case of a statement obtained in contravention of the Judges'
Rules, the Court has a discretion to admit or not to admit it in evidence but can only admit it if it is voluntary.
Mere non compliance with the Judges' Rules does not in itself render a statement inadmissible in law and that
fact in itself does not indicate that the statement is not voluntary - McCarrick v. Leavy [1964] I.R. 225.
Note re Caution:
It is important to note that with regard to the Caution, the judges rules set out below, do not take account of
s.19A of the Criminal Justice Act 1984 (as inserted by the CJA 2007) and other relevant provisions in relation
to inferences from silence. If a person is cautioned according to the Judges Rules, the prosecution will not be
able to rely on any inferences from the silence of the accused. A caution, encompassing an explanation of the
effect of their silence to the accused in terms they can understand will be required in this regard. See further
detail at Appendix II.

4.7. Judges' Rules
The Judges' Rules are as follows:
1. When a police officer is endeavouring to discover the author of a crime there is no objection to their
putting questions in respect thereof to any person or persons, whether suspected or not, from whom they
think that useful information may be obtained.
2. Whenever a police officer has made up their mind to charge a person with a crime, they should first
caution such person before asking any questions, or any further questions, as the case may be.
3. Persons in custody should not be questioned without the usual caution being first administered.
4. If a prisoner wishes to volunteer any statement, the usual caution should be administered.
5. The caution to be administered to a prisoner when he is formally charged should be in the following
words: "Do you wish to say anything in answer to the charge? You are not obliged to say
anything unless you wish to do so, but whatever you say will be taken down in writing and may
be given in evidence". Care should be taken to avoid any suggestion that their answers can only be used
as evidence against them, as this may prevent an innocent person from making a statement which might
assist to clear them of the charge.
Article 6(2) (a) of the Electronic Recording of Interviews Regulations, 1997 provides for an alternative form
of caution to be used where the interview is being video recorded. It should be in the following terms:
“You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so but whatever you say will be
taken down in writing and may be given in evidence. As you are aware this interview is being
taped and the tape may be used in evidence.”

6. A statement made by a prisoner before there is time to caution them is not rendered inadmissible in
evidence merely by reason of no caution having been given, but in such a case he should be cautioned as
soon as possible.
7. A prisoner making a voluntary statement must not be cross examined and no questions should be put to
them about it except for the purpose of removing ambiguity in what they have actually said. For instance,
if they have mentioned an hour without saying whether it was morning or evening, or have given a day of
the week and a day of the month which do not agree, or have not made it clear to what individual or
place they intended to refer in some part of their statement, they may be questioned sufficiently to clear
up the point.
8. When two or more persons are charged with the same offence, and statements are taken separately from
the persons charged, the police should not read these statements to the other persons charged but each of
such persons should be furnished by the police with a copy of such statements, and nothing should be said
or done by the police to invite a reply. If the person charged desires to make a statement in reply, the usual
caution should be administered.
9. Any statement made in accordance with the above Rules should, whenever possible, be taken down in
writing and signed by the person making it, after it has been read to him and he has been invited to make
any corrections they may wish.

4.8. Comments on Judges' Rules
Rule No. 1
This Rule acknowledges the right of the police to question persons, whether suspected or not in order to
progress the investigation. The word "suspected" is very important. It should be noted that there is no
mention of the caution in this Rule.

Rule No. 2
The word "caution" is mentioned here for the first time. The difference between Rules 1 and 2 is that in the
former case it is permissible to question a suspect without administering a caution, but in the latter case they
must be cautioned once the member has made up their mind to charge them. The phrase "has made up his
mind” is dealt with in 4.20.
There is another point about this Rule which should be mentioned. It might appear that the Rule permits the
questioning of a person after giving the caution. When a member has made up their mind to charge a person
they must then have a prima facie case against a person and there should be no necessity to ask any further
questions, except, for example, to clear up ambiguities.
Basic fairness of procedure will often require the caution to be administered even though the Garda has not
made up their mind to charge a person whom they are questioning. In People (D.P.P.) -v- Breen, unreported,
Court of Criminal Appeal, March 13, 1995, a Garda was having a general conversation with the accused
whose lands were being searched at the time for subversive firearms. The accused became agitated which
suggested to the Garda that he was worried that weapons would be found. The Garda asked him if something
was bothering him and if he wanted to talk about it. The accused made a number of incriminating statements
without the caution having been administered. The Court of Criminal Appeal held these incriminating
statements to be inadmissible on the grounds that the Garda should have appreciated that the accused was
about to admit involvement in the storage of subversive arms, therefore the caution should have been
administered on grounds of basic fairness before inviting any admissions.

Rule No. 3
The expression “person in custody” in this rule and the term “prisoner” used in the next four rules are in
law regarded as synonymous - Dunne v. Clinton [1930] I.R. 366.
