Where a person is detained under the provisions of Section 4 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended,
Section 2 of the Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act, 1996 or Section 50 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007,
such detention is authorised for the purpose of the proper investigation of the offence for which the person
was arrested. Where there are no longer grounds to believe that the detention of the suspect is necessary for
the proper investigation of the offence for which initially detained, there is provision to substitute another
offence within the meaning of the relevant Act and continue detention for the substituted offence as if it was
the offence for which originally detained (See section 2 of the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act,
1997; section 2 (6) Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act, 1996 and Section 50 (7) Criminal Justice Act, 2007).

In this regard, it is important to ensure that the proper procedure is followed in
each case to ensure compliance with the relevant provision authorising
detention where another offence is substituted for the offence in respect of
which the initial detention was authorised.
Notwithstanding the above, there is authority for the proposition that a person may be questioned in custody
in respect of some other crime other than that upon which they are in custody, provided that they are
cautioned first (R. v.Buchan [1964] 1 W.L.R. 365). In the People [A.G.] v. Cummins [1972] I.R. 312 at 324, the
following passage from the Supreme Court judgement is relevant in this context;
“Rule 3 of the Judges’ Rules, which refers to the necessity
to give a caution to a person in custody before he is
questioned, has been held to apply to a person being in
custody although the custody is not related to the matter
in respect of which he is being questioned”
Therefore, if a person in custody is questioned about some crime
other than that upon which he is in custody, he must first be
cautioned and can then be questioned about the other crime or
crimes, in the ordinary manner as indicated in Rule 1. Custody in
this sense is interpreted broadly and is not confined to a situation where a person has been arrested and
detained in a Garda Station. It seems it will apply to any situation where a person is under restraint, either
physical or verbal, and is not free to leave (R. v Narayan [1992] 3 N.Z.L.R. 145.
In the People [DPP] v. Morgan, unreported, Court of Criminal Appeal, 28th. July, 1997, it was held that it is not
necessary to repeat the caution parrot like on each occasion that the suspect is interviewed while in custody.

Rule No. 4
This means that if a person under detention asks to see a member and begins to make a statement to them,
they must be cautioned at the first opportunity.

Rule No. 5
It is obvious that the words of the caution in this Rule are only applicable when the formal charge is made and
can have no application when a violent or resisting prisoner is being taken to the station.

Rule No. 6
This Rule means that when a prisoner spontaneously makes a statement it should be recorded. As soon as they
have finished their remarks, they must be cautioned.

Rule No. 7
This Rule is self explanatory. To say "it is alleged so and so" stating to the prisoner what has been alleged
against him, is only a subtle form of cross examination - R. v. Winkel (1911) 76 J.P. 191.

In People (D.P.P.) -v- McCann [1998] 4 I.R. 397 the Court of Criminal Appeal refused to accept that there was a
breach of Rule No. 7 where the statement of the accused was taken by the investigating Garda asking leading
questions. The Court adopted the trial Judge’s findings that this method had been used at the request of the

maintaining direction and coherence.

Rule No. 8
There is nothing difficult to understand in this Rule, which applies whenever there are two or more persons
charged with the same offence. It obviously only applies where a prisoner in some way implicates another
prisoner. A copy of the statement should be given to the other prisoner and nothing should be said in
conversation with the accused to lure them into admissions. If they desire to make a statement they should
be cautioned.
As an alternative there would appear to be no objection to reading over the statement to the other prisoner,
and this would be the sensible thing to do if the other prisoner was, for example, illiterate. In the case of The
State v. Scully (1928) 62 I.L.T.R. 24, the accused was charged with receiving stolen property. Another prisoner
pleaded guilty to stealing the property and made a statement implicating the accused and as a result, a
statement was obtained from her. The statement was allowed in evidence and the Trial Judge stated that the
practice in the Central Criminal Court was to admit such statements.

Rule No. 9
The matters mentioned in this Rule are covered in 4.20 and 4.29.

4.9. Distinction between voluntariness of a confession and
observance of the Judges' Rules.
The voluntariness of a confession and observance of the Judges' Rules are separate issues. Both are closely
connected, particularly in regard to the administering of a caution, and can be easily confused. This is borne
out in three cases decided in the Supreme Court in which it was held that a lower Court had misdirected itself
and confused the issues. The three cases were as follows.

Mc Carrick v. Leavy [1964] I.R. 225
In this case the complainant (a member of the Garda Síochána) was investigating a hit and run accident in
which it was alleged that a motor car, of which the defendant (Leavy) was the registered owner, was involved.
In the course of his evidence in the District Court (on charges under the Road Traffic Act, 1933, now repealed)
the complainant stated that he interviewed the defendant and asked him who was driving the motor car at
the time of the accident. The District Justice decided not to admit evidence of the answer to the question on
the ground that as the defendant was not cautioned, it would not be a voluntary statement. In a case stated,
the Supreme Court ruled that the District Justice had erred in holding that a failure to comply with the Judges'
Rules in itself made the evidence involuntary and inadmissible. However, the District Justice could have refused
to admit the evidence on the ground that it was obtained in violation of the Judges' Rules.

The People v. Cummins [1972] I.R. 312
In this case the accused when in custody on one offence was questioned by a member of the Garda about
another wholly unconnected offence. There was no evidence to connect him with the other offence and the
reason why the member questioned him was because of his experience and knowledge of the accused, and
the nature of the offence. In reply, without any caution having been administered, the accused made a self
incriminatory statement. On the trial for the second offence, the trial Judge ruled out the self incriminatory
statement because it was not voluntary. In a case stated, the Supreme Court held that the trial Judge wrongly
rejected the evidence on the ground that it was not voluntary, firstly, because the Trial Judge appeared to have
assumed that evidence obtained in breach of the Judges' Rules is to be treated in the same way as a

confession obtained in circumstances which render it not voluntary; and secondly, the Trial Judge did not
appear to have directed his mind at all towards the question of whether it was calculated to produce an
involuntary confession. The Trial Judge could have excluded the evidence on the ground that it was obtained in
violation of the Judges' Rules.

The People v Farrell [1978] I.R. 13
In this case the Supreme Court was of the view that statements which have been obtained in breach of the
Judges Rules will normally be excluded. Chief Justice O’Higgins stated as follows:
“The Judges’ Rules are not rules of law. They are rules for the guidance of persons taking
statements. However, they have stood up to the test of time and will be departed from at peril.
In very rare cases, such as R. V Mills and Lemon [1947 K.B. 247 a statement taken in breach may be
admitted in evidence but in very exceptional circumstances. Where, however, there is a breach of
the Judges’ Rules, such as a failure to make a written record of the alleged confession or a failure
to invite the accused to accept or reject the statements, each of such breaches calls for an
adequate explanation. The breaches and the explanation (if any) together with the entire
circumstances of the case are matters to be taken into consideration by the trial judge before
exercising his judicial discretion as to whether or not he will admit such statement in evidence.”

4.10. Voluntariness of confessions
Confessions, whether oral or in writing, made by an accused person before trial can only be proved against
them if they were free and voluntary. To be free and voluntary a confession must not be influenced by hope or
fear, in the form of a threat, promise or inducement (expressed or implied) proceeding from, or in the presence
of, a person in authority or by "oppressive questioning".

4.11. Person in authority
The issue of whether a particular individual is a person in authority is always a question of fact to be decided
by the Court. A person in authority may be defined as anyone engaged in the arrest, detention, examination
or prosecution of the accused. The true test is: did the accused feel that the person to whom they confessed
(or as a result of whose intervention they confessed) could either make good their promise or carry out their
threat? If so, such person will almost certainly be regarded as a person in authority.
The nature of the charge determines whether an employer is a person in authority. The employer would clearly
be in authority in the case of a charge against their servant of embezzlement or arson of their property, but
not against the servant on a charge of murdering their own child.
A confession made to, but not induced by, a person in authority is admissible, while, conversely, a confession
induced by, but not made to, such a person will be rejected.
An inducement not held out by a person in authority but in their presence is the same as an inducement held
out by a person in authority unless the person in authority disassociates themselves completely from the
inducement - People v Murphy [1947] I.R. 236.

4.12. Inducement, threat or promise
The use or threat of violence by a person in authority would plainly render a confession inadmissible. There are
numerous recorded decisions showing that the vaguest hint of a threat or promise may be fatal in this respect.

If a confession is obtained from a person by means of an inducement or promise held out by a person in
authority, such inducement or promise must give the accused person reasonable grounds for hoping that by
making such confession they will gain some advantage or avoid some evil, usually in reference to the
accusation against them.
An inducement will not exclude confessions produced by it, unless it is of a temporal character. To urge that it
is a moral or religious duty to speak out will not affect the admissibility of what is said.

4.13. Duration of an inducement
Where an improper inducement has been held out by a person in authority, the Court must be satisfied that
the inducement had ceased to operate on the mind of the accused, and that the statement was not made
under the influence of that inducement, regardless of whether or not a caution is administered - The People v.
Murphy (supra). In the People v. Pringle, 2 Frewen 57, the Court of Criminal Appeal held that the effect of an
inducement on the accused had dissipated by the time he made his statement of admission because he had a
consultation with his solicitor prior to making the statement.

4.14. Oppressive Questioning
In The People v. Breathnach, 2 Frewen 43, and The People v. Pringle, 2 Frewen 57, the Court of Criminal
Appeal accepted with approval the description of ’oppressive questioning’ in the following cases.

R. v. Prager (1972) 56 Cr. App. R. 151
In this case, the English Court of Appeal adopted the following description of ‘oppressive questioning’ given
by Lord Mc Dermott in an address to the Bentham Club:
“Questioning which by its nature, duration or other attendant circumstances (including the fact of
custody) excites hopes (such as the hope of release) or fears, or so affects the mind of the subject
that his will crumbles and he speaks when otherwise he would have stayed silent.”

R. v. Priestly (1965) 51 Cr. App. R. 1
In this case, Sachs J. defined oppression in the context of questioning as "something which tends to sap,
and has sapped, that free will which must exist before a confession is voluntary ..." He went on
to state:
"Whether or not there is oppression in an individual case depends upon many elements. They
include such things as the length of time of any individual period of questioning, the length of
time intervening between periods of questioning, whether the accused person has been given
proper refreshment or not, and the characteristics of the person who makes the statement. What
may be oppressive as regards a child, an invalid or an old man or somebody inexperienced in the
ways of this world may turn out not to be oppressive when one finds that the accused person is
of a tough character and an experienced man of the world."

In People (D.P.P.) - D’Arcy
Unreported, Court of Criminal Appeal, 23 July, 1997 a juvenile detained pursuant to Section 4 of the Criminal
Justice Act, 1984 was interviewed by three Gardaí in the presence of his uncle. The Court held that this
admitted breach of the Custody Regulation did not warrant the exclusion of the incriminating statement made
by the juvenile as there was no suggestion that the interview was oppressive or unfair.

4.15. Examples of confessions held to be involuntary
Broadly speaking, anything suggesting to a suspect that their position in relation to the crime will be better or
worse according to what they do or do not confess will render a confession inadmissible. "It would have
been better if you had told at first" - R. v. Walkey (1833) 6 C. and P. 175. The expression "you are all right"
meaning "you will be all right" constitutes an improper inducement - The People v. Murphy [1947] I.R. 236.
The accused at a stage when he was a suspect asked a police officer: "If I make a statement will you give
me bail now?" The police officer answered "Yes". The accused then made a statement and it was held to be
inadmissible as it resulted from an inducement - R. v. Zaveckas (1970) 54 Cr. App. R. 202.
The accused asked a police officer whether a number of crimes would be "taken into account" (rather than
made the subject of separate charges). The police officer's reply was that the police would have no objection
to such a course. The accused then made a confession and it was held to be inadmissible - R. v. Northam
(1967) 52 Cr. App. R. 97.
A member investigating the unlawful taking of a motor car invoked statutory power under the Road Traffic
Act, 1961 to demand information as to who was driving the car on a particular occasion. At a subsequent trial
on charges under the Larceny Act, 1916 (only), it was sought to put the reply of the accused in evidence. It
was held by Court of Criminal Appeal that the reply was not voluntary as the failure or refusal to answer
would constitute an offence involving serious penalties. The Court expressed no opinion on the position which
would have existed if the charges had been for offences under the Road Traffic Act, 1961 - The People v.
Gilbert [1973] I.R. 383.

4.16. Examples of confessions held to be voluntary
A member said to the accused (in custody on another charge): "What
about Premier Tailors, I believe you did it?" The member was
referring to an alleged burglary and did not administer the caution
before asking the question. At the time the member had no evidence
implicating the accused but claimed that because of his experience and
knowledge of the accused and the nature of the crime, it was his belief.
The reply of the accused - which amounted to a confession - was found
by the Supreme Court to have been voluntary - The People v. Cummins
[1972] I.R. 312
The accused was being questioned regarding a murder and was not a
suspect at the time. A member told the accused that he "should not
be keeping us here all night; tell the truth and be finished with
it". A short time later he made an incriminatory statement. It was held
by the Court of Criminal Appeal that the remark did not constitute an
inducement and the statement was admissible - The People v. Galvin
[1964] I.R. 325

4.17. Confessions obtained by false pretence, trick or fraud
A confession obtained by means of a false pretence or trick is not on that basis alone inadmissible in evidence
but it will be if the effect of the false pretence or trick has been to obtain an involuntary confession. Similarly,
if there is any element of compulsion, this will also lead to the exclusion of the evidence – People (AG) v
Cummins (1972) I.R. 312.

In the Canadian case of R v Herbert (1990) 2 S.C.R. 151 at 180-181 McLachlin J. held;
“To permit the authorities to trick the suspect into making a confession to them after he or she
has…declined to make a statement, is to permit the authorities to do indirectly what the Charter
does not permit them to do directly.”
In that case a confession made by the accused to an undercover police officer placed in a cell with him was
held to be inadmissible. The European Court of Human Rights reached a similar conclusion in the case of
Allan v United Kingdom (2003) 36 E.H.R.R. 12
Confessions obtained by means of a trick or subterfuge can also be rejected on the basis that the confession
had not been obtained in compliance with a test of basic and fundamental fairness as laid down by Griffin J. in
People (DPP) v Shaw (1982) I.R.1.
Accordingly, such tactics should be avoided by members investigating crimes.

4.18. Confessions resulting from questioning
The admissibility of confessions resulting from questioning was considered by the Court of Criminal Appeal in
Attorney General v. Mc Cabe [1927] I.R. 129. The following passage has relevance.
"Though this matter has been discussed with varying opinion for many years, it is somewhat
difficult and perhaps not desirable, to extract a general positive rule from the judgments and text
writers. It can be posited, however, that it is not the law that a statement must be excluded from
evidence on the sole ground that either the statement was made in answer to questions put by a
police officer or that it was made without a caution having been first administered. But in such
cases it is a matter for the judge at the trial to decide whether, in his judicial discretion, he will
admit the statement or not having regard to all the circumstances and observing the legal
requirement that the statement shall be voluntary, though not necessarily, volunteered. He will
differentiate between statements resulting from questions put to a person not in custody for the
purpose of the investigation of crime and the tracing and arrest of the guilty party, and
confessions resulting from questions put to a person in custody, not so much to clear up doubtful
matters in a narrative by him, as to trap him or put pressure upon him".
In The People v. Murphy [1947] I.R. 236, the Court of Criminal Appeal held that a statement made by an
accused person, in answer to questions put by a police officer, is not inadmissible unless the questioning is of
such a character and carried on to such an extent, that the statement ceases to be free and voluntary.

4.19. The Caution
A legal caution is intended to bring home to the mind of the accused, after he has been charged, that he need
not make any statement but, on the other hand, if he does volunteer a statement it may be used in evidence
afterwards - Attorney General v. Durnan (No. 2) [l934] I.R. 540. The mere giving of a caution, however formal
is its terms, does not necessarily satisfy the requirements of the law that a statement must be a voluntary one.
In most cases the giving of a caution is sufficient, but where an improper inducement has previously been
offered to the accused by a person in authority, a substantially different question arises - The People v.
Murphy (supra).
In R. v. Voisin [1918] I K.B. 531, the headless body of a murdered woman was found and on which was a label
with the words "Blodie Belgium". Voisin was detained and, without being cautioned by the police officer
concerned, was asked to write down the words "Bloody Belgium". He did so with the same spelling as on

the label, and he was later charged with the crime, tried and convicted. On appeal to the Court of Criminal
Appeal, the evidence was held to be admissible even though no caution was administered. The functions of
the caution have tended to grow in importance in that the Courts generally use it as a technique for the
validation of evidence of confessions, not only against the suggestions of "fear or favour", but mainly against
any suggestion that the confession was obtained by any process that might be called unfair. The usual caution
is as follows:
“You are not obliged to say anything unless you wish to do so, but whatever you say will be
taken down in writing and may be given in evidence.”
Where a suspect volunteers to draw a map or sketch relevant to the crime they are suspected of or if they
volunteer to show the Gardaí the crime scene in question, the caution can be modified to suit those
circumstances, e.g. “You are not obliged to draw any map or sketch unless you wish to do so but any
map or sketch you do draw may be given in evidence” or “You are not obliged to point out any
scene unless you wish to do so but any scene that you do point out or any item found there may be
produced/given in evidence.”
The actual words of the caution must be proved in evidence on the hearing of the case - Attorney General v.
Mc Grath [1938] Ir. Jur. Rep. 5. Every member should know the exact words of the caution. The words of the
caution should be kept in a convenient place in the notebook.

4.20. When a caution should be administered
The occasions on which a caution should be administered are:
• Whenever a police officer has made up their mind to charge a person with a crime (Rule 2 of Judges' Rules
• When persons in custody are questioned (Rule 3 of Judges' Rules)
• When a prisoner wishes to volunteer a statement (Rule 4 of Judges' Rules)
• Where an allegation is being put to a suspect, whether in custody or not.
An easy yardstick to determine when the caution is to be given (and one which the Courts invariably use) is for
the member concerned to ask themselves this question: "What will I do if the suspect attempts to leave?" If
the answer to this question is that the member would arrest them or restrain them in any way, then they
should be cautioned forthwith.
Regarding the interpretation of the phrase "has made up their mind to charge a person with a crime",
this means the mind of the member concerned and not that of their superior or the law officers. In other
words, if the member has reached a stage when they would recommend the institution of proceedings against
the suspect, they may be regarded as having made up their mind to charge the suspect.
When a caution is administered to a suspect and they refuse to make a written statement, but make a verbal
statement, it is important that what they say be noted in writing without delay. This is necessary for two
reasons, (a) to ensure that the exact words are recorded, and (b) to conform with the wording of the caution
and the Judges' Rules.

4.21 Preparing for the interview
Preparation for the interview should be as thorough as time permits, and should include the following
elements:

Familiarity with the case
The members concerned should be thoroughly familiar with all the known facts and circumstances of the
crime and should be in possession of as many of the following details as possible:
• Nature of crime - murder, rape, robbery, etc
• Name and address of injured party
• Date, time and place of crime
• Description of the scene of the crime and the area generally
• The way in which the crime appears to have been committed and the known details of its commission,
e.g. the implements used, place of entry and/or exit
• Possible motive for the crime
• Incriminating factors respecting a particular suspect. Particular attention should be given to specific
details, especially those which have not become public knowledge

Background information of suspect
Members should collect as much data as possible concerning the suspect - personal background, age,
education, marital status, financial and social circumstances, present and former occupations, criminal record,
present physical or mental condition, whether addicted to alcohol and so on, how they are likely to react to
questioning – hostile or co operative, relationship to victim, association with the scene of the crime, ability or
opportunity to commit the crime, names of friends and associates. Apart from writing materials, the members
should as far as possible have the following in their possession:
• A pocket calendar for the purpose of fixing dates
• Particulars of the radio and television programmes in respect of the date of the crime
• Information of the weather conditions at about the time of the crime
• Rough sketch of the scene and general area where crime was committed
• Copies of any documents, e.g. witness statements and so on tending to show that the suspect was
involved in the crime
• Video/cctv footage
It is important to recognise that some of these items become exhibits once referred to and should be treated
as such. The usual rules on continuity of evidence will of course then apply.

4.22. Place of interview
A person surprised and found in the act of committing a crime, or found at or near the scene of the crime
shortly after its commission, is generally interviewed on the spot. Most criminals will at that time be under
considerable emotional strain.
In the majority of cases the interview is conducted some time after the commission of the crime. The best
place to interview a suspect is the place where the investigating members think the chances are greatest that
the suspect will disclose the truth.

12

Circumstances may compel members to interview a suspect at their home, at their place of employment or
elsewhere. Provided it can be arranged, a Garda station is by far the best place to interview a suspect.
The most suitable setting for interviewing a suspect is a room which would be free of interruptions and
disruptions during the course of the questioning. It need not be furnished lavishly - a table, chairs and writing
equipment are all that are necessary.
In relation to an arrested person, Regulation 12 (5) of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984, (Treatment of Persons in
custody in Garda Síochána Stations) Regulations, 1987 provides that as far as practicable interviews shall take
place in rooms set aside for that purpose.

4.23. Time of interview
In an ideal situation the suspect should not
be interviewed until the preliminary
investigation is completed, i.e. the scene of
the crime examined, evidence of a technical
nature collected and written statements
taken from witnesses. Notwithstanding this,
a suspect should be interviewed as quickly
as possible after the crime. Every minute
that elapses between the commission of the
crime and the time of interviewing affords
the suspect an opportunity to compose
themselves, to fabricate alibis or to
communicate with their accomplices.
Interviewing of suspects between the hours of 12 midnight and 8 a.m. whether arrested or not should be
avoided in light of the relevant Court decisions and the requirements of the Custody Regulations. There is
now an onus on the Gardaí to ensure that a suspect is rested prior to being interviewed, except where there
are extraordinary excusing circumstances such as the possibility of saving the life of a victim or the imminent
destruction of vital evidence.

Duration of interview
Each interview period shall not exceed the mandatory four hour time limit. The arrested person cannot waive
this limit. (D.P.P. -v- Vincent Connell [1995] 1 I.R. 244 C.C.A.)

4.24. Persons present at interview
If possible not more than two members should be present when a suspect is being interviewed. It must be
borne in mind that all members connected directly or indirectly with the interviewing of a suspect may have to
be produced in Court. Therefore for this reason alone, the number of members having access to the suspect
should be limited.
Regulation 12 of the 1987 Custody Regulations provides, inter alia, that not more than two members shall
question an arrested person at any one time and not more than four members shall be present at any one time
during the interview and that if an interview has lasted for four hours it shall be either terminated or
adjourned for a reasonable time.
In some cases a suspect will show an inclination to confide in one particular member, and where this occurs it
should be arranged insofar as possible.

In difficult cases, the value of calling in the assistance of a member who successfully questioned the suspect on
a previous occasion should not be overlooked.
The matter of privacy when interviewing a suspect is of the utmost importance. The interviewing of children is
dealt with at 4.27.

4.25. Conducting the interview
When there are two or more suspects, they should be interviewed separately, even in a case of on the spot
questioning as at 4.22. Much time and difficulty will be saved if the suspect who is more likely to be truthful is
interviewed first. Members should, when interviewing a suspect, have an open mind and be completely free of
all bias and prejudices. This will enable the members to judge things in their proper perspective and to read
correctly the mind of the suspect which is so essential if the interviewing is to prove useful.
All that is known about the case and about the suspect
should not be revealed at the outset. The suspect will be
attempting early in the interview to decide how much
information the investigating members have in their
possession.
A member should be extremely patient and should never
lose their temper during the interview even in the face of
great provocation, because if they lose their temper they
will be unable to formulate effective questions.
A suspect should always be given an opportunity to give
their own account of the crime under investigation. It
may be necessary to keep them from wandering or to
guide them when they become involved in unimportant
details. Members should always be on the alert to
maintain the initiative in the interview.

Guidelines
As stated earlier, before interviewing suspects the investigating members should acquaint themselves with all
details of the case, and the background, character and habits of all witnesses and suspects. It is impossible to
conduct an interview unless you are in possession of all the facts.
The following is a list of sources where relevant information may be obtained:
• Previous investigation reports
• Dialogue with other members of the investigation team with a view to developing a strategy for the
interview
• Reading statements already taken
• Visiting the crime scene
• Reviewing related circumstantial evidence
• Examining relevant physical evidence
• Examining the profile of the suspect.

The selection of questions to be put is important. These questions should be composed in a way that forms a
basis applicable to the crime under investigation. The following questions are useful in that complete answers
to them will assist in resolving the matter under investigation:
• What occurred?
• When did it occur?
• Where did it occur?
• Why did it occur?
• How did it occur?
• Who was involved?
• Which one did it?
When a suspect is answering questions, it is generally best to ask the questions in the following order: a broad
question first, then a more limited one and then a very specific question to remove any ambiguity.
Investigating members should seek evidence that is relevant to the crime under investigation. Any fact which
assists in establishing the truth, unless specifically excluded by some rule or principle of law, is relevant. The
following matters are usually considered relevant:
• The nature
• The capability
• The opportunity
• The method of commission
• Behaviour before and after the fact
• Avoiding interview or arrest
• Concealing one's identity and related pertinent evidence
• The giving of false evidence
• Escape or attempted escape
• Confessions
• Contributory circumstances
It is important that interviewing members direct their efforts towards resolving questionable issues. An issue in
an investigation is an occurrence, situation or object that requires explanation; therefore, questions should be
asked so that issues can be explained or resolved.
During the interview, cognisance must be taken of the law of evidence, be it direct, circumstantial or physical.
To this end, investigating members should obtain evidence that is relevant, competent and material.

Throughout the interview the suspect should be treated with consideration and respect regardless of the
nature of the crime or their status in life.

Electronic recording of interviews
The Criminal Justice Act, 1984 (Electronic Recording
of Interviews) Regulations 1997 (S.I. No. 74 of 1997)
makes provisions for audio-visual recording of the
interview of a suspect who is detained under one of
the four statutory detention provisions in a Garda
Station. Not all Garda Stations are equipped for the
electronic recording of interviews and the obligation
to electronically record an interview only applies
where the interview is being conducted at a Garda
Station where the equipment is installed.
In People (DPP) v Holland (Unreported), CCA, June 15 1998, the accused was detained in relation to serious
drug related offences at Lucan Garda Station which did not have the electronic recording equipment. The
Court of Criminal Appeal accepted the submission by the Gardaí that Lucan Garda Station was chosen
because it was the centre for the investigation of serious crimes in which the suspect was believed to be
implicated. The Court of Criminal Appeal was of the opinion that “if it had been established that the
accused was brought to Lucan Garda Station deliberately to deprive him of the benefit of having an
electronic recording made of his interview, then that would be a serious matter.”
There is no provision in the Regulations for the suspect to decline the electronic recording of their interview.
However provision is made for the termination of the recording during the course of an interview when the
suspect objects.
The Regulations of 1997 sets out in detail the procedures to be followed where an interview is being
electronically recorded in accordance with the Regulations. They also contain provisions in relation to the
custody and storage of tapes of the interview pending Court proceedings and their subsequent destruction
when the matter has been finally disposed of by the Courts. The procedures set out in the Regulations must be
strictly adhered to, so as to avoid any difficulties in relation to their admissibility. See Appendix II in this context.

4.26. Ages of criminal responsibility
When interviewing children (persons under 18 years) suspected of committing crime, due regard must be had
to the legal presumptions affecting criminal culpability, namely:
• No child under 12 years of age shall be charged with an offence except in the case of a child who has
attained the age 10 or 11 years and who is charged with murder, manslaughter, rape, rape under section 4
of the Criminal Law (Rape) Amendment Act 1990 or aggravated sexual assault.
• The rebuttable presumption under any rule of law, namely, that a child who is not less than 7 but under 14
years of age is incapable of committing an offence because the child did not have the capacity to know
that the act or omission concerned was wrong has been abolished.
• Where a child under 14 years of age is charged with an offence, no further proceedings in the matter
(other than any remand in custody or on bail) shall be taken except by or with the consent of the Director
of Public Prosecutions.
See Section 52, Children Act 2001 as substituted by Section 129 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2006.

4.27. Treatment of child suspects in Garda stations
Part 6 of the Children Act, 2001 (Sections 55 to 70) as amended by Part
12 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2006 deals with the treatment of child
suspects in Garda Siochana stations. This partly re-enacts, with additions
and modifications, the relevant provisions of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984
(Treatment of Persons in Custody in Garda Siochana Stations) Regulations,
1987, as they apply to children. It obliges members of An Garda Siochana
to have due regard to the personal rights and dignity of children in custody
and their vulnerability owing to their age and level of maturity. It provides
that a child suspect will be detained in custody separate from adults. It
provides for the attendance at a Garda station of the child’s parent or
guardian, and where the parents or guardian cannot or will not attend the
child is entitled to nominate an adult relative or some other adult to attend
at the Garda station. Where the child is in need of care or protection there
is a mandatory requirement on the member in charge of the Garda station
to inform the Health Service Executive which is obliged to send a
representative to the station ‘as soon as possible’.
Section 61 of the Act sets out the details regarding the interviewing of children, which cannot take place
unless in the presence of his/her parent or guardian, except where it is believed that their presence would
inhibit the investigation. The member in charge of a Garda station may remove an adult from where a child is
being questioned or a written statement is being taken where such member has reasonable grounds for
believing that the conduct of the adult amounts to an obstruction of the course of justice. If an admission is
made without a parent or another adult being present it should be recorded in writing by the member present
adhering to the usual legal principles. These admissions should then be read over as soon as possible in the
presence of a parent or guardian or where appropriate on videotape following arrest. Members should ensure
that admissions made by a child are recorded and are not lost simply because the appropriate person was not
present at the time.
The interviewing of children at school should be avoided unless absolutely necessary. If unavoidable the
consent of the school principal should be obtained, and if a parent or guardian is not present, the principal or
some other teacher should be asked to be present during the course of the interview.
Where a child is arrested, the interviewing of such child in the absence of a parent or guardian is governed by
Regulation 13 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 (Treatment of Persons in Custody in Garda Siochana Stations)
Regulations, 1987.

4.28. Form of statement
A written statement may take one of two forms, narrative or question and answer. The narrative form is the
one more commonly used however the question and answer version is also admissible. The statement should
be written down in the actual words used and should not be translated into ‘official’ or more orthodox
language.
Words or expressions should not be excluded from a statement because they seem to be unintelligible or
unusual. Such words and expressions may be natural to the suspect's profession, trade or business or may be
peculiar to their geographical area, environment, background or culture. However, an explanation should be
sought from the suspect as to the meaning of any language used by them which is doubtful or ambiguous
and the explanation given should be included in their statement. Any colloquial term used by the suspect
can be explained in brackets after the term used e.g. tackies (runners/trainers).

It is not the law that the statement of an accused must be rejected if it be not in their actual words; it is,
nevertheless, a matter for consideration by the trial Judge in arriving at a decision as to the admission or
rejection of the statement - Attorney General v. McCabe [1927] I.R. 129.

4.29. Recording written statements
When a suspect is willing to make a written statement, the rules listed at Chapter 3.19 for the recording of a
statement of a witness should be observed. In the case of a suspect, a notebook may be used to record the
statement, but only in exceptional circumstances.
In the case of a statement made by an accused person, the caution should be written in the third person
immediately after the heading as follows: "You are not obliged to say anything, etc.")
When a particular stage has been reached at which it is considered necessary to administer the caution, the
caution should be administered. The caution as given should be inserted in brackets in the statement before
continuing it, as follows: “(At this stage I – name and rank – cautioned – name of suspect – as follows:
‘You are not obliged to say anything, etc’.)”
If a person making a statement wishes to write it
themselves, and is able to do so, they should be
supplied with writing materials. In this instance the
member should write out the heading on the
statement and write in the caution where
appropriate. At the end of the statement, the
member should append a note to the effect that the
statement was written out in their presence and
that they have read it over to the person who wrote
it, and have invited them to make any corrections
they thought necessary. This is something which
rarely occurs. However, should such an instance
occur members should be in a position to approach
such persons where certain issues need to be
clarified after a full review of all the facts.
When an accused person makes a statement which is taken down in writing and read over to them and
acknowledged by them as correct, it may be admitted in evidence notwithstanding the fact that the statement
has not been signed by them – The People v. Wickham [1949] I.R. 180.
In the case of a suspect handing in a prepared statement, the member receiving it should read it over to the
suspect. Any alterations or additions should be initialled by the suspect and they should be requested to sign it
in the presence of the member. The same procedure would apply in the case of a statement received other
than by personal delivery.

4.30. Report from member(s) receiving a confession from the
accused
There is a presumption that a confession made out of Court by an accused person is not voluntary, and unless
the prosecution can rebut this presumption and show beyond reasonable doubt that it is voluntary, the
confession is inadmissible.

18

Chapter 4 Suspects

Therefore, in every case in which a confession - oral or written - is made to a member or members, the
member(s) concerned should furnish a statement of evidence outlining in full all the circumstances in which it
was received and everything which was said to or by the accused, before receiving the confession.

4.31. Memo of Interview
There is a view in legal circles that the memo of interview made by the members who conducted the interview
is more highly regarded by the higher Courts in this jurisdiction than the statement of admission, as it gives a
clearer picture of the chain of events which led to the making of the admission by the accused.

4.32. Where a suspect declines to make a written statement
If a suspect declines to make a written statement, it is proper for the members who questioned them to
exchange notes and examine these notes immediately afterwards to satisfy each other that they form an
accurate record of the events. It is vitally important that written notes are taken in the presence of a suspect
and the fact that he does not wish to co-operate should be noted. The member should record in writing other
questions that the member was obliged to put to the suspect and their negative replies. At the end of the
interview all notes, including both questions and answers, should be read over to the suspect and they should
be invited to sign same.
If each member is satisfied that the notes of the other member are accurate, then they should initial these
notes with the date and be satisfied to use them at a subsequent stage to refresh their memory - R. v. Bass
(1953) 37 Cr. App. R. 51.
In People (D.P.P) v Mc Cowan [2003] 4 I.R. 349 the suspect in a larceny investigation refused to answer
questions put to him by the Gardaí on the advice of his Solicitor. The Court of Criminal Appeal was of the view
that the failure of the Gardaí conducting the interview to take sufficient notes of the denials by the accused
was a “serious matter.” If the Gardaí thought it important enough to ask a question then the answer must
be recorded. Where the same question is asked several times, the reply should be recorded on each occasion
so as to give a true flavour of the interview.

4.33. Verifying information received
The information supplied by a suspect or an accused should be evaluated in respect to known facts, the
information supplied by witnesses and the physical evidence available.
As far as possible, every item of information supplied by a suspect should be verified. In particular, in a case
where there is no evidence apart from a confession to connect a suspect with the crime, it is very important to
take steps to verify and corroborate, if possible, all aspects of the confession, because false confessions,
though unusual, are not unknown.

4.34. Preservation of Investigation Files and Furnishing Copies of
Statements
The member in charge of the investigation should carefully preserve original statements of accused persons
until they are produced and handed into Court. When the case is finally disposed of, the original statements (if
not retained by the court) should be forwarded with the rest of the file to the Garda National Repository at
Santry in accordance with the procedure outlined at Section 7, Appendix II of this Manual.

In serious cases where an incident room has been established, the procedure outlined at Chapter 8.23 should
be followed. Again, the file should be forwarded to the National Repository at Santry on conclusion of
proceedings or in accordance with the procedure set out at Appendix II already noted above.
Extreme care must be exercised in copying a statement so as to ensure that it corresponds in every detail with
the original. When a person, against whom proceedings have been brought or are contemplated, makes a
statement to a member, a copy of their statement should be furnished to the person or their solicitor, if
requested. When possible, photocopies should be supplied.

4.35. Where a suspect has no knowledge of the Irish or English
languages
Where a person who does not understand either the Irish or English language is to be questioned, it will be
necessary to secure the service of an interpreter. The following procedure should be observed.
• The suspect should be questioned through the interpreter who should record the statement in the
language in which it is made
• A verbal translation should be made as the statement is taken so that any ambiguities can be rectified at
the time.
• Any statement made should be read over to the maker and signed by them.
• An official Irish or English (as appropriate) translation should then be made and be proved (by the
interpreter) as an exhibit with the original statement.
• Depending on the specific case, consideration should be given to supplying a separate interpreter for use
by the suspect’s solicitor for the purpose of fairness and transparency

4.36 Access to a solicitor
Where a suspect is detained in Garda custody, that person has a constitutional right of access to their solicitor.
(Supreme Court D.P.P.-v- Healy [1990] ILRM 313). The Court further held that this right of reasonable access
means that “in the event of the arrival of a solicitor at the Garda station, in which a person is
detained, an immediate right to that person to be told of the arrival and, if he/she request it an
immediate access.”
The Court would not accept the evidence of a Garda Superintendent who stated that he believed that it would
be bad manners to interrupt the ongoing interrogation in order to facilitate access. The fact that a suspect is
making a statement to the Gardaí would not be considered a valid reason to delay the solicitor access to their
client. The Supreme Court concluded in the Healy case that if a person in custody requests a right of access to
their solicitor and that request is refused, the refusal will render their detention unlawful.
In the People (D.P.P.) -v- Finnegan [Unreported], Court of Criminal Appeal, July, 15, 1997, the Court was
considering a case where a suspect in custody was permitted to phone his solicitor, but was not permitted to
do so without the Garda present eavesdropping on his conversation. The Court held that the constitutional
right of access to a solicitor necessarily implies, apart from the most exceptional circumstances, a right to
communicate in private and out of hearing of the Gardaí. The denial of a right of access will result in the
exclusion of any incriminating statement obtained from the suspect during the period of unlawful detention.

In Lavery -v- Member in charge, Carrickmacross Garda Station [1999] 2 I.R. 390 the Supreme Court held that
the right of access does not extend to having the solicitor present during the course of the interview while the
Gardaí are interrogating the suspect. Judge O’Flaherty stated that not only is the solicitor not entitled to be
present at the interview, but also they are not entitled to the records of the interview before the suspect has
been charged.
In the People (D.P.P.) -v- Buck [2002] 2 ILRM 454 the Supreme Court held that there is no obligation on
members of An Garda Siochana to postpone the arrest of a suspect until such time as a solicitor is likely to
be available.
Section 5 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984, places an onus on the member in charge of the station to ensure
that a detainee is informed without delay of their entitlement to have access to a solicitor and, when the
detainee so requests, to inform the solicitor of the request for access. See further detail on a suspect’s
entitlement to legal advice under ECHR principles at Appendix II.

Chapter 5 Searches

5.1. Introduction
All members will at some stage of their service be involved in the carrying out of searches. It is imperative that
all searches are carried out in accordance with the relevant legislation and have due regard for the human
rights and dignity of the individuals involved.

5.2. Objectives
When it comes to criminal investigations, searches will be carried out with a view to any one of the following:
• Locating offenders
• Locating instruments, weapons or vehicles used in the commission of a crime
• Locating evidence to connect particular individuals with a crime
• Locating and recovering stolen or otherwise unlawfully obtained property
• Locating a missing person or persons.

5.3. Categorisation of Searches
In order to provide guidance for members with regard to the type of search to be conducted, and the level of
resources to be allocated, the following categorisation process should be utilised.

Category One Searches
This category relates to a simple search of an individual where the members concerned are looking for stolen
property and/or drugs.
For category one searches members may conduct the search in the normal manner and should ensure that
they maintain a full record of the search in their notebook.

Category Two Searches
This category relates to a search of a vehicle, building
or place, for stolen property, evidence in relation to a
crime, and/or drugs.
For category two searches members should give
consideration to consultation with the local Divisional
Search Team Leader. Due consideration should be
given to the type of search being carried out and the
possible requirement for specialist searchers.

Category Three Searches
This category relates to a search of a vehicle, building,
person or place, which is being conducted in the
course an investigation into serious crime.
For category three searches members must consult with the local Divisional Search Team Leader and should
ensure in as far as practicable that the Divisional Search Team is utilised in all aspects of the search. The Garda
Technical Bureau should also be consulted.

5.4. Types of Search
There are five basic types of search:
• Person Search
• Building Search
• Vehicle Search
• Area Search
• Missing Person Search.

Person Search
There are four types of person search.
• Quick
• Detailed
• Strip
• Intimate
The type of search that you choose to carry out will depend on a number of factors:
Circumstances
• Superficial search (entry to sports event)
• Preliminary to more detailed on arrest
• High/Low degree of confidence required
Environment
Is it practicable to conduct the type of search?
Authority
Under what authority is the search being conducted? All searches must be carried out under the European
Convention of Human Rights (ECHR) guiding principles, i.e. Legality, Accountability, Proportionality, and
Necessity. Good searchers will know when, where and to what extent they can search.
Practical Tips
Before the search
• At all times - think safety, possible assault, needles, infection, and so on
• Work in pairs - a searcher and another officer
• Is this the best place to conduct this search?
• Check ground/floor prior to search - verify area is clear with subject
• Safety Officer to note subject’s demeanour
Commencing the search
• Explain to the subject how the search is going to occur.
• Do not lean subject against a wall (possibility of forensic contamination)
• Ask subject first if they have any illegal items

Conducting the search
• Invite subject to empty pockets- unless you are concerned about member’s safety
• Invite subject to remove clothing - unless you are concerned about member’s safety - know your powers
• Search items of clothing as they are removed
• Attribute baggage/clothing to the subject
• Consider information from clothing name tags and laundry marks
• Be systematic - head to foot down one side then up the other
• If your search is interrupted by a find/conversation, make sure you know where you are and overlap
when you recommence searching
The physical aspects
• Do not pat subject, danger from needle stick, - firmly stroke and squeeze
• Seek out the natural hollows of the body - under arms, small of back and soles of feet
• Carefully check seams, waistbands, lapels, padding, cuffs, turn-ups, socks and medical dressings
(consider use of medical practitioner)
During the search
• Do not stand directly in front or behind the subject at any time
• Searcher should avoid eye contact at all times
• Use your two best search tools - eyes and hands
• Do not be shy or intimidated by subject - most prefer to be searched firmly and professionally
Collection of evidence and recording
• Do not show emotion on finding items
• Do not set items aside from other articles found
• Keep all articles out of reach of the subject.
• Use your notebook and record details of search
Remember the majority of searches are negative so always give due consideration to the subject and the
importance of Garda Community Relations.

Inventory
Where items have been seized in the course of any search, particularly where there are numerous items
involved, a full inventory should be prepared by the member in charge and agreed in writing with the
owner/occupier/suspect before concluding the search.

Building Search
Before commencing a building search make sure you know your legal powers and that you have the
appropriate authority to carry out the search.
Practical Tips
A building search can be divided into four phases:
• Planning
• Entry
• Search
• Exit

Planning
• Check that you have the correct authority to search
• Is it the correct address?
• Have there been any previous searches at this address?
• Details of occupants?
• Any dogs or other occupants likely?
• Are outbuildings, land or associated vehicles covered in the search?
• Get specialist advice from the Search Team Supervisor or members trained in searching
• Get the assistance of specialist teams where necessary
• Consider nominating a note taker and exhibits officer
• Get the right equipment to ensure the maximum search with the minimum damage
• Decide the most appropriate method of entry
• Ensure entry/search teams are properly briefed
• Establish the parameters and extent of search
• Establish the action to be taken on making a find e.g. photograph in situ, notify exhibits officers, recover
• Establish a plan to deal with callers/phone calls to premises
Entry
• Consider cordon
• Establish entry and initial areas of responsibility
• Quickly locate occupants and secure them
• Conduct a ‘quick’ search of occupants where they are found
• Assemble occupants into one room for a more detailed search, (two rooms if male and female)
• Identify responsible person and inform them of search and serve warrant
• Stand down cordon if appropriate.
• Establish control and keep it - do not allow occupants to influence or dictate your search.
• Maintain calm and quiet conditions.
• Causing minimum damage on entry and during search is a key factor to a good search
Search
Preliminary actions:
• Consider a pre-search damage check with occupant
• Consider photographs or video of premises prior to search
• Ask occupants if they have any illegal articles on the premises
• Draw plan of the premises, use notebook or search log
• Consider searching kitchen/bathroom first
• Note meter readings on gas/electricity (even if not used)
Searching:
• Work in pairs - be systematic
• As you enter your search area, carry out a visual search for obvious items
• Think about what you would do if you occupied the premises, where would you hide items?
• Think forensic and health and safety - wear latex gloves at all times
• Use your eyes as well as your hands - items may be more than arms reach away
• Consider the run of the floorboards before you move furniture
• Decide on your method before you start to search
• Move furniture to one half of the room, having made space for it
• In empty half of room search walls, including fittings, ceilings, floors
• As you move furniture to the searched half of the room search the furniture clearing the other
half of the room as you do so. This will ensure that you have searched ALL furniture

• Search other half of room ensuring you overlap
• Consider moving furniture to another room or outside
• Remember - you can buy small safes which have the appearance of canned food
• Loft insulation - always wear a mask, initially pat down and feel for items underneath
• Do not touch computers - if you consider a computer to be evidence seek specialist advice
(See also 13.19, Computers and crime)
• Correct use of basic search tools will cause minimum damage
• Consider measuring external and internal walls to detect larger hides
• Think evidence and intelligence - diaries, address books, phone numbers, bank statements, letters
documentation, lap tops, P.D.A.’s, G.P.S. systems etc.
Recording finds and results
• Report any finds, damage, and completed tasks to the member in charge of search
• Ensure that accurate contemporaneous records are kept of search. Do not allow the search to
‘overtake’ the records
• Consider use of video when counting large sums of money
Exit
• The sergeant in charge of search should ensure that all documentation is correct and completed
• All exhibits must be accounted for
• All equipment must be accounted for and all search team rubbish must be cleared
• The sergeant in charge of the search should carry out a visible damage check with the occupant
and where possible video record it
• Closing readings on meters should be taken
• Ensure premises are left secure

Vehicle Search
A vehicle, as part of its construction, has a number of voids. Some of these are easily accessible and
others not. Think about why you are conducting the search. Are you looking for items that are hidden
but retrievable, or is the intelligence such that you require a much higher level of confidence that the
vehicle is clean.
Practical Tips
Preliminary actions
• Know and understand your legal powers to search
• If you have stopped the vehicle take time to study the faces of the driver and passengers - are
they relaxed or nervous?
• Be guarded of the over-polite - particularly from persons who you believe would not normally act
in that way
• Ask the driver to switch off the engine, operate accessories, open the bonnet and describe the
contents of the boot
• Consider the services of the Stolen Motor Vehicle Investigation Unit if you suspect the vehicle
to be stolen.

The Search
• A careful systematic and thorough approach with good communication between pairs of searchers
will produce a good search.
• Treat the vehicle as five areas:
• Interior
• Exterior
• Boot/cargo area
• Engine compartment
• Underside.
• If you have two pairs of searchers, one pair should search the dirty areas with the other searching
the clean areas
• Start by a visual ‘no touch’ search around the outside of the vehicle
• Consider safety e.g. disconnect battery and ensure there is sufficient room to search underside of vehicle
• A vehicle has identical features on either side - work in pairs from front to back and talk to your partner
as you discover any possible voids, ensuring that they have identified the same before moving on
• Although most items will be hidden in the interior or boot do not discount the possibility of the vehicle
being altered in some way to introduce a convenient hide
• Check that all accessories are in working order and investigate further if not
• Do not enter the interior if members are underneath – communicate at all times
Finds
• Be mindful of the need to forensically prove ownership of any finds.
• Wear latex gloves and exhibit them with any finds.

Area Search
Practical tips for planning an area search
• Identify area to be searched
• Identify the owner of the land
• Arrange aerial photographs and/or aerial reconnaissance
• Divide into sectors using clearly identifiable landmarks – hedgerows, roads, ditches etc
• Obtain maps from local authority or mapping section
• Consider other services - local authority and forestry commission
• Consider the type of area search - are you looking for something that needs to be recovered by
offender? (Winthrop method) Or are you looking for a missing person or clues to a crime?
• Base your search on intelligence
• Identify an Incident Command Point (ICP)
• If the search is being carried out under a warrant, ensure it covers overlap boundaries.
Winthrop Method
If the search is looking for a hide/cache consider the needs of the terrorist, criminal or associate. They are:
• Ease and speed of access/egress to site – therefore near a road or track
• Easily identifiable landmark on road or track e.g. post, tree
• Good camouflage and easy access to hide from road or track
• Easily identifiable landmark to hide e.g. small copse, tree, other outstanding but natural feature
• Accessible and visible both day and night

Practical tips (Winthrop)
• Establish control point within search area
• The whole team should observe and identify likely Winthrop points
• Boundary walk – split the team in two with half walking clockwise and the other half walk
anti-clockwise around boundary of the search area
• Each team should cross over and cover the entire boundary
• All boundaries to be overlapped by 1 metre (inside/outside)
• One pair should work inside the boundary looking in, the other to work outside looking out for
prominent objects such as access or exit markers
• Pairs of searchers to search Winthrop Points to a radius of approximately 15 metres
• Remaining area to be searched using extended line search
• Open areas such as sports grounds only need a searcher every 20 metres. After one sweep, reform
line at right angles and complete a second sweep of area
• On more complex areas searchers should be positioned dependent on the level of confidence required.
If the area narrows - drop off searchers from lines and re-deploy
• Always look up as well as down
• Ensure you are equipped with the right tools for the job – coveralls, gloves, knee pads, proper foliage
clearing tools
• Remember, the offender or terrorist may be watching you search – you may not want them to know
of any significant finds so don’t make a big show of anything

Missing Person Searches
While not categorised separately every search for a missing person is an emergency and should be treated as
such. The current training provided to Divisional Search Teams includes modules on missing person behaviour
and subsequent search management. In all searches involving missing persons the expertise of the local
Divisional Search Team should be called upon.
In addition to the basic searches outlined above, the search for a missing person involves the application of
specific search management skills and resources. Given the nature of this type of search all such searches
should be controlled by an appropriately qualified Search Manager not below the rank of Sergeant.
“A missing person is anyone whose whereabouts are unknown and the circumstances of the disappearance
present a risk of harm to the missing person or any other person”.
A full and accurate description of the missing person must be obtained for information on any subsequent
searches. Place Last Seen or Last Known Position must also be established.
The first Garda attending to a report of a missing person will be responsible for ensuring that a search is made
of the person’s address and any other relevant premises or locations. The objectives of the search are to
discover:
• The missing person
• Evidence relating to their disappearance
• Intelligence that may lead to their discovery
The Garda taking the report should glean as much information as possible that will assist in the search for the
missing person (e.g. mobile phone, who they last spoke to, any notes left, evidence that they have planned or
prepared for their disappearance and so on).
Obtain a photograph of the missing person as soon as possible.

Place Last Seen
When a search for a missing person commences, the likeliest places should be searched first. These
include the place last seen, last known point, and the routes over which the person may have
travelled. A quick superficial search should first be made since this may save valuable time. If
unsuccessful, the same ground should be covered later by a methodical search in the manner
determined by the appointed Search Manager.
Significant assistance can also be provided by an IMEI mobile phone trace. This service can be provided by
mobile phone service providers. IMEI traces and the use of Garda dogs should be looked upon as a first
response resource.

Civilian Involvement
If civilians take part in searches, they should be under Garda control. Civilians should not be called in to search
for a body where foul play is suspected. However, Search Managers should be aware of the possibility of large
numbers of civilians presenting themselves as volunteer searchers. Accordingly, appropriate plans should be
put in place to utilise this resource as effectively as possible from a Garda point of view.
Involving civilians in such searches can be highly problematic, especially in an emotionally charged atmosphere
where the search is for a missing child. There are huge advantages to be gained through the use of civilian
searchers and potential pitfalls in the event that Gardaí turn down such offers of assistance. The rule of thumb
should be that once confirmation of a potential crime is received then all civilian searchers should be
withdrawn from the search area.
The initiative for organising a search should never be permitted to pass to the relatives or friends of the
Missing Person. Care should be taken to prevent civilians interfering with a body or potential items of
evidence. The possibility of the culprit being among the search party should not be overlooked.

5.5. Role of Search Manager
Search management is ultimately the responsibility
of the Search Manager. The Search Manager must
have the required equipment, people and other
resources available to carry out the search
effectively. They must be fully conversant with the
availability and capability of all search resources.

5.6. Safety/Risk Assessment
Members should exercise due care and vigilance in the conduct of searches both for their own safety and the
safety of all other persons who may be affected by their acts and/or omissions.
District Officers in co-operation with local management will prepare a risk assessment prior to carrying out a
search and this will form part of the search briefing documents (see An Garda Síochána’s Generic Risk
Assessment for further guidance).
Appendix III at the end of this chapter gives guidance on the safety controls to be adhered to in the
risk assessment.

5.7. Co-ordination and briefing of search party
The Search Manager should set up an Incident Command Point (ICP) as close as possible to the area of
operation. The nearest Garda station should be used if convenient but in remote areas the use of any
suitable vehicle and/or location should be considered. Details of all messages or incidents should be recorded
in an incident log. The log should be cross-indexed with ‘action-taken’ reports, name indexes, and
townland indexes.
An Inspector or Sergeant and a sufficient number of Gardaí should staff the Incident Command Point. Where
possible, members with good local knowledge should be detailed for this duty. In addition, members with
good local knowledge should be utilised as radio operators.
The Search Manager will give consideration to the category, urgency, and purpose of the search, the size of
the area it is proposed to cover, the type of terrain, the prevailing weather conditions, and remaining hours of
daylight. As a rule, searches during hours of darkness should be avoided unless there is some real urgency.
The Search Manager will brief search teams on all relevant aspects of the search. The method of briefing will
depend on the number of personnel. If the number is large, the Search Manager should brief individual party
leaders, and they in turn should ensure that all members of their parties are fully briefed.
It may be necessary to prepare and issue to party leaders a search operation order on the following lines:
• Information: nature of crime or incident and any subsequent developments arising from
investigations up to the present date
• Description: fullest possible details of person(s) (photographs if available) or article(s) being searched for
• Method: topographical description of area and manner in which it has been divided for search
• Personnel: names of search party leaders and other personnel allocated to each party
• Conduct of search: manner in which individual search party leaders will deploy their resources and
action to be taken if anything significant is found
• Administration: routine administration details such as parading and retiring points, times and places
of refreshments, availability of special clothing and so on

Searches should be carried out by properly equipped teams which may require additional specialist support

Divisional Search Team
Each Garda Division has at its disposal a Divisional Search Team. A member of Inspector rank has responsibility

The Divisional Search Team is trained in the following: Missing Person Search, Land/Area Search, Building
Search, Vehicle Search, Person Search, Defensive Venue Searching, Defensive Vehicle Searching, Defensive
Route Search, European Convention of Human Rights (ECHR) and Risk Assessments.

Garda Operational Support
The Garda Operational Support Unit includes Garda Air Support Unit, Garda Mounted Unit, Garda Dog Unit,
and Garda Water Unit. The call out procedure is by fax or telephone to Superintendent Operational Support,
Harcourt Square, Harcourt Street, Dublin 2. Appendix II at the end of this chapter gives an outline of the types
of searches that can be undertaken by Operational Support units.

5.9. Search of a Major Crime Scene
The Senior Investigating Officer (SIO) should utilise a Garda Search Manager or Search Team Leader and agree
the following:
• Parameters of the search
• The type/detail of items sought
• The probability of finding items in the area identified (based on intelligence)
• The probability of detection (based on the agreed level of search, resources, skills and
equipment available)
The Search Team should operate bearing the following points in mind:
• Pay particular attention to fragile evidence found that may be destroyed or contaminated if it is not
collected when discovered
• If any doubt exists as to the value of an item, treat it as evidence until proven otherwise.
The appointed member will take responsibility for the conduct of all searches relating to the investigation.
This member will coordinate all search resources, and will be responsible for keeping a detailed record of all
aspects of searches conducted during the course of the investigation. In addition this member will ensure that
all relevant details are entered under the appropriate case in the PULSE system.

This is the application of scientific and methodical archaeological techniques to the locating, recording and
recovering of human remains in a forensic context. Members who are searching and who become suspicious
of a particular site or area should preserve it and seek the advice and assistance of the Garda Technical Bureau.
The Garda Technical Bureau when assisting in carrying out searches will utilise the following to pinpoint

• Observation Techniques
• Geophysical Survey
• Ground Penetrating Radar
• Cadaver Dogs.

5.11. Searches Involving a Potential Chemical, Biological,
Radiological and Nuclear (CBRN) Incident.
CBRN stands for Chemical, Biological, Radiological and Nuclear. As a term CBRN should only be used in the
context of a deliberate and malicious act to cause harm or fear amongst a population by the use or threatened
use of CBRN materials.
CBRN is terrorist crime focused and members may therefore find themselves in a situation where they are
conducting a search which can either be a direct result of intelligence on a specific CBRN related incident or a
search which develops into a CBRN incident as a result of items discovered in the course of the search.
If a search is required based on specific CBRN intelligence then that search should be conducted by the Garda
CBRN Response Team and the member in charge of the investigation should make appropriate contact with
the CBRN team leader through Command & Control, Harcourt Square, Dublin 2.
In the event that members are involved in a search which develops into a CBRN incident the guidelines at
Appendix 1 at the end of this chapter are set out to assist in identifying the type of agent involved and the
appropriate steps, which should then be taken.

5.12. Reports on searches
When a search of premises or other private property has been carried
out, a report should be prepared immediately by the senior member
present giving details of the members of the search party, the time
involved in the search, the results of the search, and particulars of any
complaints received from the owner or other person in charge. The
report should be passed without delay to the member in charge of the
sub-district concerned. In any case in which an Officer or Inspector is
in charge of a search operation, the report should be sent to the

Appendix I Searches involving a potential CBRN incident
In the event that members are involved in a CBRN search or a search which as a result of items found develops
into a CBRN incident, the following indicators are outlined below to assist in identifying the type of agent
involved and the appropriate steps which should then be taken.

Chemical agent
• Dead or dying animals
• Unexplained casualties e.g. multiple victims, serious illnesses, nausea, disorientation, difficulty
in breathing, convulsions, definite casualty patterns, no logical explanation such as a chemical tanker
accident etc
• Unusual liquid, spray or vapour e.g. droplets, oily film, unexplained odour, unexplained pools of liquid,
fog, cloud, or mist not related to weather
• Suspicious devices/packages possibly containing unexplained wiring or tubing, liquid containers,
aerosols, timers, batteries, and/or unexplained munitions.

Biological agent
• Unusual casualties e.g. unusual types of illnesses for region/area, definite pattern inconsistent with natural
disease, unexplained groupings of casualties, confirmation from Public Health agencies
• Unusual liquid, spray or vapour

Radiological/nuclear agents
• Suspicious devices/packages may contain these
• Nausea, vomiting, diarrhoea
• Fatigue
• Black ‘burn’ marks on skin.
Note: Symptoms for Biological/Radiological agents may not appear for days or even weeks after the incident.
If you suspect a CBRN incident (remember this may be a terrorist incident) the STEP 1-2-3 Protocol should be
followed, in the absence of any other information.
Step 1-2-3 Protocol - Safety triggers for First Responder Gardaí
If there is one casualty with no logical explanation or cause, make the normal response.
If there are two casualties with no logical explanation or cause, consider further investigation before
proceeding to scene.
If there are three or more casualties with no logical explanation or cause, do not proceed, but stand off,
obtain further information and reassess.

Searches involving ‘white powder’ incidents in a premises
• Close and isolate room where the item is and call for assistance
• Reassure recipient/handler/opener that they are unlikely to feel any immediate effects if at all and that
medical aid will be provided
• Allow potential casualties to wash their hands in diluted bleach (1 teaspoon of bleach per litre of water)
and rinse with soap and water
• Ascertain how many people have been exposed to the material (keep a detailed list)
• Possible casualties should be removed to a comfortable room as close to the isolated room as is
practicable to avoid further spread of potential contamination
• If either room is connected to an integrated/recycled air conditioning system, that room or local system
must be shut down. All air conditioning units have a Red Emergency Stop button located on the outer
casing - locate and activate it
Once the room has been physically and technically isolated there is no requirement for further evacuation.
If outside, retreat to 50 metres from device. Advise people to stay indoors, close windows and doors and
await assistance. Do not ask for other units to attend the scene but suggest an RVP upwind, 400 metres
away.

Symptoms of nerve agent poisoning
Early Symptoms
• Pin pointing of pupils, dimming of vision
• Running nose
• Increased saliva
• Tightness of chest, difficulty in breathing.
Later Symptoms
• Headache
• Increasing saliva, drooling at mouth
• Dizziness and general weakness
• Excessive sweating.
Danger symptoms
• Nausea and vomiting
• Involuntary urination and defecation
• Muscles twitching and jerking
• Cessation of breathing.

Symptoms of blister agent poisoning

Symptoms of blood agent poisoning
Low Concentrations

High Concentrations
