Appendix III Health and Safety
Safety when carrying out searches
Members should exercise due care and vigilance in the conduct of searches both for their own safety and the
safety of all other persons who may be affected by their acts and/or omissions. District Officers in co-operation
with local management will compile a risk assessment prior to carrying out a search and this will form part of
the search briefing documents.
The following safety controls will be adhered to in the risk assessment:

Search of buildings
• Adequate briefings will be given to all personnel in relation to the investigation prior to arriving
at the building to be searched
• Consider information on and reconnaissance of building to be searched, refer to previous search
records if possible
• Consider the need for specialist units: ERU, Dog Unit, Divisional Search Team, EOD etc.
• If possible consult with owner/occupier to ascertain hazards/risks to members
• Search team to be provided with appropriate Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) based on
known hazards
• PPE to include suitable clothing, footwear, helmet etc., and appropriate equipment to carry out search
• Adequate training/instruction and information should be provided for personnel who have to use
specialised equipment during the search
• First aid kit to be with search party involved in search
• Use the Ampel Probe
• Records to be kept of personnel involved in search.
• Report any incidents and injuries received by personnel during search
• Comply with Garda Code Instructions
• Notify local Garda Station of search
• Ensure good supervision
• Maintain records
• Identify nearest or more appropriate hospital
• Consideration to be given to nominating one vehicle as emergency evacuation vehicle in the
event of extended distances from Hospital

Search of prisoners
• Sufficient personnel should be available to deal with prisoners
• Ensure Personal Protective Equipment (PPE) is available in all stations (e.g. gloves, overalls, masks,
protective glasses, ample probe etc.) and to search personnel
• The co-operation of prisoners should be sought prior to searching
• All dangerous items to be removed from prisoner as soon as possible after arrest
• Members should exercise caution where prisoner is suspected to have HIV, TB or other infectious diseases
• Use double glove where prisoner is suspected of abusing drugs or is infected with Hepatitis B, C or HIV
• Maintain good hygiene practices (washing hands, no eating, no smoking, safe disposal of gloves)
• Cover all breaks on exposed skin
• Under no circumstances should members place their hands into the mouths of prisoners in an effort to
retrieve drugs or other items they are trying to swallow

Chapter 6 Identification

6.1. Introduction
When it is established that a crime has been committed, the task of the investigating officer is to identify, or
have identified, the culprit with the crime.
The means of the identification can be:
• Natural
• Scientific
• Incidental

6.2. Natural Means of Identification
The most natural means of identification is the visual identification by a witness who saw the offender commit
the crime or who saw them at or near the crime scene at or about the time the offence was committed.
Courts, however, are more wary of the natural identification process than of many of those supported by
scientific interpretation. Such evidence is frequently challenged in Court due to the possibility of error. Indeed
the Judge is bound to warn of this possibility. Identification evidence supported by scientific methods is
always preferred.
In this Chapter, visual identification, identification through the medium of composite sketches, dental
identification and voice identification are considered; in later Chapters the other types of identification process
are discussed.

6.3. Scientific Means of Identification
Fingerprints, for example, (together with palm and soleprints) have been established as a positive means of
identification by the scientific process. They are unique in that they provide specific proof of the personal
presence of the culprit at the scene of the crime. No other means of identification serves this dual role except,
perhaps, bite marks (Odontology). Handwriting comparison enjoys a formidable measure of general
acceptance as a means of positive identification.
DNA profiling greatly increases the significance of blood and body fluid evidence both in its powers of
exclusion and inclusion. In the latter case, it may allow for association with an individual to a very high degree
of probability.
Ballistics (the identification of ammunition discharged from firearms) is likewise a positive identification process
as regards the ammunition of the firearm from which it was discharged.
Many other forms of trace evidence may only be corroborative or eliminatory in respect of the suspect or
culprit involved. For example, blood can be identified as belonging to a specific group and sub group, but not
necessarily to a specific individual, therefore its identification, while positive in itself, is only corroborative or
eliminatory according to circumstances.
The Courts have displayed moderate enthusiasm for new means of proof of identity. In the decision of Dunne
v DPP [2002] 2IR 305 (SC), Hardiman J. observed:

“If science or technology can provide certainty in matters of great importance which would
otherwise be determined or human testimony which may be fallible or worse, who but a guilty
man would not willingly invoke its aid? … The balance has long been struck in favour of the use
of technology in the search for the perpetrators of crime, even when the processes involved are
minimally invasive or transiently painful or undignified for innocent people. The greater good
prevails. This development is due in large measure to the development of techniques of
previously unimagined sophistication, from the telephoto lens and the video camera to the
extraordinary precision of DNA analysis.”
Footmarks, tyre-marks and tool-marks all serve to eliminate or corroborate, as the case may be, but depending
on the uniqueness of their impressions through wear, damage, and so on they can on many occasions be
identified positively.

6.4. Incidental Means of Identification
Many types of trace evidence – such as blood and other body fluids, hairs, fibres and so on which can be
termed both scientific and incidental, can provide the linkage whereby an identifiable substance can
corroborate an individual's identification.
There are other means of linkage whereby a culprit is made identifiable. Their modus operandi, possession of
the proceeds of a crime, the use of or possession of identifiable objects such as vehicles, coins or marked
objects; even injuries received in the commission of the crime, all serve as incidental means to make
identification possible, if not providing positive identification in themselves.

6.5. Visual (or eye witness) Identification
In cases of crime or of suspected crime it is frequently essential to identify a
person who has been seen at a particular place at a particular time. To do this
satisfactorily is not always easy. Evidence as to identity, based on personal
impressions is, perhaps of all classes of evidence, the least to be relied upon. In
fact, one of the major problems in the administration of justice is that posed by
evidence of visual identification.
The question of visual identification was considered by the Supreme Court in The
People v. Casey (No. 2) [1963] I.R. 22. The following extract from the judgment
delivered in that case indicates that approach of the Courts to the matter:
"We are of opinion that juries as a whole may not be fully aware of the dangers involved in visual
identification nor of the considerable number of cases in which such identification has been
proved to be erroneous; and also that they may be inclined to attribute too much probative effect
to the test of an identification parade.
In our opinion it is desirable that in all cases where the verdict depends substantially on the
correctness of an identification, their attention should be called in general terms to the fact that
in a number of instances such identification has proved erroneous, to the possibilities of mistake
in the case before them and to the necessity for caution. Nor do we think that such warning
should be confined to cases where the identification is that of only one witness. Experience has
shown that mistakes can occur where two or more witnesses have made positive identifications.
We consider juries in cases where the correctness of an identification is challenged should be
directed on the following lines, namely, that if their verdict as to the guilt of the prisoner is to
depend wholly or substantially on the correctness of such identification, they should bear in mind
that there have been a number of instances where responsible witnesses, whose honesty was not

The degree of reliance which should be placed on visual identification is dependent on the following:
(i) The witness: age, character, credibility, accuracy, alertness, powers of observation and memory.
(ii) Circumstances of case: length of time the witness had to observe what happened, the position they were
in, the distance they were from the person they claim to identify, the number of suspects they purport to
identify, the quality of light, the possible effect the crime had on their emotions, and the length of time
which has elapsed between the occurrence and the carrying out of the identification procedure.
When seeking evidence of identification it is essential to be cautious, to ask many questions, and to explore
the details of when, where and how, and in what circumstances, the witness saw the person they are prepared
to identify. Every statement should be tested thoroughly.
Because of the unreliable nature of visual identification, it is vitally important that all available scientific aids
and methods be utilised to the fullest possible extent and that every avenue of enquiry suggested by basic
leads be followed up with a view to seeking evidence that will corroborate an identification by an eye witness.

Visual identification is substantially stronger where a person recognises an
individual who is familiar to them from past meetings, associations and so on.
See note at People (DPP) v Behan at 6.17 1st February 1992 CCA.

6.6. Improper Methods of Visual Identification
The following methods of visual identification should not be adopted:
• Direct confrontation: that is showing a suspect alone to a witness or otherwise pointing out the suspect
to a witness and asking the witness if they can identify them. This method was used in The People v.
Martin [1954] I.R. 22 in respect of two different accused persons.
• Identification on Court appearance: that is when a suspect is actually appearing before the Court and a
witness is asked for the first time to identify the accused as the person responsible.

6.7. Correct Visual Identification Procedures
The visual identification of suspects may be established as follows:
• By the use of photographs
• By the use of videotape
• By identification parade (formal or informal)
• By the use of composite sketches.

6.8. Identification by Photographs
When a crime has been committed by a person or persons unknown, and the description of its perpetrators as
given by one or more eye witnesses is of little or no value towards establishing identification, photographs in
the possession of the Gardaí may provide the only hope for a break in the investigation.
The danger posed is that the witness is being presented with a greatly reduced number of suspects and a
witness may be unwilling afterwards to concede the extent to which they were reliant on the photographs to
recollect the offender. There may also be a tendency for a witness who has been shown photographs “to
substitute a photographic image once seen for a hazy recollection of the person initially observed” (Alexander
v R (1981) 145 C.L.R. 395 at 426). Subsequently the witness’s recollection of the culprit and their recollection
of the photograph may become merged so that the witness cannot separate them leading to the possibility of
a wrong identification.
The Court of Criminal Appeal in The People v. Mills [1956] I.R. 106 acknowledged that it is permissible for a
Garda who has received a description of a person suspected of a particular offence to show photographs to
the person giving the description for the purpose of confirming such description. Where photographs are used
for this purpose, numerous photographs of possible suspects and not merely one or two should be shown to
the witness . A detailed description should first be obtained from the witness as a means of recording the
parameters of the witness’s memory prior to the inspection of the photographs The Court of Criminal Appeal
in People (DPP) v Rapple [1999] 1 ILRM 113 affirmed Lord Hewart’s statement in R. v Melaney (1923) 18 Cr.
App. R. 2 at 3 (CA) of the use of photographs only when “the person has not been arrested and the
police are in the greatest doubt who is the culprit”.
Photographic identification procedure then may be used only as a preliminary step in a situation where the
identity of a culprit is unknown. Photographs should be used only where the following conditions exist:
• The suspect has not been arrested
• There are no prospects of effecting an early arrest
• The identity of the suspect cannot otherwise be established
The Court of Criminal in Rapple cited above at 117-118 distinguished between two situations in which
photographs may be shown to a witness. The first is where the Gardaí, having taken a description from a
witness, show the witness a series of photographs in an attempt to generate a suspect (‘suspect generation’).
The second is where the Gardaí have a suspect in mind and wish to confirm that they are the perpetrator seen
by the witness (‘suspect confirmation’).

Suspect Generation
So far as the use of photographs for the purpose of suspect generation is concerned, the Court in Rapple
accepted that this is a necessary investigative technique which is justified by reference to the exigencies of
criminal investigation and is, therefore, permissible subject to the observation of certain safeguards.
The first is that the Gardaí should behave with what is described as ‘exemplary fairness’ when showing
photographs to a witness so as to minimise any prejudice to the accused. Any element of suggestiveness
should be avoided.
The second is that a witness who identifies a suspect from photographs should also identify them ‘in the flesh’
by means of an identification parade or, where this is not practicable, by some other informal identification
procedure.
There is a further complicating factor from the point of view of the defence in challenging identification

evidence where Garda photographs were shown to a witness as this could clearly prejudice the accused in the
minds of the jury if it is revealed that the Gardai were in possession of photographs of the accused. There is a
risk when photographs are shown to a witness that the trial judge may rule the identification evidence
inadmissible if there is any doubt that the procedures adopted were sufficiently reliable to safely ground a
conviction. In general, the test applied is whether in all the circumstances of the case, the prejudicial
effect outweighs the probative value of the evidence.
In Rapple cited above, Barron J. approved the principle laid down in R. v Dwyer [1925] 2 K.B. 799 at 803 that
“it is the duty of the police to behave with exemplary fairness, remembering always that the Crown
has no interest in securing a conviction, but it has an interest only in securing the conviction of the
right person”.

Suspect Confirmation
With regard to the use of photographs for the purpose of suspect confirmation, this procedure was criticised
by the Court in Rapple as ‘clearly prejudicial and unfair’. Therefore, if photographs are used for this
objective in advance of a formal identification parade or informal identification procedure, this is very likely to
lead to the exclusion of the identification evidence of that witness.
In People (DPP) v Brazil Unreported, Court of Criminal Appeal, 22nd. March, 2002, Keane CJ stated that it
would be unsafe for a jury to consider the identification evidence of a witness who had been shown a
photograph of the accused before an identification parade as such evidence would be ‘tainted and rendered
almost valueless’. A similar decision was reached by the People [DPP] v Meleady and Grogan [1995] 2
I.R.517; [1999] 2 I.R.249
Photographic evidence: A photograph taken during the commission of a crime may be produced in Court as
evidence that the crime was committed and of the identity of the offender.
In R. v. Dodson (1984) 79 Cr. App. R. 220, photographs taken automatically by a security camera during an
attempted armed robbery were held to be admissible to show the identity of the accused. In that case, the
English Court of Appeal stated that in deciding the question of identity, the jury should receive guidance from
the trial judge on the following factors:
• The quality of the photographs
• The extent of the exposure of the facial features of the person photographed
• Evidence, or the absence of it, of a change in the appearance of the accused
• The opportunity the jury has to look at the accused in the dock and over what
period of time.

6.9. Rules for Operating Photographic Identification Procedures
Subject to the above, the following rules should be observed when attempting to establish identity by the use
of photographs:
• A single photograph should never be shown to a witness
• At least twelve photographs should be shown to the witness
• The photographs used should bear as close a resemblance as possible to the photograph of the suspects
• Photographs should not bear names, numbers, dates or identifying marks

to influence the judgement of any witness
• Where there are a number of witnesses and one makes a positive identification of a suspect, other

parade or obtain identification informally
• When a suspect is put up for identification through parade or otherwise, all witnesses (including any

Following identification using photographs the following rules apply:
• Where photographs have been shown to a witness and where evidence of identification is in issue, the

• Witnesses (including members of the Gardaí) should not disclose the use of photographs in their direct

• Any photographs used should be available for production in Court, if required, and the member who

manner in which the procedure was carried out, i.e. by keeping a record of the procedure.

6.10. Publication of Photograph of Suspect on TV or in
Newspapers
The publication of the photograph of a suspect on television or in newspapers before the carrying out of the
appropriate identification procedure should be avoided unless there is a very good reason for doing so.

6.11. Identification by Videotape
Videotape evidence is as capable of admission as a photograph. (Barber and Sons v. Lloyd's Underwriters
[1986] 2 All E.R. 845) It may be admitted to show both the commission of a crime and the identity of the
offender. Before such evidence is admitted, the authenticity of the videotape must be proved: R. v. Robson and
Harris [1972] 2 All E.R. 699; where the original is not available a copy may be admitted: Kajala v. Noble (1982)
75 Cr. App. R. 149.
Video evidence of crimes in progress will not only assist witnesses to identify the suspect(s) but also enable the
jury to determine the facts and observe the roles played by each of the participants in the crime. A videotape
produced as evidence in Court becomes an exhibit in the case.
In People (DPP) v Maguire [1995] 2 IR 286 , the Court of Criminal Appeal considered the admission and use of
video evidence for the purposes of identification in circumstances where the applicant had been convicted of
robbery on the basis of video footage of the robbery and still photographs taken from the film without any
other evidence of identification. Barron J., delivering the judgment of the Court, distinguished three
situations in which video evidence or stills taken from video footage may be put before a jury.
First, such evidence could be used to augment the evidence of a witness at the scene of the crime in order to
show whether or not the witnesses had a reasonable chance of making the particular identification or to

explain why they were unable to make an identification. Where evidence was used for this purpose, the role
of the jury was not to decide the issue of identification based on the video evidence but rather to decide
whether the identification evidence of the witnesses was credible.
Secondly, the video tape or stills taken from it could be
used by persons who were not at the scene but who
knew the accused in order to identify them as the
perpetrator of the crime. In order to lay the foundation
for the admission of such an identification, it is
necessary for the witnesses to indicate the capacity in
which and how well they know the accused and they
should also indicate any feature which, in their view,
identifies the accused with the person in the still image.
Once more, the video evidence is not admitted in this

themselves from it but rather to enable them to assess
the credibility of the witnesses making the
identification.
The Court acknowledged that identification evidence of this type may cause difficulties where the witness
making the identification from the video is a police officer or other person in authority because of the danger
of prejudice to the accused arising from the occupation of the witness. Barron J stated that such evidence is
admissible but the accused must be protected from the prejudice which may arise and thus the evidence
should be excluded if, in all the circumstances of the case, its prejudicial effect would outweigh its
probative value.
Thirdly, although it is unsatisfactory to do so, video evidence can be left to the jury to form its own view on the
question of identity in circumstances where no witnesses who are acquainted with the accused can be found
who can or are prepared to come to Court to identify him or her.
In reaching that conclusion the Court followed R v Dodson, [1984] 1 WLR 971 where the English Court of
Appeal had rejected the argument that identification by the jury could not be allowed because in carrying out
this function they were acting as experts. The Court took the view that no special training or expertise was
required to make an identification and that the jury “were called upon to do no more than the average
person in domestic, social and other situations does from time to time, namely to say whether he is
sure that a person shown in a photograph is the person he is then looking at or whom he has
seen recently”.
The Court emphasised that, whenever video evidence is admitted, the Casey (People [AG]-v- Casey no.2 1963
IR33) direction must be given and they should be instructed that, before they can convict, they must be
satisfied beyond a reasonable doubt that the accused is the person shown on the film.

6.12. Formal/Informal Identification
The Courts have criticised recourse to informal identification procedures in cases where an identification
parade could have been conducted and also to the practice of asking witnesses to identify the accused as he
enters or leaves a Courthouse building.
Identification parades have not been legislated for by statute or regulation in Ireland; indeed, to a significant
extent in Ireland, procedures for obtaining identification evidence have been distilled in response to precedents
and decisions of the Courts.

Identification evidence is evidence given by the victim or a witness to an event or incident that identifies the
accused as the person who committed the crime. Identification evidence is considered to be doubtful
evidence because honest people often make mistakes and identify the wrong person. As a consequence,
safeguards have been put in place to protect the accused. The most important safeguard is the warning given
by the judge to the jury at the end of the trial about all types of identification evidence.
The Irish Supreme Court recognised in People (Attorney General) v Casey (No.2) [1963] IR 33 (SC) that owing
to the special nature of identification evidence, juries ought to be warned at the close of the trial that positive
identifications have in the past proved erroneous, even where made by more than one witness, and that for
reasons general to identification evidence and specific to the circumstances of each case, special caution must
be exercised when deciding whether or not to accept a witness’s identification.
Kingsmill Moore J. referred throughout to the need for a strong direction in any case where the identification
is challenged and the prosecution’s case against the accused depends “wholly or substantially on the
correctness of such identification”.
The Court of Criminal Appeal in The People (Attorney General) v O’Driscoll 1 Frewen 351, 3 March 1972,
applying the ‘Casey warning’ stated that the whole point of the warning at the close of a trial, is that errors
in identification can be made even when the testimony as to the identification is given by several persons in
the same case to the same effect. The Irish Courts have applied and continue to apply the Casey ruling strictly.

6.13. Nature of Identification Parade
As stated earlier there is neither legislation nor formal regulations covering the precise nature of an
identification parade. We rely on court decisions for this. In the case of The People (DPP) v O’Reilly [1990] 2 IR
415 (CCA) and in the case of The People (DPP) v James O’Hanlon (CCA 12 October 1998) formal identification
parades were held to involve the assembly of eight or nine people of similar age, height, appearance, dress
and walk of life as the suspect.
It was further held that the parade should be supervised
by an independent Garda (that is, one not concerned
with the actual investigation); that full details would be
kept of the description of the various people making up
the parade and that the witness should not have any
opportunity of seeing the suspect in advance of the
holding of the parade.
The Court of Criminal Appeal acknowledged that this is
not intended to be an exhaustive list for such parades.
The Court further accepted that the people chosen to
form the parade should be physically and dressed
similar to the suspect – so far as is reasonably possible.
In the case of The People (DPP) v O’Hanlon (CCA, October 12, 1998) it was held that it is sufficient that the
volunteers look “roughly similar” to the suspect in which case a parade was not defective merely because its
participants were a few inches shorter or taller than the suspect. Where the witness fails to identify the
accused, this fact should be disclosed by the prosecution to the defence.
The single issue of the trial in DPP v O’Reilly was whether the identification was adequate. In this case the
witness was brought by the Gardaí to an area at or near the Courthouse so that she might observe people in
the street and ascertain if she could identify anyone as having been in her house on the day of the crime.
After sometime a group of five people appeared and the injured party said of one of them “I think that’s him
there” and “I didn’t get a good look at him”. Sometime later the people emerged from the Courthouse
and the witness identified the same person positively.

The upshot of the evidence of the Gardaí was that they were not in a position to give any description of what
the other people passing up and down on the street on that day looked like. Details of the number of people
present, their ages, descriptions, their height and so forth were needed in respect of the informal identification
that was attempted in the circumstance of this case.
The reason given for not holding a formal identification parade was that it might be ‘more beneficial’ to the
defendant not to hold one. The Court said that this explanation was less than satisfactory.
The Court was clearly of the opinion that this was a case that required the holding of a formal identification
parade and the Court will express disapproval of any suggestion that the holding of formal identification
parades has out-lived its usefulness; they are important in that they provide a filter for both prosecution and
defence.
If a suspect is not picked out at an identification parade, then very often the prosecution may go no further.
Conversely, if a suspect is identified he may well think that the time has arrived to reach an accommodation
with the prosecution by pleading guilty to the alleged offence.
The Court was also mindful that an important difference between a formal identification parade and an
informal identification is that in the former the accused (and their legal adviser if one is present) has full
knowledge about the composition of the parade and may object if it is perceived to be unfair. Furthermore,
the Court will have the benefit of a detailed account of the parade and a description of those who participated
in it.
By contrast, an accused has no input where there is an informal identification and is unlikely even to have
knowledge of its happening. Therefore, they may be seriously inhibited in challenging its fairness at the trial.
The Court recognised that there will be circumstances where it is not possible or practicable to hold an
identification parade. If, for example, the suspect is of singular appearance it may not be possible to get
together sufficient people to make up a fair parade. The holding of an identification parade would probably
be a redundant exercise if the witness knew the suspect previously. In The People (DPP) v Behan (CCA, Ex
tempore, February 1, 1993) the Court recognised that a formal identification parade is not required where the
witness may already know the accused.
The Courts are not concerned with a situation where a suspect will not agree to go on an identification parade
or attempts to frustrate it once it is assembled. If that is the course a suspect takes, then that is the particular
option that they have exercised and they may have to live with the consequences. However, the situation in
the case of D.P.P. v O’Reilly is that the accused man was given no such option at all.

6.14. Constitutional Right to Fair Procedures
In the case of The People (DPP) v Maples [1999] ILRM 113 (SC) O’Flaherty J held that the right to be identified
formally in a properly constituted parade is an important element of the fundamental constitutional right to
fair procedures. The Courts have, however, resisted the claim that the need to identify the accused formally is
a distinct constitutional right of the accused. O’Flaherty J also held that prior to identification, the witness
should not see the accused being arrested, where this can reasonably be avoided, or being taken from custody
to the parade room.

6.15. Delay in Identification
Where there is a delay from the witness’s first observation of the wrongdoer to formal identification the
weight subsequently to be given to the identification will be affected and if serious enough may affect its

identification thus becomes potentially and actually unreliable. Guidelines in the appropriate use of CD Fit

6.16. Dock Identification
As a general rule, save for cases where the accused is known to the witness, identification of the accused must
be made by a witness prior to the trial. Common law does not favour formal identification for the first time in
Court (dock identification), since by this stage in the proceedings the witness may feel under pressure to make
a positive identification.
In The People (DPP) v Cooney [1998] 1 ILRM 321 (SC) the Supreme Court held that Irish law has for a long
time recognised that so called ‘dock identifications’ are undesirable and unsatisfactory, for the reason that the
identification of the accused for the first time when he or she is sitting in the place normally reserved for the
accused and usually flanked by prison officers is of limited probative value.
For that reason, in cases where the identification of the accused is likely to be at issue, the appropriate
procedure is to hold an identification parade or, if that is impracticable, to afford the identification witness
some other opportunity of identifying the accused. Of course, it may not be possible to hold an identification
parade, or any other form of identification procedure, because of the refusal of the suspect to co-operate.
In the course of The People (DPP) v Lynda Lee (CCA 20 July 2004) the Court acknowledged that the law
relating to the frailty and undesirability of so-called ‘dock identification’ applies to the circumstances where a
witness, who, prior to the events giving rise to the prosecution had no knowledge or acquaintance of an
accused, identifies them for the first time by pointing them out in the Courtroom at the trial.
The reasons why such an identification may be considered unsatisfactory are self evident since a witness may
be inclined to make assumptions concerning the identity of the person who is sitting in a dock with a warder
on either side. Hence the term ‘dock identification’.

6.17. Informal Identification
Informal identification evidence may be admitted as evidence but it depends on the circumstances of the case.
In the case of The People (DPP) v McDermott (C.C.A. 17 December, 1990) the evidence against the accused of
a serious offence was wholly that of visual identification of one witness. Five days after the incident occurred
the witness was brought by the Gardaí to a labour exchange where, after approximately one hundred people
had entered and exited from the exchange, he identified the accused to the Gardaí.
The Court held that an identification parade is not the only method of identification, nor is it mandatory in
every case, and that not only is a visual identification of that kind admissible in evidence, but it may in a
particular case lead to a conviction, even without corroborative evidence.
But the Court was also satisfied that there must, in a case where no identification parade is held, be an
additional warning to the jury in relation to the difference in opportunity, control and credibility between an
informal identification and an ordinary controlled identification parade.
A formal parade is mainly a matter of procedural fairness to the accused. However, it depends on their cooperation. An informal identification may be necessary where the suspect attempts to frustrate the formal
parade once it has been assembled or refuses to take part in it.

In this instance the Gardai are entitled to obtain identification by less formal means. In the case of The People
(Attorney General) v Martin [1956] IR 22 the suspect was identified from a makeshift identification parade that
was sprung on the suspect while in custody in the Bridewell. The Supreme Court held that there is no rule of
law or practice that requires visual identification of a person to be proved by means of an identification
parade; each case must be considered on its own facts.
It is readily understandable that other types of identification may in certain circumstances be fairer and more
dependable than a formal identification parade which, because of its surroundings, atmosphere, range of
choice and limited opportunity for observation, may be a less than satisfactory means of achieving a reliable
identification.
But the acceptability of an alternative method must always depend on the circumstances of the case.
In the case of The People (DPP) v Rapple [1999] 7 ILRM 113 (CCA) the witness was brought in a Garda car to
see whether he could identify the suspect whom it was expected would be walking along a particular road,
and the victim identified the appellant as his attacker. The appellant was subsequently convicted. One of
the grounds of appeal was that the evidence of the actual identification of the suspect on the public road
was insufficient.
The Court held in this case that the suspect was not prepared to go into an identification parade and the
Gardaí accordingly had to find some other means by which the witness could identify him so as to give
evidence to that effect in Court. Such a course of action is permissible once what was done by the Gardaí was
reasonable and fair in the circumstances.
What the Gardaí did in this case was found to be reasonable and fair. The suspect had refused to take part in
an identification parade and therefore he could not expect the usual procedures in Court to be altered and for
him to thereby gain some benefit.
In the case of The People (DPP) v Behan (1 February 1992 CCA) the injured party recognised the suspect at the
time of the incident. Some days later she was brought to the Children’s Court in Smithfield and in the
precincts of the Court she picked out the suspect from a number of people who simply happened to be there
at the time.
It was argued that the evidence of the informal identification should not have been admitted at all. The Court
stated that this was a recognition case rather than an identification case. The Court held that the trial judge
had given a very strong warning to the jury in accordance with the Casey criteria. The visual identification
evidence was admitted.
The Court did, however, emphasise that if the parties had not known each other previously the Court would
reiterate again that insofar as is practicable a proper identification parade is the ideal thing and should be put
in place whenever possible.
It is necessary where suspect identification is an issue that a suspect must first be afforded the opportunity to
stand in a formal parade before considering informal identification.

6.18. Criticisms for Recourse to Informal Identification
The Courts have criticised recourse by the Gardaí to informal procedures in cases where an identification
parade could have been conducted, and have condemned the Garda practice of asking witnesses to identify
the accused as they enter or leave a Courthouse building; surroundings that earmark the accused in advance
of identification as a criminal type.
In the case of The People (Attorney-General) v Fagan (CCA, May 13, 1974) the witness was brought to the

primary issue at the trial was whether the identification of the applicant was adequate.
The reason given for not holding a formal identification parade was that the suspect did not live at home and
was “not always that readily available”. The Court stated that this explanation for not holding a formal
identification parade must be accounted less than satisfactory.
In the case of The People (DPP) v Duff (CCA May 10 1993) a witness was requested to sit in an unmarked
Garda car located outside a Courthouse. When a person approached the Courthouse the witness was unsure
whether he was the suspect. Subsequently, when the same person emerged from the Courthouse the witness
told the Garda that she was sure it was him.
The Court held that the proper, regular and optimum method of holding an identification for a witness who
believed they could visually identify an accused person was an identification parade. If the informal method of
identification had been followed by a formal method of identification, then the evidence of identification
might have been sufficient to have been left to the jury. The reason offered by the Garda for the failure to hold
a formal identification parade, which was that the witnesses was reluctant to come into personal contact with
suspects, did not alter the unsatisfactory nature of the evidence emanating from the informal identification.
In the case of The People (DPP) v O’Reilly [1990] 2 IR 415 (CCA) the application given for not holding a formal
identification parade was that it might be ‘more beneficial’ to the defendant. The Court held that this reason
must be accounted less than satisfactory.
In the case of The People (DPP) v Carroll (CCA ex tempore December 18, 2002), the Court of Criminal Appeal
was unimpressed by the Garda’s explanation that it would have been difficult to arrange a parade of scruffy
males in their 40s or 50s and it quashed convictions in a case where informal identification was the only
evidence against the accused.
The Garda had not explained why it would have been difficult to arrange a parade, such as that the accused
was of singular appearance, and appeared not to have made any attempt to arrange a formal parade before
recourse to informal means. In this case the accused was identified from a crowd by night.
In the case of The People (DPP) v Lynda Lee (CCA, July 20, 2004) the appellant was identified informally in the
District Court in the absence of any justification or explanation for not holding a formal parade. The other
women in the Courtroom were of all ages ranging from 16 years to 50 years and of different hair colouring.
The suspect may also have been the only blonde woman sitting in an isolated position near a door.
It was held that these frailties in the informal identification were serious. Furthermore, this was a case in which
the appellant was identified as a suspect very soon after the offence had been committed. There was no
reason given by way of explanation or justification by the prosecution as to why an identification parade was
not arranged or that it would have been difficult or impractical to do so.
The Court held that it was clear from the judgments in the Fagan and O’Reilly cases that because of certain
inherent dangers in relying exclusively or predominantly on evidence of visual identification there is an onus
on the investigating Gardaí and the prosecution to ensure that evidence of visual identification is obtained in
the most reasonably reliable form which, in the absence of reasonable cause, should be in the form of a
formal identification.
It was therefore held that that this was a classic case in which a formal identification parade should have been
put in place.
In The People (DPP) v. O’Reilly [1991] 2 IR 415 the Court held that even where a person has been identified
informally, they should be given the option of submitting to a formal identification parade and it was a matter
for them to exercise that option or not. O’Flaherty J. acknowledged that a suspect who is identified informally

were unlikely to have had knowledge of its happening.
This has been consistently highlighted by the Courts. However, the Court of Criminal Appeal held this is
not the case where a suspect is identified informally without the assistance of any other person including
the Gardaí.
In the case of The People (DPP) v George Christo (CCA
31 January 2005) the suspect assaulted the injured
party as she walked home from work. Five days later
while she was outside the door of a premises she saw
two black males walking along the street. She gave
evidence that she recognised one of them as being the
man who had assaulted her. The injured party identified
the suspect without assistance from any other person
including the Gardaí, unlike the identifying witness in
DPP v O’Reilly.
The Court accepted that an identification parade in the
present case would serve only to reinforce the
identification already made by the injured party in the
street and that this would, in fact, be prejudicial to
the suspect.
In these circumstances the Court held that an identification parade was not required, and would have added
nothing to the quality of the identification evidence already available to the Court.
It is clear from the judgments above that because of certain inherent dangers in relying exclusively or
predominantly on evidence of visual identification there is an onus on the investigating Gardaí and the
prosecution to ensure that evidence of visual identification is obtained in the most reasonably reliable
form which, in the absence of reasonable cause (i.e. refusal/frustration), should be in the form of a
formal identification.
When arrangements are made for the holding of an identification parade, every precaution should be taken to:
• Exclude any suspicion of unfairness or the risk of erroneous identifications
• Make sure that the witnesses' ability to recognise the suspect has been fairly and adequately tested.
The chief advantage of conducting a formal parade is that identification may be made under controlled
circumstances in the presence of the accused or the accused and their solicitor who may later inform the Court
of any inadequacies in the parade or the procedures employed.
Having regard to precedent and the suspect’s constitutional right to fair procedures it is imperative that when
carrying out an informal identification that there is no absence of reasonable cause to hold a formal
identification parade.

6.19. Conduct of Formal Identification Parade
The Director of Public Prosecutions has agreed that a witness may identify a suspect by means of numbers
rather than by physical touching. A number should be assigned ( in ascending sequential order - 1, 2, 3, 4 etc.)
to each individual volunteer and suspect after the identification parade has formed i.e. after the suspect has

taken their place in the line-up. To avoid any possible confusion between the numbers six and nine these
numbers should be underlined.
Where a suspect wishes to change their position in the line-up subsequent to numbers being assigned, the
numbers must be reassigned accordingly to preserve the ascending sequential order of the numbers.
In conducting a formal identification parade, members should be conscious of the need to ensure that
procedures are fair and seen to be fair. It should be borne in mind that the object of the exercise is to
provide the witness with an opportunity to view the parade and make an independent evaluation
and /or identification.
The following rules should be observed:
6.19.1. Organising the Parade:
• A member of the Gardaí other than the member in charge of the investigation should conduct the
identification parade
• The member in charge of the investigation may be present at the parade but should take no active part
• The conducting member should have the names, addresses and descriptions of the persons forming the
parade and the names of the witnesses and be familiar with the facts of the case
• The suspect should be placed among at least eight volunteers who are, as far as possible, roughly of the
same (a) age, (b) height, (c) general appearance and, (d) dress of the suspect
• Volunteers taking part in the parade must not be known to any of the witnesses and witnesses should not
be given an opportunity of seeing the volunteers, their descriptions or photographs, before the parade
• All unauthorised persons, including members of the Gardaí, should be excluded from the parade or the
area in which the witnesses are gathered
• The parade should be formed in line and those present should remain in front of the parade. A number
should be assigned to each participant
• If the parade is held indoors, a well lighted room is essential to ensure the witness has the best view
possible to prevent the risk of an erroneous identification. In a situation where a witness has seen the
person outdoors in daylight on the occasion of the crime, it would be desirable to hold the parade
outdoors, if possible
• Where there are two suspects of roughly similar appearance, they may be paraded together with at least
twelve other persons. If the suspects are not similar in appearance, or if there are more than two suspects,
separate parades should be held using different volunteers on each parade
• Where all members of a particular group are possible suspects there is no objection to having a parade
made up entirely of suspects. This would only apply where there are not more than two culprits sought
and all the suspects are of roughly similar appearance. Two suspects of obviously dissimilar appearance
should not be included in the same parade
16.19.2. Suspect’s Rights:
• The conducting member should see the suspect before the parade, explain the arrangements to them and
ask if they have any objection. If the suspect objects to participating in the parade it should not be held.
The suspect should be asked if they object to any of the volunteers forming the parade. They should be
asked to give reasons for the objections and any reasonable objection should be met
• The conducting member should inform the suspect that they may have a solicitor or a friend present for
the duration of the parade; the person whom the suspect nominates may be in the presence of the witness
unless there are particular circumstances which dictate otherwise, such as the intimidation of the witness
or otherwise
• No witness should be given an opportunity of seeing the suspect before they are paraded or of seeing a
description or photograph of the suspect before the parade
• The suspect should be told that they may select their own position in the parade and that they may change
their position after each witness has left the parade

• Once the identification parade has been formed, everything thereafter in respect of it should take place in
the presence and hearing of the suspect and/or the presence and hearing of the solicitor or other person
nominated by the suspect
• In the absence of a solicitor or in the event of failure or refusal by the suspect to nominate an independent
person, the services of a peace commissioner should be sought to act as an independent scrutineer
• When all the witnesses have been called the suspect should be asked whether they have any comments to
make concerning the manner in which the parade has been conducted, these comments should be noted
6.19.3. Witnesses:
• The witnesses should be isolated in a room out of sight and hearing of the room or place where the parade
is being held. A member should be available to ensure that there will be no communication between a
witness who has viewed the parade and the witnesses who have yet to view the parade
• The conducting member should address each witness in the presence and hearing of the suspect, the
person nominated by them or the independent scrutineer, on the following lines: “This is an
identification parade. I want you to look very carefully at this line of persons and see if you can
recognise the person you have come to identify (or the person who assaulted you, or the person
you saw at —————————— (place) at ——————— (time) on —————————— (date),
etc.). Do not say anything until I ask you a question"
• Any reasonable request made by a witness should be granted, e.g. see parade walk; remove headgear;
hear them speak; see the parade from the rear. In the last mentioned case all present should remain in
front of the parade
• When the witness has scrutinised the parade the conducting member should ask them if they have
identified any person
6.19.4. Identification:
• If the witness indicates that they have made an identification, the conducting member should ask them to
clearly indicate the person concerned
• The witness need not touch the suspect/person identified
• A clear identification will be made where the witness leaves no doubt as to the person who has been
picked. This may be done in any manner including pointing to or stating the position or the number
assigned to the person in the line-up
• When a witness has made an identification, the person identified (if they are the suspect) should be
cautioned and their reply, if any, noted.
• It should be noted how the identification was actually carried out by each witness

6.20. Some Uncommon Cases
• Suspect with one eye: Volunteers and suspect should wear a similar type bandage or eye shield.
• Suspect with one leg: Have volunteers and suspect stand or sit behind tables draped in front with table
cloth or other suitable material.
• Suspect with one arm: Volunteers to slip corresponding arm from jacket or overcoat and button over.

6.22. Conduct of an Informal Identification Procedure
Prior to adopting this procedure, members should carefully consider the issues discussed at 6.12, 6.13, 6.14,
6.17 and 6.18 above with particular regard to the suspect’s constitutional right to fair procedures in the
identification process. Recourse to informal identification procedures will not be permissible by the courts as a
matter of course and an objectively reasonable justification for not holding a formal a parade will be required.
The circumstances which will justify not holding a parade will include cases where the suspect is known to the
witness or is of “singular appearance”, where it is not possible to get together sufficient people to make up
a fair parade or where the suspect refuses to participate in or frustrates a parade (People [DPP] v Reilly [1990] 2
I.R. 415). In the latter case, best practice is that the suspect should be advised that the Gardai will resort to
other methods of identification in response to the suspect’s attitude to the formal parade.
In the event of recourse being made to the informal procedure, the following points should be observed when
an identification by this means is attempted:
• The witness should be brought to a suitable place for making observation. This can be a ‘fairly busy’ street,
football ground, church, dancehall, market, cinema, labour exchange, entering or leaving a factory and so
on, provided in each case that a reasonable number of people are congregated there and the suspect is
allowed to mingle freely with them. Places such as courts where the accused will appear on a criminal
charge should be avoided, if possible.
• No other informal procedure such as showing the witness photographs of the suspect, indicating the
possible presence of the suspect or doing anything to influence the witness who is attempting to make an
identification should be engaged in by any member in advance of the arrangements put in place. Any such
action by members will attract severe criticism from the trial court (People (A.G.) v Fagan (1974) 1 Frewen
375)
• The conducting member should explain to the witness what is required of them, that they are to watch out
carefully and see if they can recognise the person who assaulted them or whom they saw at —————
(place) at ————— (time) on ————— (date), as the case may be
• It should be arranged that if the witness makes an identification they should either give a signal or place
their hand on the person they identify
• There should be no communication, apart from giving the pre arranged signal mentioned above, between
the conducting member and the witness once observation has commenced
• The witness should be left on their own, except in the case of a child when a parent or guardian should, if
possible, be present
• The conducting member should remain in the vicinity in sight of the witness but out of speaking distance
with him
• The conducting member should record all details in relation to the matter, and be prepared to prove same,
including the approximate number of persons of same sex as the identified person in the vicinity at the
time and as much detail as possible on the surrounding circumstances including weather and light
conditions. See further at Chapter 2 on factors which can cause inaccuracy in observation in this context.
Under this heading may be included the situation where a member is approached on the street or elsewhere
by a witness (or victim) of a crime and a tour is made of nearby areas with the witness, and the witness is
asked to point out the suspect. However, the courts have on occasion displayed a reluctance to accept such an
identification when it would have been feasible to conduct a formal identification parade. Where this course is
adopted, the suspect should not be placed in the Garda patrol car with the witness if the event of an arrest

Again, the conducting member should record as much detail as possible on the informal procedure carried

If possible, best practice is that an informal identification procedure should be photographed or

6.23. Cases where a Formal or Informal Identification Parade are
not Feasible
Where it is not feasible to have an informal identification or a formal identification parade (for example, where
the suspect frustrates the holding of a formal identification parade) the suspect may be placed in a room with
at least eight other persons of similar build and appearance. The witnesses should be invited one at a time to
view those present and asked if they could recognise the person responsible. This particular method of
identification should only be adopted as a last resort.

6.24. Identification through the Medium of CD Fit Composites
The task of identifying suspects, criminals and
missing persons is beset with various difficulties
because of the fact that no physical description of a
person's face or body, however exact, can help in
either catching them or identifying them beyond any
shadow of doubt.
A description of physical characteristics such as
height, build, complexion, nose, ears, mouth, eyes,
hair, scars, clothing, or habit peculiarities – while
important in themselves – cannot be considered as
absolutely successful and conclusive for the purpose
of identification.
The difficulty facing an investigator is not just eliciting the best available description from a witness, but in
transmitting that description so that a recipient could recognise the person from the details given.
See further at Chapter 7.4 under CD Fit Composite Unit and Chapter 2 on Observation and description.
The rules applying to the showing of photographs to witnesses, in an endeavour to establish the identify of a
suspect, operate in the case of composite sketches. Should the likeness lead to the arrest of a suspect, then a
full scale identification parade should be arranged and the witnesses given the chance of identifying the
suspect in person.
Where several witnesses may be able to describe the suspect, CD composites should be built from the
minimum number necessary to arrive at a likeness. The value of the other witnesses' evidence, should they
identify the suspect later at a parade, will thus be much greater.

6.25. Dental Identification
The human dentition (the particular form, number and arrangement of teeth) consists of 20 deciduous, or milk
teeth, and 32 permanent teeth, each one having its own characteristic shape and size. This uniqueness may be
further added to by extractions and/or fillings in individual cases. Only when a dentition is severely mutilated,
or no more than fragments remain, may it become doubtful whether enough details exist to consider the
combination unique.
Teeth, jaws, and dental restoration, are all highly resistant to destruction by fire, chemicals, post mortem
destruction and decomposition. Thus they may well be recoverable in an unidentified body after the
fingerprints, for example, have been destroyed. Teeth can also be of assistance in estimating the age, sex,
ethnicity and so on of a dead body. While these factors individually may not carry much significance, in
combination they may narrow the field of suspects considerably and lead to identification.
Teeth can also be a useful source of DNA analysis.
Forensic odontology, a branch of Forensic Medicine, is the science concerned with the proper handling,
examination and evaluation of dental evidence in the interests of justice. Forensic odontology may be divided
into two main sections:
• The identification of persons from their teeth
• The identification of persons from their teeth marks

6.26. The Identification of Persons from their Teeth
This, the largest branch of the science, includes the identification of persons living and dead and is based on a
comparison made between the person's dentition and the positively established previous dental record of a
known person. This comparison method can establish or assist in establishing identity in cases of
unconsciousness, loss of memory, missing persons, and fatalities arising from air, car, or train crash, fire,
drowning, or murder.
The method is of great importance when
bodies have been accidentally charred
beyond recognition, immersed in water for
long periods, or deliberately damaged,
making fingerprinting and other means of
identification impossible.
While dental records are frequently
incomplete or non existent, present
developments in dental science and
procedures are leading to a greater
availability of satisfactory dental records.
Consequently, this development is resulting
in greater relevance and use of dental
identification.
Particular difficulties are encountered in cases where unidentified persons have lost many or all of their
teeth. In such cases the fact that dentures are fitted can be of great assistance as some now bear
identification marks.

No unidentified dead body should be buried until full mouth x rays and models are available, as the dental
record of that person may subsequently become available.

6.27. The Identification of Persons from their Teeth Marks
Teeth marks occur in the form of bite marks on:
• Foodstuffs and other substances
• The victim of crime
• Assailants
Generally these teeth marks come from the front teeth in the upper and lower jaws. Bite marks can at times
be so characteristic that they make possible the definite identification of a suspect. The unique dental
differences among individuals manifest themselves in the bite pattern. The basis of bite pattern identification
depends upon the observation of the relative position of the teeth, their size, normal wear, irregularities,
injuries and loss, as well as bite pressure.
Teeth marks on foodstuffs and other substances
On basically non changeable foodstuff and other substances, the teeth marks will be permanent. If they are on
fruit or similar substances, however, the marks will become somewhat altered during the drying out process. In
such cases where drying out or spoilage will change the shape of the substance bearing the teeth marks, the
marks should be photographed and a cast made without delay.
Teeth marks on the victim of crime
These marks may be found in cases of assault and murder and are usually caused during or after a sexual act.
They are most commonly caused by the assailant and are nearly always due to the lower front teeth. These
marks are usually well defined, being the marks of a sadist who bites their victim slowly and intentionally and
are sometimes referred to as love bites. They will be found most frequently on the breast, neck, cheek, and
so on.

Teeth marks on assailants
These marks will be found in cases where the victim bites the assailant in self defence. They are not always as
clear as the marks left on the victim by the sadist, as they are caused by the victim biting quickly and which
often causes tearing of the skin. These marks may be found in cases of murder, sexual and other assaults.

As in the case of foodstuffs and other substances affected by drying out or spoilage, teeth marks on skin
should be promptly photographed and casts taken before the marks are affected by healing processes or,
where the marks are on a dead body, decomposition.
The work of casting marks and dental comparisons should be carried out by a dental surgeon, preferably one

6.28. Voice Identification
There are very few judicial pronouncements in this area, even though convictions have been procured as the
result of the Courts’ acceptance of the identification of the human voice as evidence. However, in each of the
available recorded cases, corroboration of the relevant voice identification was present.
In the English case, R. v. Maqsud Ali [1965] 3 W.L.R. 229, which dealt with tape recordings as evidence, it was
held that there is no difference between a tape recording and a photograph. A tape recording is admissible in
evidence provided the accuracy of the recording can be proved, the voices properly identified and the evidence
is relevant and otherwise admissible. Such evidence should always be regarded with caution and assessed in
the light of all the circumstances. The accuracy and authenticity of the equipment must be proven to the
satisfaction of the Court.

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

7.1. Introduction
The forensic investigative process consists of all steps from crime scene to Court. The crime scene occupies a
vital position in this process as errors and omissions at this stage cannot be repaired.
Scientific examination of scenes is becoming increasingly complex as is the sophistication of criminal activities
and knowledge. It is therefore crucial to maximise the potential of forensic evidence retrieval for investigators
so as to minimise errors in information at an early stage.
This can only be achieved through the most careful preservation of the scene. Professional forensic
investigation at scenes has positive impacts on crime detection and reduction, both for volume and
serious crime.
Correct preservation of the scene assists crime scene investigators in retrieving high quality crime samples that
lead to best forensic outcomes at the Technical Bureau and the Forensic Science Laboratory and will have the
effect of producing robust provable evidence which in turn will assist in the early removal of criminals from
their cycles of crime.

7.2. Trace Evidence
Culprits may leave evidence of having been at the
scene of the crime. In many cases this is visible and
takes the form of finger-marks, shoe-marks, toolmarks and so on. In other instances the indication of
their presence is not so obvious and may easily be
overlooked.
Less obvious traces such as minute particles of paint,
glass, dust, hair, fibres and similar substances may be
valuable in establishing a link with the culprit. In
addition to small particles of trace evidence, marks,
scratches and abrasions made by the criminal may
provide clues which could result in a successful
prosecution.

Locard’s Exchange Principle
The work of Professor Edmund Locard is widely regarded
as the cornerstone of modern forensic science. He
developed the exchange principle of forensic evidence in
the early part of the twentieth century. This principle holds
that when there is contact between two items there will be
an exchange of material between them, however small. It
follows that when a person comes into contact with an
object or another person, a cross-transfer of physical
evidence occurs. This applies to crime scenes, items of
evidence, perpetrators and victims.
The diagram on the left illustrates the importance of trace
evidence in the investigation of crimes.

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

The principle was enunciated by Professor Locard as follows:
“Wherever he steps, whatever he touches, whatever he leaves, even unconsciously, will serve as a
silent witness against him. Not only his fingerprints or his footprints, but his hair, the fibres from
his clothes, the glass he breaks, the tool mark he leaves, the paint he scratches, the blood or semen
he deposits or collects: all of these and more bear mute witness against him. This is evidence that
does not forget. It is not confused by the excitement of the moment. It is not absent because
human witnesses are. It is factual evidence. Physical evidence cannot be wrong, it cannot perjure
itself, it cannot be wholly absent. Only human failure to find it, study it and understand it can
diminish its value.”
Locard went on to become the director of the world’s first crime laboratory at Lyons, France.

DNA Evidence
The sensitivity of modern DNA detection methods means that there is potential to retrieve this highly
discriminating evidential material from almost anything. The challenge to the skill of the crime scene examiner
is to sample areas likely to be used by criminals without excessive interference from previous contacts. An
object with routine public access such as a phone box or a public seat will not be a suitable source while a
discarded cigarette or drinking can are more likely to yield single DNA profiles.
The criminal could also have carried away on their body or clothing small particles of material from the scene
of the crime and these could assist in placing them at the crime scene and establishing their guilt. In this
regard, biological traces may be available that will facilitate DNA profiling.

Assessing the value of Trace Evidence
In assessing the potential value of trace evidence, there are four general principles:
• The less common the material the more valuable is the potential of its occurrence as a contact trace
• The evidential value of a contact trace depends not only on the rarity of the material composing it but
also on the circumstances in which it is found
• The occurrence together of different types of trace evidence materials is very much more significant for
potential evidence purposes than the occurrence of one type alone
• Often the most cogent evidence is produced when there is a two-way (or reciprocal) transfer of traces
and the probability, or lack of probability, of such occurrences taking place can be of evidential value

7.3. Preservation of scene
Evidence is always present at a crime scene. The forensic examination and interpretation of crime scenes is an
integral part of the investigative process. Every scene has the potential to:
• Assist in reconstructing the sequence of events
• Identify the culprits
• Identify items such as shoes, tools or vehicles used at the scene
• Provide evidence which supports or negates a witness’s version of events.

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

The full forensic potential of any crime scene can only be realised if:
• The relative positions of items and evidence at the scene are maintained (also known as
‘freezing the scene’)
• Trace evidence, marks and prints - both visible and microscopic - at the scene remain intact
• No extraneous items or trace materials are brought into the scene (contamination)
• No trace materials are inadvertently transferred to other scenes or persons
The objective is to ensure that the scene is maintained precisely as the suspect left it and to minimise the
potential for loss of valuable evidence from the outset. To this end it is important that the scene is recorded at
an early stage by photographing all items in-situ. If necessary maps or sketches to support the evidential
impact of various items should be produced.

Responsibility
It is the duty and responsibility of the first responders to preserve scenes until the scene is forensically
examined or they are relieved. All scenes involving death, serious assault or serious crime must be preserved in
such a way as to ensure there is no loss of potential evidence or risk of cross contamination. A scene log must
be maintained for all major incidents.

Action of first responder at scene
• Stop and think forensically
• Do not permit unauthorised access to the scene
• Remember the ground in and around the crime scene can harbour vital evidence such as tyre marks
and shoe marks
• Avoid parking in the vicinity of the scene and take due care when approaching the scene, approach
by alternative pathways if possible
• Control the movements and presence of all persons and vehicles at the scene
• A record should be taken of all persons present at the scene to include names, what is said, condition
of clothes etc
• Extend the area of preservation to ensure that it is sufficiently wide and cordoned off to maximise
the retrieval of all potential trace evidence
• Prevent interference with the scene or items within it
• Protect areas which are dry but under threat by rain. Evidence such as blood, prints and points of
access may otherwise be destroyed. Use should be made of plastic sheeting and crime scene tents
for this purpose

Members who carried out arrests or searches of suspects should be aware of the potential risk of cross
contamination from their person and the subsequent affect on a case. The member should ask themselves
the following questions:
• Was I at the scene?
• Can I cross contaminate the suspect?
• Is there a firearm involved in this case?
• Am I carrying a firearm?
• Have I recently been to a range?
• Can the chance of cross contamination be reduced by some one else arresting/handling this suspect?

Packaging and handling of exhibits
A guide to packaging and labelling can be found in Chapter 16, Forensic Science Laboratory.
(See also Chapter 8.11.)

7.4. Forensic Photography
Crime scene investigation photography
Photography serves as a tool to investigators to make a record of the scene of a crime or other incident, to
show particular items of evidence and their relationship to the scene, and to make close up records of
significant portions of the scene.
Some items of evidence are transient or perishable and must be recorded at the scene. Other items of
evidence can be removed to be examined and photographed with the use of specialist techniques.
Photographs have proven to be one of the most accurate and reliable sources of evidence to the Courts for
over a century. The photographic process can record and store information more accurately and much longer
than the human mind.
The role of the photographer in a criminal investigation
is to provide a complete and permanent pictorial record
of the crime scene. Members who have completed the
Standard Crime Scene Examiners Photographic Course
should carry out crime scene photography.
Only in extreme circumstances should untrained
members be tasked with photographing a crime scene.
In all cases the veracity and integrity of evidence
handling procedures must be observed. In the cases of
conventional film photography the chain of custody

When can a Request be made for a Composite?
A request for a Composite can be made for all crimes but preference will be given to those of a serious nature.
If there is any doubt, advice may be sought from one of the listed CD-Fit Operators.
Criteria to be considered before requesting a CD-Fit Composite
It is essential that the witness has had good sight of the suspect’s full face and is able to describe their facial
features. The witness should have some definitive reason for remembering the suspect. The operator may
deem the witness unsuitable following discussions with them and not complete the CD Fit.
Consideration will be given to the potential for accuracy of witnesses in recognition and recall. The
following factors will be taken into consideration:
• Age of witnesses
• Reason for remembering suspect
• Observational opportunity and any impediment to it
• Mental status
• Alcohol or drug consumption
• Visual impairment
• Length of time suspect observed
• Distance from suspect
• Visibility at time
• Time lapse between the time of observation and proposed compilation of CD-Fit
Note: Requesting members should be aware that the CD-Fit composite may be introduced as evidence
by the prosecution or defence regardless of its value to the investigation.

Requirements of Operators
The Operators will need time with the witness (between 2 - 4 hours) in order to complete a composite.
Witnesses should therefore, be asked if they are willing to devote the time necessary.
Whenever possible a witness should be interviewed by a CD-Fit Operator within 24 - 48 hours of the incident
to allow for maximum level of recall. This may not be possible in all cases, particularly where stress or trauma
is involved.
A CD-Fit Composite will not be prepared if the suspect is known to the witness or can be identified by other
means of identification.

Circulation of Composite Images
Circulation of composite images including in Fógra Tóra is the responsibility of the investigator.

7.5. Forensic archaeology in An Garda Siochana
Forensic archaeology is the application of scientific and methodical archaeological techniques to the locating,
recording and recovery for forensic purposes of buried human remains.
Buried human remains come to the attention of An Garda Siochana in two main ways:
• Information received from a suspect or informant
• Accidental discovery through land drainage or construction projects
Information received from a suspect may be unreliable in identifying the exact location of the buried remains.
This is due to the fact that there may be a long time span since the deposition of the body. As a result there
may be significant landscape changes in the locality or a loss of memory on the part of the suspect or
informant. This may lead to doubts as to where to start digging.
There are a number of methods utilised worldwide
to locate these ‘clandestine’ graves without the
intrusive use of large plant equipment. These
methods are more scientific, efficient, and costeffective than large plant equipment and more
importantly maintain the forensic integrity of the
grave and the evidence.
Geophysics is a type of non-intrusive survey which, when used by an experienced operator and data
interpreter, can yield very successful results. It works on the principle that electric currents passed into the
ground will either encounter more or less resistance in disturbed soil than in the surrounding undisturbed soil.
However, this is dependent on soil type and the contents of the grave.
It is recognised both within archaeological circles and policing circles worldwide that a geophysics survey can
make a valuable contribution to the selection of target areas for further investigation.
Consultation with a geophysicist prior to excavation will result in information becoming available on the most
suitable method to be used. Resistivity testing, followed up by Ground Penetrating Radar, is recommended for
use in forensic cases. The survey is quick with computerised data being available for expert interpretation
within hours.
The geophysics survey should be followed up by use of a cadaver dog. There is a cadaver dog currently
attached to the Garda Dog Unit.
Observations should also be made for surface disturbances, such as changes in growth rates of plant coverage,
differences in the plant coverage itself and differences in surface levels.
When target areas have been selected, they should either be eliminated from the investigation as soon as
possible or marked for further investigation. This is achieved by a small investigative trench being opened
along one end of the target area.
Once investigative excavation has taken place and human bone has been identified, the investigative trench
should be extended to define the parameters of the grave. The grave should then be excavated in a scientific
and methodical manner bearing in mind Locard’s principle that every contact leaves a trace. There will always
be trace evidence at a scene.
The grave should be excavated by someone who knows the total skeletal assemblage. This is important for a
number of reasons.
