Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

• Observation of the pelvic bone and the skull will immediately identify the sex of the skeletal remains.
Determinations on age, ethnicity, height and size can also be made
• It is vital that all the bones are identified and recovered. Some bones are easily missed and can either be
left behind or trampled underfoot. These bones may identify the cause of death.
• Some bones may yield evidence that will assist in the eventual identification of the skeletal remains. Bones
will show evidence of healed trauma that occurred during life such as a broken leg or collarbone. Bones
also show evidence of disease such as osteoarthritis or diabetes.
• Bones will show evidence of fractures and analysis of these bones will confirm whether these fractures
were caused before, during or after death.
An Garda Siochana and the Office of the State Pathologist currently have access to and utilise the expertise
provided by an Osteoarchaeologist, a Forensic Dentist and an Entomologist.

7.6. Fingerprints
The fingerprint system of identification is universally
accepted as a positive means of identification and a
very useful tool in the investigation of crime.
The nature of the skin on the inner surfaces of the
hands and the soles of the feet is ridged and
furrowed. These ridges and furrows form patterns at
the fingertips and on the palms. Across the patterns
there is a constant variation in the continuity of the
ridges, in that they abruptly end (ridge ending) or
diverge into two ridges (bifurcation).
These ridges are randomly dispersed creating a unique pattern and can be used to individualise a fingerprint.
The ridges are made up of ridge units each represented at the skin surface by a sweat pore opening through
which sweat reaches the surface and spreads along the ridge.
When a surface is touched by a finger or palm the sweat transfers to that surface leaving a mirror image of the
ridge details on the surface. This image is usually invisible (latent), until it is developed by the application of a
suitable chemical or powder.
Sometimes a finger-mark may be visible, for example when it is made in dust, blood, putty, grease, paint or
other substance. Developed or visible finger-marks at crime scenes can be recorded by photography or lifted.
The developed or lifted finger-marks can then be searched against the National Fingerprint Database or
compared against the fingerprints of nominated suspects.
Fingerprints are recorded by the application of ink to the ridge surfaces and pressing onto a fingerprint form or
by the use of electronic Livescan units where available.
The National Fingerprint Database is held at Garda Headquarters, Phoenix Park. The database consists of two
separate records of all fingerprints received for recording, a hard copy record and a digital computer record.
The hard copy record is the actual fingerprint form received and these are stored in bundles in numerical
order. The computer record consists of the nominal index, the digital images and the encoded matrix of the
ridge patterns and characteristics. The computer system is referred to as (AFIS) Automatic Fingerprint
Identification System.

When a new set of fingerprints is received at the fingerprint section it is scanned onto the AFIS system. The
database is checked to see if the individual has been on the system previously. When a set is received for the
first time a new number is allocated and a reverse check is made of the fingerprints with unidentified crime
scene marks on the system.
The AFIS system not only records the fingerprints of individuals, it also enables the operator to search the
fingerprint database by using finger-marks found at crime scenes. If an identification is established it is
confirmed by the operator on the hard copy and verified before forwarding a report via the relevant Divisional
Officer to the Investigating Officer.
In order to maximise the effectiveness of the system it is essential that each Divisional Officer ensures that the
identification is followed up in a timely fashion. The suspect identified from the finger or palm print should
either be eliminated or charged.

Recording fingerprints
It is absolutely critical that members take every legal opportunity to fingerprint suspected persons. It should
never be assumed that a set of fingerprints already exists in respect of any suspect. In fact a suspect should be
fingerprinted on every occasion permissible by legislation. This will ensure the optimum availability of
identifiable marks on the system.
There has been a very significant investment on
sophisticated technology to support fingerprint
identification. This technology is rendered useless if
there are no fingerprints available from criminals to
match with fingerprints lifted from crime scenes.
There are a large number of active criminals who
have never been fingerprinted even though several
legal opportunities have arisen to do so. It should
be borne in mind that a set of fingerprints taken
for a minor offence could lead to the identification
of a suspect for a serious crime.
Therefore suspects should be fingerprinted and the fingerprints submitted to the Fingerprint Section, Garda
Technical Bureau immediately for inclusion on the AFIS system. Under no circumstance should fingerprints be
retained locally.
Members should be familiar with the legal authority and circumstances under which fingerprints and palmprints can be taken, which include the following:
• Section 6 Criminal Justice Act, 1984 and Section 6(A) of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended by
Section 48 of the Criminal Justice Act, 2007.
• Section 28 Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended by Section 12 of the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous
Provisions) Act, 1997
• Section 5 Criminal Justice (Drug Trafficking) Act, 1996
• Section 30 Offences against the State Act, 1939
• Section 7 of the Criminal Law Act, 1976
• Section 52 Criminal Justice Act, 2007
• Section 11 of the Criminal Justice (Miscellaneous Provisions) Act, 1997 legislates for the electronic
recording of fingerprints under any of the above powers

Fingerprints can, of course, be taken with consent of the donor.
Section 8 of the Criminal Justice Act, 1984 as amended by Section 49 of the Criminal Justice Act 2007
provides the circumstances and the procedures to be taken by a person (referred to as a requester) to have

A further appeal can be made to the Circuit Court. When a destruction order is made the Commissioner

Fingerprints are recorded on a Fingerprint form, PC65. Some Garda Stations have special desks for taking

There are also stations equipped with Livescan work stations for the electronic recording of fingerprints. Only
trained persons should use the Livescan equipment.
Whichever method is employed the following procedures should be followed:
• Enter the personal details of donor and other required information on the fingerprint form
• Take a rolled impression of each finger and thumb in the allotted boxes
• Take plain impressions of the fingers and thumbs
• Take palm impressions on the rear of the fingerprint form
• If fingerprints are being taken following a conviction, record the conviction details in the space provided
• Request the donor to sign the form
• Forward the fingerprint form to the Fingerprint Section without delay

Taking Fingerprints
The taking of good quality
fingerprints on a fingerprint form
PC65 is usually achieved by applying
a thin film of ink to the ridged skin
areas of the fingers and then
pressing the inked skin against the
fingerprint form. This should result
in clear impressions of the ridge
detail on the form. Fingerprints and
palmprints can also be recorded by
the use of Livescan units that are
linked to the AFIS computer system.

Taking palm-prints
Since the introduction of the palm-print searching facility on AFIS, the method of taking palm-prints has had to
be modified to facilitate the rapid scanning of the pattern area. The main palm area to be recorded is from
the base of the fingers to the wrist joint and the full width of the ridged area.

Elimination of fingerprints
Examinations of crime scenes not only yield finger-marks of culprits, but can also yield the finger-marks of the
injured party and other persons with legitimate access to the scene prior to the crime. It is therefore essential
to obtain fingerprints of these persons for the purpose of eliminating their marks from the investigation.

Providing suspects
Where a suspect’s name is available it should be entered on form C56 for the information of the fingerprint
expert. If suspects become available after the file has been delivered their details can be forwarded by fax or
report to the Fingerprint Section. It is necessary to quote the Technical Bureau reference number on all
correspondence. Details should include the name, address, date of birth, the PULSE number and any other
details which may be available.

Recording Injured Fingers
Under no circumstances should infection be risked by applying ink to injured areas of a person’s hands where
they have open cuts or sores on any part of their fingers or palms, or where their fingers or palms are
bandaged. A note of the condition should be made on the fingerprint form and the fingers(s) or palm(s)
passed over. If possible, the pattern type of the injured fingers(s) should be recorded on the fingerprint form.

Fingerprinting Persons with a Suspected Contagious Disease
Where the person being fingerprinted is suspected of being HIV positive, infected with Hepatitis B or suffering
from any other contagious disease, it is imperative that the member recording the fingerprints should take
precautions to avoid any possibility of contacting the disease.
It should be recognised that disease can be passed on if either the carrier, or any person coming into contact
with that person, has open cuts or sores. Adequate protection can be provided against transmission of the
disease through cuts or sores by the simple act of wearing rubber gloves.
The set of fingerprints taken in these circumstances should always be placed in a plastic bag with an attached
note giving particulars of the donor’s suspected ailment and with a biological hazard label attached.

Location and Preservation of Finger-marks
The first member to arrive at the scene of a crime should be aware of the possibility of fingerprint evidence
being present and should protect the scene adequately so as to prevent its destruction. Areas likely to yield
finger-marks include points of forced entry and items moved or opened.
Articles found outdoors and liable to be damaged should be protected where found by covering with
polythene, cardboard, or other such material. In serious cases it is desirable that the crime scene should be
photographed. It should be noted that handling an item even with a gloved hand may destroy finger-marks.

with current law and regulations

and regulations

Fingerprint technology and Biometrics
Fingerprint identification and recognition has extended beyond the scope of criminal investigation and is now
being used widely as part of the biometric data inserted in passports and personal documents. There are three
main areas of biometric data used for this purpose, fingerprints, facial recognition and iris recognition.
The Omnitrak system now installed at the Fingerprint Bureau has been designed to facilitate this form of

7.7. Earprints
Research has indicated that next to fingerprints and footprints, the external ear constitutes the most unique
characteristic features and peculiarities and has potential for identification evidence.
Ear identification has been a source of interest to medical doctors and scientists for well over a century
but only in recent times has earprint identification been considered as a means of identifying culprits at
crime scenes.

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

The physical characteristics of the ear are well suited for use in criminal investigations for the following
reasons:
• They are individual to the person
• Like fingerprints, the shape and form of earprints remain consistent throughout life except where
affect by injury or disease
• Once detected, earprints can easily be stored and compared against controlled samples taken
from culprits
In the course of searches for fingerprints at crime scenes earprints may be found on surfaces such as doors and
windows, caused by culprits listening before entry. Surfaces likely to provide the best forensic opportunities
are usually glass, wood and painted surfaces. Fingerprint powder has been shown to be the best development
medium and once developed, earprints are lifted and recorded in the same way as fingerprints.
A calculation to establish the height of a culprit can be made by measuring the distance from the centre of the
earprint to the ground.

7.8. Forensic Surveying
The primary function of the Garda Mapping Section, which is based at Harcourt Square, is to provide a
mapping service for An Garda Siochana and the courts. In its role as part of the Technical Bureau this section
deals mainly with the mapping needs of major criminal investigations and subsequent court proceedings.
The equipment available to the section includes the latest Electronic Distance Measuring (EDM) device used
mostly for outdoor scenes (e.g. traffic accidents) and the handheld Disto laser measuring device which is used
for indoor scenes.
Regardless of how a scene is surveyed it is vital that
traditional surveying methodologies are complied with
and in that regard measurements of any movable object
should be made to three fixed points. Its location is then
regarded as being fixed and can then be used to locate
further unknown points. The Section also has the most
up to date Computer Aided Drawing (CAD) and Survey
Control Centre (SCC) software which is used to edit and
prepare the finished exhibits for court.
The function of a forensic surveyor is to measure
accurately and record the location of items of forensic
significance at a crime scene in addition to the general
layout and structure of the scene.
A plan visually and accurately represents the scene of a crime and items within it as recorded on a given date
and time. The plan is drawn to scale and is the only means of exactly measuring distances between items
within the scene and its wider environs.
Plans are an important tool for the modern day investigator and eventually to the prosecution in court as they
give a true sense of the location, proportionality and extent of the scene. All plans are constructed solely from
measurements taken at the scene while all maps are used under licence from Ordinance Survey Ireland.

Plans generally show a relatively small area in great detail while maps are used to show locations and items
large distances apart.
There are two kinds of plans:
• A rough sketch: this is a graphic representation of the scene made at the time of scene examination.
It is not to scale but should resemble the scene as accurately as possible and should show detailed
measurements. All sketches, notes and measurements taken at the scene are retained as they can be
made available to the defence under a disclosure order.
• A scaled drawing: this is also an accurate portrayal of the scene but is drawn back in the office from
measurements which were taken at the scene. It is to scale and should be precise and understandable.

7.9. Shoeprints
The discovery of shoe-marks or shoe impressions at a crime scene is valuable evidence of a person’s presence.
This is frequently overlooked by the perpetrators of crime. Shoe-marks therefore must not be overlooked by
the investigator.

Value of shoe-marks
Shoe-marks at crime scenes have the potential to:
• Assist in the interpretation of the scene
• Differentiate between various participants
• Eliminate suspects
• Identify the culprit(s)
• Link crimes
A shoe-mark is the transferred image of a shoe outsole onto a hard surface. ‘Positive image’ marks involve the
transfer of either the sole material (e.g. rubber) or material picked up by the sole such as dust, soil, oil or
blood. ‘Negative image’ shoe-marks occur when the shoe outsole removes surface residue such as dust, wax
and so on.

16

Page 17

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

Shoe Impressions
A shoe impression is the three dimensional deformation of a material (such as soil, snow or sand) made by the
shoe. If the receiving surface is resilient the impression may only be temporary, this applies to carpet, grass,
skin and so on. Shoe impressions can also be located under water.

Location of shoe-marks and shoe impressions
All surfaces have the potential to hold a shoe-mark or shoe impression. A proactive approach to searching
for shoeprint evidence at the crime scene using common sense and experience will, in most cases,
prove successful.
The importance to the outcome of a case of a shoe-mark/shoe impression at a crime scene, unless obvious,
will be determined by the investigation itself. Even a partial shoe-mark of poor quality may prove useful in the
context of the investigation or in court proceedings.

Examination of scene
Crime scene investigators are trained and equipped to examine scenes for shoeprint evidence. Shoe-marks on
hard surfaces may be visualised using oblique lighting or enhancement techniques such as fingerprint
powders. Following note taking and photography, located shoe-marks are lifted by using either gelatine lifters
or electrostatic shoeprint lifting apparatus. All lifted marks must be properly packaged and labelled.
Following recording shoe impressions may be cast using dental
stone or lifted by electrostatic apparatus as required. Movable
items on which the culprit(s) are suspected of standing must be
properly packaged and submitted. (Examples are broken glass,
paper and seats of chairs).
Submitted shoe-mark/shoe impressions (without shoes for
comparison)
The Ballistics Section and the Forensic Science Laboratory maintain databases of lifted shoe-marks/shoe
impressions from crime scenes that are submitted to them. A second database of identified shoe sole patterns
is maintained which may identify the make of shoe which created the submitted crime scene shoemark/impression.

Submission of comparison case
A comparison between a suspect’s shoes and recovered crime scene shoe-marks/impressions has the
potential to:
• Identify the shoe with the mark
• Eliminate the shoe
The Forensic Science Laboratory and the Ballistics Section carry out shoeprint comparisons. Comparisons
involving other forms of trace evidence are submitted to the Forensic Science Laboratory. Examples could
include cases where it is suspected that the culprit may have stood on broken glass or in a substance such as
blood or oil.
Every effort should be made as soon as possible after the crime to seize shoes from the suspect. Shoe soles
will continue to wear and this could obscure a valuable identifying characteristic.
The investigation should attempt to establish ownership of the submitted shoes to prevent unnecessary waste
of resources at the Forensic Science Laboratory/Technical Bureau.

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

Investigators should endeavour to eliminate any shoes which clearly do not have the same shoe sole pattern as
that on the crime scene shoe-mark/impression.

Science of Comparisons
Shoeprint examiners seek to make positive identifications through the science of comparison. Shoe soles, in
addition to wearing, also pick up cuts or other damage which, by their random positioning and shape, can be
unique. Items which adhere to shoe soles such as thumb tacks, nails and stones can also produce identifying
unique irregularities in shoe marks and shoe impressions. If such unique marks or damage are visible in the
crime scene mark or impression as well as on the submitted shoe and are of sufficient quality and quantity
then a shoeprint examiner may be in a position to make a positive identification.

Barefoot Morphology
It may be possible to link an individual’s barefoot impression either to a crime scene or to a pair of shoes. This
service can be provided by the Forensic Science Laboratory. Crimes committed in bare or stocking feet are rare
in this country though they have been encountered in other countries. A more likely situation in this country is
where shoes are abandoned at or near a crime scene and the investigator wishes to check whether or not
there is an association between a suspect and the shoes.
Barefoot or stocking impressions left at a scene may
be compared with barefoot impressions made by the
suspect to determine whether or not they have the
same morphology (size, shape, deformities and so
on). As a fingerprint expert may be able to identify a
barefoot impression by its ridge detail, it is likely that
the morphology will be considered only if there is no
ridge detail present or is insufficient for
identification.
Impressions unique to the wearer may be found on
the insoles of shoes. These are caused by the weight
of the body, sweat, grit and so on. Such impressions
may be compared with impressions from shoes
known to have been worn by the suspect and with
barefoot impressions taken from the suspect.
In handling shoes appropriate anti-contamination precautions should be applied as trace evidence examination
and DNA profiling may also be available. Investigators are advised to take as many pairs of shoes known to
have been worn by the suspect as possible, as not all shoes will have impressions on the insoles.
It is unlikely that a barefoot impression will be identified conclusively as having been made by a particular foot,
unless the foot has some unusual deformity. However such evidence may be valuable to corroborate other
evidence in the case. This examination may assist in identifying the habitual wearer of the shoe. DNA should
be considered if the main purpose is to identify a link between a person and a shoe.
Further information and assistance
For further information and assistance contact either the Ballistics Section of the Garda Technical Bureau or the
Chemistry Section of the Forensic Science Laboratory.

7.10. Tyre print identification
The use of vehicles in the commission of crime is commonplace. Although our roads and driveways are paved,
tyre prints are frequently found on soft margins, on flowerbeds and at entrances to farm gates.
Tyre prints at crime scenes can prove useful to an investigation and be important evidence in a prosecution.
Tyre prints are also useful in eliminating suspects. In the examination of scenes, tyre print evidence should be
sought by the crime scene investigator and when located should be photographed, mapped and lifted.
Tyre prints at a crime scene have the potential to:
• Identify the type of vehicle which made them
• Identify the make of tyre and their position on the vehicle
• Assist in the reconstruction of the scene including the sequence of events
• Identify a given tyre to the tyre-mark

Tyre marks and tyre impressions
Tyre prints like shoe prints consists of both tyre marks and tyre impressions. The vast majority of tyre prints will
consist of tyre impressions in impressible surfaces such as soil and silt. However, they can also be encountered
in packaging such as polystyrene. There is an additional type of tyre mark to be considered - those occurring
on the clothing and skin of pedestrians involved in traffic accidents, especially hit and runs.
Areas where tyre impressions are visible on soft ground at the crime scene should be cordoned off by the first
responder for later examination. Tyre impressions which could be lost through inclement weather should be
covered.

Examining tyre prints
The Divisional Crime Scene Examiner is trained and equipped to examine, photograph and cast tyre prints.
Casting tyre prints is a highly specialised activity. The pattern of a tyre may vary around the circumference of
the tyre. It is difficult to get sufficiently good comparisons to include or exclude sources of the suspect marks.

Comparison between tyre prints and suspect tyre
The Crime Scene Investigator should print all the tyres on the suspect vehicle and submit them for comparison.
This should be done with the wheels on the vehicle. Comparisons are made at the Ballistics Section of the
Technical Bureau or at the Chemistry Section of the Forensic Science Laboratory. Advice and assistance can be
sought by contacting either of these laboratories.

7.11. Tool-marks
Tool-marks at crime scenes are a valuable source of forensic evidence in criminal investigations. Tool-marks
have the potential to:
• Aid in crime scene interpretation
• Forensically link crimes
• Identify the tool which created the tool-mark

Chapter 7 Forensic Investigation and Identification

A tool-mark is created when an instrument engages with a softer material. These can be compressed marks or
striated marks. Tool-marks in criminal cases come from a variety of sources the most common being:
• Screwdriver or crow bar marks on windows and door locks in burglaries and vehicle break-ins
• Vice-grip marks on door locks
• Bolt-cutter marks on padlocks
In addition tool-marks can be located
• On victims of assault or hit and runs
• Safes and containers forced open
• Altered seals such as cattle tags
• Improvised explosive devices such as pipe bombs
• Sawn objects such as shotgun barrels
• Stamped false number plates.
Scene examinations
Crime scene examiners are trained and equipped to locate and retrieve tool-marks. Following note taking and
photography items bearing tool-marks should be recovered if possible or cast otherwise. Items or casts
packaged and properly labelled should be forwarded to Garda Headquarters.
Tool mark individuality
The edges of most tools have microscopic characteristics of a random nature imparted to them in
manufacturing processes including casting and grinding as well as polishing subsequent to manufacture.
These characteristics can be added to by damage arising from use. If details are present in a tool mark at a
scene and a tool is available for comparison, sufficient detail may be present to allow for identification
between that mark and the tool.
Tool mark examination and comparisons
Both the Ballistics Section of the Garda Technical Bureau and the Chemistry Section of the Forensic
Science Laboratory examine and compare tool marks. Comparisons where trace evidence on the tool is
also being considered should be submitted to the Forensic Science Laboratory along with samples of the
damaged material
Considerable success has been seen in the examination and comparisons of outside door locks broken off
during the course of burglaries. A database of vice-grip marks on these locks is kept in the Ballistics Section
with all seized vice-grips compared against them.

7.12. Glass and Glass Breakage
In addition to its obvious use in windows, both in buildings and vehicles, glass is used to make containers,
tableware and light bulbs. Glass occurs frequently in crimes such as hit and run accidents, burglaries, criminal
damage and assaults (beer glasses/bottles).

When a glass surface is broken a number of fragments are projected backwards in the direction of the blow.
This raises the possibility of glass fragments being transferred to the clothing of the person who delivered the
blow or to the clothing of a person close to the breaking glass. Glass may also be transferred to
implements/items used to break glass.
Broken glass at the crime scene or at the scene of a hit and run traffic accident can assist an investigation by:
• Helping in the interpretation of the scene
• Linking a suspect to the scene
• Identifying a make of vehicle and linking it to an accident scene

Interpretation of Scene
Broken glass at a crime scene may indicate the point of entry or exit. Bullet holes in glass also aid in
determining trajectories of shots. By analysing the radial and concentric fracture lines of bullet holes it may be
possible to determine the sequence of shots.
Determination of Direction of Force
In some investigations it may be necessary to determine whether a window was broken from the outside or
inside. This can be achieved by an examination of the glass. In practical terms, it is difficult to get sufficient
information to make this determination.
Fire Scenes
The examination of broken glass at a fire scene may help determine if the window was broken before or
during the fire.
Linking a suspect to a scene
Broken glass is an excellent medium for shoe marks which can be identified to a suspect shoe. Shoe-marks
may be highlighted when testing broken glass for finger-marks. Fragments of broken glass can also become
imbedded in a suspect’s shoes.
Minute fragments can shower on a culprit during breakage. Fragments of broken glass can be linked
to the original window glass by physical fit or by other methods such as glass refractive index or
chemical composition.
A culprit may receive a cut from broken glass which may provide DNA evidence. Fibres from a garment may
also be left on sharp edges of a broken window.
Glass Examination
Divisional Crime Scene Examiners are trained and equipped to
examine scenes where glass is broken and fragmented. The Ballistics
Section and the Forensic Science Laboratory (Chemistry Section) are
available to provide advice or assistance if required. The Ballistics
Section accepts cases involving physical fits and direction of impact
determination. In addition to these cases, the Forensic Science
Laboratory examines clothing, shoes and tools for the presence of
glass. The transfer is dependent on a number of factors including the
proximity of the breaker to the glass surface. Activity causes these
transferred glass fragments to be lost from the surface of the
clothing over time.
Glass embedded in shoes or tools may last indefinitely, however.

Potential of the evidence
The presence of glass on a person’s clothing may provide valuable evidence of the involvement of that person
in an offence during which glass was broken.
Finding glass fragments on a suspect’s clothing which match the glass sample from the scene may be of
evidential value. The level of significance of the evidence will depend on the quantity of glass recovered, the
time interval between the incident and the clothes being taken, the retentive properties of the clothing and
the relative commonness or rarity of that particular type of glass.
If more than one glass source is broken the significance of a match will be higher if matching glass from all
sources is found.
Glass embedded in cuts in the uppers of shoes may provide good evidence of the shoes being used to kick a
glass pane.
The presence of powdered and/or embedded glass on tools is indicative that the tool was used to break glass.

Limitations
Like other trace evidence, glass fragments will be lost from the surfaces of clothes with time. It is therefore
important that the clothes are taken as soon as possible - 50% of glass transferred is lost within the first
30 minutes.
Glass fragments may lodge in pockets and may remain there for weeks after an incident. Finding glass in
pockets is therefore of poorer evidential value than finding glass on the surfaces of clothes.

When should the evidence be considered?
Cases where the known circumstances and follow-on events indicate a likelihood of fragments being
transferred and where findings can have significance should be accepted (e.g. cases where the breaker is close
to the breaking surface and where the time between the incident and the taking of clothing for examination is
less than four hours).
Footwear is not normally examined unless there is an allegation that glass was broken by kicking.

Samples
Control glass should be taken from the broken window or door rather than from the ground - if a pane has
been completely smashed then remove fragments remaining in the edges of the frame.
Control glass should be packed in such a way as to prevent cuts
and also sealed to prevent any further transfer of particles.
If a number of panes of glass are broken, controls are needed
from each one and each control should be packed separately.
This means that for a double-glazed window samples from both
the inner and outer panes are required while a separate sample
from each square broken from a Georgian window and a
sample from each broken window in a vehicle are also required.
Footwear should always be packed separately from clothing.
If large fragments are noticed on a suspect’s clothing at the time of apprehension they should be removed,
packaged separately and a note taken of the area where they were found. Most glass fragments on clothing
are very small and will not readily be seen by the naked eye.

7.13. Forensic firearms investigation
The forensic examination of firearms and ammunition is a function of the Ballistics Section, Garda Technical
Bureau, Garda Headquarters.
Investigators should note, map and if possible photograph firearms where they have been found. Notes
should include details of firearm type (shotgun, rifle, submachine gun, handgun, etc) and its status
(closed/open breech, magazine in/out, hammer cocked/or not).
All firearms recovered, seized or retrieved by members must always be considered loaded and
capable of killing unless proven unloaded and safe.
The possibility of there being trace evidence on the firearm should always be considered and efforts made to
preserve such evidence. This includes finger-marks, fibres, paint and DNA.
A firearm should only be lifted and proven safe by a member competent, through training or experience, in
handling the type of firearm in question.
Never lift the firearm by inserting a pen or other object in the barrel or trigger guard. Unloaded ammunition
should be packaged separately from the firearm.
Under no circumstances should a loaded firearm (not proved unloaded) be transported to Garda Headquarters.
Members can telephone the Ballistics Section up to 12 midnight each day for advice.

Submission of firearms/ammunition
• Firearms to be submitted must be properly packaged
• The Technical Bureau submission form (C.56) must be fully completed, either typed or in handwritten
format
• Consider the possibility of trace evidence on the firearm which may assist the investigation
• All firearms must be certified as unloaded and safe (on the C.56) by a Ballistics expert before passing
on to any other Section
• Firearms examined for trace evidence at the Fingerprint Section or the Forensic Science Laboratory
will be sent for expert examination at the Ballistics Section of the Technical Bureau
• Following examination at the Technical Bureau each firearm will be returned to the member in charge
of the case via the Forensic Liaison Office

Forensic Firearms Investigations at the Ballistics Section
Each examination will result in a statement/report being issued to the member in charge of the case.
Examinations can vary due to case circumstances however most investigations will cover:
• The full and proper description of the firearm (calibre, type, make, model)
• Serial number - should this be removed attempts will be made to recover the numbers using various
processes
• The origin, age and history of the firearm

• General condition of firearm (new, worn, rusted, sawn off)
• Internal condition of firearm (evidence of residue in barrels, chamber)
• Serviceability (can it function?)
• Particulars of trigger pressure, weight, size, measurements, magazine capacity and defects
• The legal status of the firearm - The Ballistics expert will give an opinion as to what Section of the Firearms
Act or other Acts the firearm in question fits into
• Range tests (reproducing a particular shot pattern, ricochet, damage or determining distances or position
of a shooter when a firearm was discharged)
• Each firearm is test fired using live ammunition as part of its examination and to carry out Ballistic
comparisons

Forensic Examination
Comparisons are made between test fired bullets and cartridge cases from the recovered firearm with
discharged bullets and cartridge cases of that calibre recovered from shooting incidents in the State and also
for particular cases in Northern Ireland and elsewhere.
Examination involves microscopic comparisons of the unique markings impressed on the cartridge cases (firing
pin impressions, breech face markings, chamber markings, extractor, ejector, magazine marks) and bullets
(rifling marks) when a firearm is discharged.
All discharged bullets and cartridge cases recovered at shooting incidents are recorded, and filed at the Data
Reference Centre, Ballistics Section. An automated Ballistics Identification System is utilised to provide
intelligence links between different shooting scenes.

Recovery of discharged shot and wadding from shooting scenes
Discharged shotgun cartridge shot and wadding recovered from shooting scenes are examined to determine
the gauge of shotgun cartridges, the size of shot and brand of ammunition. In exceptional circumstances it
may be possible to identify a discharged plastic wadding to a sawn off shotgun which is suspected of
discharging it.

Reference collections at the Ballistics Section
The Ballistics Section maintains an up to date collection of firearms of historical and contemporary significance
including improvised firearms. These are used for educational, technical and forensic purposes.
Occasionally, bulletins are issued via Fogra Tora when unusual firearms or covert firearms are encountered (pen
guns, key fob guns). A collection is also kept of various ammunition types.

Examination of live ammunition at the Ballistics Section
The examination of live ammunition at the Ballistics Section will cover:
• Calibre
• Manufacture and date
• Condition

• The class of firearm in which it is designed to be discharged
• Legal status under the Firearms and other Acts
• Whether it is capable of being discharged in a firearm or not (attempts will be made to discharge
ammunition in the appropriate firearm)

Firearms Residue
Firearms residue is analysed at the Forensic Science Laboratory. A round of ammunition consists of a cartridge
case, a projectile, a primer composition and a propellant. When the round is placed in the breech of a firearm
and the trigger is pulled the hammer of the firearm delivers a blow to the firing pin. The firing pin goes
through a small hole in the breech face and strikes the primer cap, which contains a mixture of chemicals
including oxidisers, fuels and frictionisers, which sensitise each other to percussion and rapid burning. This
primer burns rapidly, producing a flame and a shower of hot molten particles which penetrate and ignite
the propellant.
The burning of the propellant produces a large volume of
gas in a very confined space which is accompanied by a
large rise in temperature and pressure. The resultant gas
pressure forces the projectile (bullet) from the cartridge case
and down the barrel of the firearm. The large increase in
temperature and pressure causes the cartridge case to
expand in the chamber, thus sealing the chamber and
thereby preventing any backward escape of the gases.
The time span from detonation of the primer cap to the
projectile leaving the muzzle of the gun is typically in the
region of 0.01 to 0.03 seconds. Temperatures and
pressures inside the gun during the discharge have been
estimated to be in the region 3,000ºC and 3.5 tonnes per
square centimetre. The gases, vapours and particulate
materials formed during the discharge of the firearm are
collectively known as firearms residue (FAR). The residue
consists of both inorganic and organic components.
Inorganic components originate from the primer contained within the primer cap, the interior of the primer
cap itself, the cartridge case, the bullet core and jacket, along with debris from the interior of the firearm.
However, the majority of the firearms residue originates from the primer and from the bullet.
Organic components originate from the primer mixture and the propellant.
When the firearm is discharged, in addition to the projectile(s), firearms residue is emitted mainly from the
muzzle but also from the cylinder area (revolver), ejection ports and other vents in the firearm. This firearms
residue is deposited on the hands, face and outer clothing (such as the cuff area) of the firer along with the
surfaces of the firearm itself. Firearms residue persists on the hands of a firer for approximately three to four
hours. Persistence on face and clothing will be for longer periods.
Sampling a suspect for firearms residue
Firearms Residue Kits are designed and distributed by the Forensic Science Laboratory and each Divisional
Crime Scene Investigation (CSI) Unit should maintain a stock. The kits contain instruction sheets on how to
use them.

It is vital that these kits are available around the clock because firearms residue only persists for a short period
and it is imperative that the suspect is sampled for firearms residue as soon as possible after apprehension.
NB: No person who uses firearms should participate in the sampling of a suspect for firearms residue. This is
to avoid the possibility of cross contamination.
The firearms residue kit and/or outer clothing collected from the suspect(s) should be forwarded to the
Forensic Science Laboratory as soon as possible. (The officer in charge of the case should make contact with
the laboratory informing the laboratory of the requirements). Footwear and underclothing should not be
submitted for firearms residue examination.
It may be useful to submit discharged cartridge cases to the laboratory as reference samples in particular cases.
Typically, these will also be required for ballistic examination.
Limitations
Particles generated from the discharge of a firearm
can fall into different categories; some are
characteristic of firearms residue while others are
consistent with firearms residue but could also arise
from other sources.
Even if characteristic particles are found on a
suspect, it is normally not possible to distinguish
between three possible scenarios: that the suspect
fired a gun, was close to someone firing a gun, or
had handled something with firearms residue on it.
A further limitation is the rapid loss of firearms residue from hands with time (the expected persistence on a
living subject is of the order of three to four hours, even without washing or making an effort to clean up).
In contrast to residue on hands, firearms residue on clothing will persist for longer but for this reason it is not
possible to associate it with a particular shooting event.
Residue on other surfaces or objects (the door surrounds or roof lining of a car, for example) can also be
collected using the firearms residue kits provided by the laboratory.
Despite these limitations, the presence of firearms residue on a person is very unusual, unless they have been
involved in a recent shooting event. Its presence is most useful if the suspect denies having had any contact
with firearms.

7.13.1. Anti-Personnel Sprays
Anti-personnel sprays are analysed in the Forensic Science Laboratory. They consist of a canister holding a
liquid with an active ingredient dissolved in the liquid. The canister is usually equipped with a spray
mechanism for discharging the liquid. Such canisters are commercially available in other countries. However,
in this country unauthorised possession of such a spray is an offence due to the harmful effects they can have.
Section 9(4) of the Firearms and Offensive Weapons Act (1990) provides that “where a person, without lawful
authority or reasonable excuse (the onus of proving which shall lie on him) has with him in any public place –
(a) any flick knife, or (b) any other article whatsoever made or adapted for use for causing injury to or
incapacitating a person, he shall be guilty of an offence.”
